title: Overview - Ontology - Palantir
summary: The Palantir Ontology is an operational layer that sits on top of digital assets (datasets, virtual tables, models) and connects them to real-world counterparts. It serves as a digital twin containing semantic elements (objects, properties, links) and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security). The Ontology maps data to meaningful business concepts rather than raw tables and columns.
relevantParts: Defines the Ontology as operational layer connecting data to real-world entities
Introduces semantic vs kinetic distinction in Ontology elements
Core reference for all Ontology primitives and their relationships
How datasources are mapped to Ontology objects, properties, links
Object Data Funnel microservice for data indexing
Ontology as the bridge between raw data and applications

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: An operational layer sitting on top of digital assets (datasets, virtual tables, models) that connects them to real-world counterparts, serving as a digital twin of an organization.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Schema definition of a real-world entity or event within the Ontology, representing things like plants, equipment, products, or customer orders.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A characteristic of an object type that defines attributes of a real-world entity or event.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Schema definition of a relationship between two object types in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: A kinetic element of the Ontology that enables capturing data from operators or orchestrating decision-making processes connected to existing systems.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A kinetic element providing a way to author and evolve business logic with arbitrary complexity within the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology type that describes the shape and capabilities of an object type, enabling object type polymorphism for consistent modeling.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property that can be used on multiple object types for consistent data modeling and centralized metadata management.

extractedEntities.name: Digital Twin
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where the Ontology serves as a digital replica of the organization, containing both semantic elements (objects, properties, links) and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security).

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of mapping raw data (tables, columns) to meaningful business concepts (objects, properties, links) to enable domain-driven interaction.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform where the Ontology is built and managed, integrating datasets, virtual tables, and models.

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: Palantir tool for searching and exploring objects of interest within the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir tool for building high-quality operational applications powered by the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Palantir analytical tool for performing complex analyses against the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object Views
extractedEntities.description: Reusable views for displaying and interacting with Ontology objects across Palantir applications.

keyInsights: The Ontology has two fundamental layers: semantic elements (objects, properties, links) for modeling, and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security) for enabling change.
The Ontology sits on top of digital assets (datasets, virtual tables, models) and maps them to real-world business concepts, serving as a digital twin.
Interfaces provide object type polymorphism, allowing consistent interaction with object types sharing a common shape.
The Ontology is deeply integrated into all Palantir user-facing tools (Object Explorer, Quiver, Workshop, Object Views), making it the central abstraction for all applications.
Actions enable organizational change while complying with governance controls, while functions provide arbitrarily complex business logic.

title: Core concepts - Palantir
summary: Defines the fundamental building blocks of the Ontology: object types (schema definition of real-world entities), properties (characteristics), link types (relationships), and action types (mutations). An object type is analogous to a dataset, an object to a row, and a property to a column. The Ontology creates a complete picture by mapping datasets and models to these primitives.
relevantParts: Formal definitions of all core Ontology primitives
Analogies between Ontology concepts and traditional data structures
Explains how datasets map to Ontology types

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A categorization of the world; in Foundry, it is the digital twin of an organization, a rich semantic layer on top of datasets and models.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Defines an entity or event in an organization. Analogous to a dataset in the traditional data model.

extractedEntities.name: Object
extractedEntities.description: A single instance of an object type, analogous to a row in a dataset, corresponding to one real-world entity or event.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set
extractedEntities.description: A collection of multiple object instances representing a group of real-world entities or events, analogous to a filtered set of rows.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Defines a characteristic of an object type. Analogous to a column in a dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Property Value
extractedEntities.description: The value of a property on a specific object instance, analogous to a field/cell in a dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Defines the relationship between two object types. Analogous to a join between datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Defines how an object type can be modified through edits to objects, property values, and links.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property reusable across multiple object types for consistent modeling and centralized metadata management.

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: The central permissioning model in the Ontology, granting access to ontological resources similar to filesystem roles in Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset-Ontology Mapping
extractedEntities.description: Conceptual mapping where Dataset=Object Type, Row=Object, Column=Property, Field=Property Value, Join=Link Type.

keyInsights: The Ontology maps directly to dataset concepts: Object Type=Dataset, Object=Row, Property=Column, Property Value=Field, Link Type=Join.
An object set is a collection of object instances, functionally equivalent to a filtered view of rows in a dataset.
Roles serve as the central permissioning model for the Ontology, paralleling Foundry filesystem roles but applied to ontological resources.
Shared properties enable consistent data modeling across object types with centralized metadata management.
The Ontology is not just a schema layer but maps each concept to actual organizational data to power real-world applications.

title: Object and link types - Object types - Overview - Palantir
summary: An object type is the schema definition of a real-world entity or event. An object instance refers to a single instance of an object type corresponding to a single real-world entity. To create objects, organizations add backing datasources to the object type and connect their enterprise data into the Ontology. Properties define characteristics, and shared properties allow consistent modeling across types.
relevantParts: Detailed definition of Object Type as schema for real-world entities
Relationship between object types and backing datasources
Object type as schema definition mapping to real data

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The schema definition of a real-world entity or event. Created in Ontology Manager by adding backing datasources to connect enterprise data into the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object
extractedEntities.description: A single instance of an object type corresponding to one real-world entity or event, such as a specific employee or flight.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set
extractedEntities.description: A collection of multiple object instances representing a group of real-world entities or events, like 'All tenured employees'.

extractedEntities.name: Backing Datasource
extractedEntities.description: The data source (dataset) added to an object type in Ontology Manager that provides the actual data for creating and displaying objects.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristics of an object type that map to columns in backing datasources.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property reusable across multiple object types for consistent data modeling.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application for defining and managing object types, properties, link types, and other Ontology elements.

extractedEntities.name: Employee Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Example object type representing all employees in an organization, backed by employee directory data.

extractedEntities.name: Flight Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Example object type representing all flights, with objects like 'JFK to SFO 2021-02-24'.

keyInsights: Object types are not abstract models; they are backed by actual datasources, connecting enterprise data into the Ontology through Ontology Manager.
The three-level hierarchy is: Object Type (schema) -> Object (instance) -> Object Set (collection of instances).
Objects are created by adding backing datasources to an object type, which connects enterprise data (like employee directories) into the Ontology.
An object set is analogous to a filtered set of rows in a dataset, representing a specific subset of objects.
The Ontology maps each concept to actual organizational data, enabling it to power real-world applications rather than being an abstract data model.

title: Object and link types - Properties - Overview - Palantir
summary: A property of an object type is the schema definition of a characteristic of a real-world entity or event. Properties are analogous to columns in a dataset, and property values are analogous to fields. Properties have metadata including base type, render hints, visibility settings, title key (display name), and primary key (unique identifier).
relevantParts: Formal property definition with dataset/column analogy
Property metadata: base type, render hints, visibility, keys
Distinction between title key and primary key
Mandatory control properties for access control

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Schema definition of a characteristic of a real-world entity or event, analogous to a column in a dataset. Includes metadata like base type, render hints, visibility, and key designations.

extractedEntities.name: Property Value
extractedEntities.description: The value of a property on a specific object instance, analogous to a field in a dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Base Type
extractedEntities.description: The primitive data type of a property: String, Integer, Short, Date, Timestamp, Boolean, Byte, Long, Float, Double, Decimal, Vector, Array, Struct, Media Reference, Time Series, or Attachment.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: A property designated as the unique identifier for objects of a given type. Valid types: String, Integer, Short; discouraged for time-based and boolean types.

extractedEntities.name: Title Key
extractedEntities.description: A property designated as the display name for objects. Supports most base types except Vector, Struct, Media Reference, Time Series, and Attachment.

extractedEntities.name: Render Hints
extractedEntities.description: Property metadata controlling how property values are displayed and indexed, including the Searchable hint that enables search indexing.

extractedEntities.name: Struct
extractedEntities.description: A composite property type that does not support nesting and cannot contain array fields. Fields have limited type support.

extractedEntities.name: Array
extractedEntities.description: A property base type that cannot contain null elements. Nested arrays are not supported in Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Property Visibility
extractedEntities.description: Access control setting determining which users can see a property, enabling fine-grained data access within object types.

extractedEntities.name: Long Type Limitation
extractedEntities.description: Long property values greater than 1e15 have representational issues in JavaScript, so String is recommended instead.

keyInsights: Properties support a wide range of base types: primitives (String, Integer, Boolean), temporal (Date, Timestamp), numeric (Float, Double, Decimal), and complex (Array, Struct, Vector, Media Reference, Time Series, Attachment).
Primary keys must be unique per object and are restricted to String, Integer, or Short types; time-based and boolean types are discouraged.
Long values greater than 1e15 have JavaScript representational issues, so String is recommended for large numeric identifiers.
The Searchable render hint must be enabled on properties for them to be filterable, orderable, and aggregatable via search APIs.
Struct properties do not support nesting or array fields, and Array properties cannot contain null elements or be nested in Object Storage V2.
Property visibility controls which users can see a property, enabling column-level access control within object types.

title: Object and link types - Types reference - Palantir
summary: Comprehensive type reference for all Ontology types including object types, link types, properties, interfaces, and their metadata. Serves as the canonical reference for how types are defined, their relationships, and their API representations.
relevantParts: Complete type system reference for all Ontology primitives

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Type
extractedEntities.description: Types used to model a real-world domain into an Ontology, including Object Type, Property, Link Type, Action Type, Interface, and Object Type Group.

extractedEntities.name: Data Type
extractedEntities.description: Types used to represent data values in Foundry, inspired by concepts in RDF, OWL, and XSD.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Group
extractedEntities.description: A classification primitive that helps users better search and explore their ontology by grouping related object types.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Definition
extractedEntities.description: Type-level information about ontology entities including display name, property names, data types, and description. Does not include actual data values.

extractedEntities.name: Object Instance
extractedEntities.description: The actual primary key and property values for specific instances of an ontology entity, as opposed to the type definition.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology type describing the shape and capabilities of an object type, enabling polymorphism for consistent modeling across types sharing a common shape.

extractedEntities.name: RDF/OWL/XSD Inspiration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry data types are inspired by RDF, OWL, and XSD standards, grounding the type system in established semantic web standards.

extractedEntities.name: Type-Level vs Instance-Level
extractedEntities.description: Architectural distinction between object type definitions (metadata/schema) and object instances (actual data values), paralleling class vs instance in OOP.

keyInsights: Foundry distinguishes between Ontology types (for modeling domains) and Data types (for representing values), with Data types inspired by RDF, OWL, and XSD semantic web standards.
Object type definitions (metadata/schema) are distinct from object instances (actual data), paralleling class vs instance in object-oriented programming.
Object Type Groups provide an organizational layer for searching and exploring the ontology, grouping related object types together.
The type system encompasses Object Type, Property, Shared Property, Link Type, Action Type, Interface, and Object Type Group as the core Ontology types.
Ontology metadata includes display name, property names, property data types, and description, while instance data contains actual primary key and property values.

title: Object and link types - Link types - Overview - Palantir
summary: A link type is the schema definition of a relationship between two object types. Supports cardinalities: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many. Foreign keys define one-to-one/many-to-one links, while many-to-many requires a separate datasource containing link combinations. Each side of a link type has its own display name.
relevantParts: Link type cardinality options and their semantics
Foreign key vs datasource-backed link implementation
Link type metadata including bidirectional display names

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Schema definition of a relationship between two object types. Supports cardinalities one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many. Each side has its own display name.

extractedEntities.name: Link
extractedEntities.description: A single instance of a link type representing the actual relationship between two specific objects.

extractedEntities.name: Cardinality
extractedEntities.description: The multiplicity of a link type: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, or many-to-many, determining how objects relate.

extractedEntities.name: Foreign Key
extractedEntities.description: The mechanism defining one-to-one and many-to-one links by matching property values between object types.

extractedEntities.name: Many-to-Many Backing
extractedEntities.description: Many-to-many link types require a separate datasource containing all link combinations, as opposed to foreign key-based links.

extractedEntities.name: Self-Referential Link
extractedEntities.description: Links can exist between two objects of the same type, such as a Direct Report to Manager link on the Employee object type.

extractedEntities.name: Employee-Employer Link
extractedEntities.description: Example link type connecting Employee objects to Company objects via an 'Employee to Employer' relationship.

extractedEntities.name: Flight-Aircraft Link
extractedEntities.description: Example link type connecting Flight objects to Aircraft objects via a 'Scheduled Flight to Assigned Aircraft' relationship.

keyInsights: Link types support four cardinalities: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many, with foreign keys defining the first three and a separate datasource required for many-to-many.
Each side of a link type has its own independent display name, allowing asymmetric naming (e.g., 'Employee' side vs 'Employer' side).
Self-referential links are supported, allowing an object type to link to itself (e.g., Employee Direct Report to Manager).
Cross-ontology links are not supported; link types can only connect object types within the same Ontology.
Many-to-many links require their own backing datasource containing the link combinations, while foreign-key links derive from existing object type properties.

title: Action types - Overview - Palantir
summary: An action type defines a set of changes or edits to objects, property values, and links that a user can take at once. Changes are committed atomically to the Ontology and reflected across all applications. Action types include rules (Ontology edits), parameters (user inputs), submission criteria (validation), and side effects (notifications, webhooks).
relevantParts: Action types as atomic mutation definitions
Composition of actions: rules, parameters, submission criteria, side effects
Actions as the kinetic layer of the Ontology
Actions as the write mechanism for Ontology data
Integration with object databases for data modification
Action type architecture and transaction model
Structured writeback to Ontology with permissions/conditions

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: The definition of a set of changes or edits to objects, property values, and links that a user can take at once, including side effect behaviors. Changes are committed atomically.

extractedEntities.name: Action
extractedEntities.description: A single transaction that changes properties of one or more objects based on user-defined logic, committed atomically to the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Rules
extractedEntities.description: Logic component of an action type that defines the Ontology edits or effects triggered by the action.

extractedEntities.name: Parameters
extractedEntities.description: User input definitions for an action type that capture data needed to execute the action, like selecting a new role value.

extractedEntities.name: Submission Criteria
extractedEntities.description: Validation conditions determining whether an action can be submitted, encoding business logic into data editing permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Side Effects
extractedEntities.description: Behaviors triggered upon action submission such as notifications and webhooks, occurring alongside the primary Ontology edits.

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The dataset associated with an object type that captures the most up-to-date version of object data with user edits incorporated via actions.

extractedEntities.name: Atomic Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where all changes from an action are committed at once to the Ontology and reflected across all applications atomically.

extractedEntities.name: Assign Employee Action
extractedEntities.description: Example action type for changing an employee's role, including parameter for new role, auto-linking to new manager, notification side effects, and HR authorization checks.

keyInsights: Action types have four core components: rules (logic/edits), parameters (user inputs), submission criteria (validation), and side effects (notifications/webhooks).
Actions are committed atomically to the Ontology and reflected consistently across all user-facing applications.
The same action logic and validations are shared across all user-facing applications, ensuring consistent edits.
User edits via actions are captured in an object type's writeback dataset, maintaining the most up-to-date version of object data.
Actions enable users to think about objectives rather than specific property edits, abstracting data manipulation behind business-meaningful operations.

title: Action types - Rules - Palantir
summary: Rules define the logic of an action type, transforming parameters into Ontology edits or effects. Ontology rules include create object, modify object, delete object, add/remove links, and function rules. Function rules reference Ontology edit functions and preclude other rules since function code handles everything. Rules can also operate on interfaces for polymorphic actions.
relevantParts: Detailed rule types: create, modify, delete objects and links
Function rules as code-based alternatives to declarative rules

extractedEntities.name: Rules
extractedEntities.description: The logic component of an action type that transforms parameters into Ontology edits or other effects. Two main types: Ontology rules and effect rules.

extractedEntities.name: Create Object Rule
extractedEntities.description: Ontology rule for creating an object of a predefined type, requiring the primary key property to be filled.

extractedEntities.name: Modify Object Rule
extractedEntities.description: Ontology rule for modifying an existing object whose primary key is derived from object reference parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Delete Object Rule
extractedEntities.description: Ontology rule for deleting an existing object whose primary key is derived from object reference parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Create Link Rule
extractedEntities.description: Ontology rule for creating many-to-many links between objects via object reference parameters. Foreign key links use Modify Object rule instead.

extractedEntities.name: Delete Link Rule
extractedEntities.description: Ontology rule for deleting many-to-many links. Foreign key links use Modify Object rule instead.

extractedEntities.name: Function Rule
extractedEntities.description: A rule referencing an Ontology edit function whose inputs are derived from action parameters. When present, no other rules may be configured since function code handles everything.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Rule
extractedEntities.description: Rules (create, modify, delete) that operate on objects of any type implementing a particular interface, enabling polymorphic actions.

extractedEntities.name: Function-Backed Action
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where an action type uses a single Function Rule instead of declarative rules, delegating all logic to code for maximum flexibility.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Polymorphism
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of defining action rules that operate on interface types rather than concrete object types, enabling actions that work across all implementing types.

keyInsights: Function rules are mutually exclusive with all other rule types: when a function rule is present, no other rules can be configured because function code handles everything.
Foreign key-based links (one-to-one, one-to-many) must be created/deleted via the Modify Object rule by changing the foreign key property, not through Create/Delete Link rules.
Create/Delete Link rules only work for many-to-many relationships; all other link cardinalities are managed through property modifications.
Interface rules enable polymorphic actions that can create, modify, or delete objects of any type implementing a particular interface.
Rule values can be sourced from parameters, object parameter properties, static values, conditionals, auto-generated IDs, or current timestamps.

title: Action types - Submission criteria - Palantir
summary: Documentation on submission criteria (formerly validations) that determine whether an action can be submitted. Criteria combine conditions based on user context, parameters, and static information into logical statements. Supports encoding business logic into data editing permissions for Ontology data quality.
relevantParts: Performance trade-offs in validation (no object existence checks)
Submission criteria encode business logic into action permissions
Conditions combine user context, parameters, and static values
Independent from permissions governing action type editing

extractedEntities.name: Submission Criteria
extractedEntities.description: Conditions determining whether an action can be submitted, encoding business logic into data editing permissions for Ontology data quality and governance.

extractedEntities.name: Condition
extractedEntities.description: A single comparison check between two values within submission criteria, using templates based on current user or parameter.

extractedEntities.name: Operator
extractedEntities.description: Logical combinators (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR) used to combine and nest conditions into sophisticated submission criteria statements.

extractedEntities.name: Current User Condition
extractedEntities.description: Submission criteria template that evaluates user properties like user ID, group memberships, or multipass attributes to determine action authorization.

extractedEntities.name: Parameter Condition
extractedEntities.description: Submission criteria template that evaluates action parameters and object properties to determine whether an action meets business rules.

extractedEntities.name: Group Membership Check
extractedEntities.description: A condition checking whether the submitting user belongs to specific groups (direct or inherited membership) for authorization.

extractedEntities.name: Flight Aircraft Assignment
extractedEntities.description: Example where submission criteria ensure only flight controllers can change aircraft assignments and only operational aircraft can be assigned.

keyInsights: Submission criteria are independent from Ontology editing permissions; they govern data quality and business logic while permissions govern type-level access.
Conditions come in two templates: 'Current User' (evaluating user identity, group membership, multipass attributes) and 'Parameter' (evaluating action input values).
Logical operators (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR) allow nesting conditions into complex business rules for determining action eligibility.
Actions can only be submitted if ALL submission criteria are met, providing a strict validation gate before Ontology changes.
Group membership checks support both direct and inherited membership, enabling hierarchical authorization structures.

title: Interfaces - Overview - Palantir
summary: An interface is an Ontology type describing the shape and capabilities of object types, enabling polymorphism. Interfaces are composed of shared properties, link type constraints, and metadata. Unlike concrete object types, interfaces are abstract (no backing datasets, cannot be instantiated directly). Multiple object types can implement an interface, and interfaces can extend other interfaces.
relevantParts: Interface definition as abstract shape descriptor
Composition: shared properties + link type constraints + metadata
Key distinction: concrete object types vs abstract interfaces

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An abstract Ontology type describing the shape and capabilities of object types. Composed of interface properties, link type constraints, and metadata. Cannot be instantiated directly.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Property
extractedEntities.description: Properties defined on an interface, either locally (recommended) or using shared properties, that implementing object types must map to.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Constraint
extractedEntities.description: A constraint on an interface specifying required relationships that implementing object types must satisfy with their concrete link types.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Polymorphism
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where interfaces enable workflows to interact with any implementing object type without knowing specific details, automatically supporting new types.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Inheritance
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where child interfaces extend parent interfaces, inheriting properties and adding more specific ones, supporting multi-level inheritance chains.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application for defining, editing, and implementing interfaces within the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Marketplace
extractedEntities.description: Palantir platform feature for packaging and installing interfaces across deployments.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set Service
extractedEntities.description: Service that supports searching across interfaces and returning matching objects from all implementing types.

extractedEntities.name: Facility Interface
extractedEntities.description: Example interface with Facility Name and Location properties, implemented by Airport, Manufacturing Plant, and Maintenance Hangar object types.

keyInsights: Interfaces are abstract (no backing datasets, cannot be instantiated), while object types are concrete (backed by datasets, can be instantiated as objects).
Interfaces support multi-level inheritance: interfaces can extend other interfaces, and object types can implement multiple interfaces.
Interface polymorphism allows workflows to automatically support new object types that implement the interface without code changes.
Interfaces are currently fully supported in Ontology Manager, Marketplace, and Functions (TypeScript v2), with partial support in Actions, Object Set Service, and other tools.
Interface properties can be defined locally on the interface (recommended) or via shared properties, with implementing types mapping their properties to satisfy the interface.

title: Interfaces - Implement an interface - Palantir
summary: Describes how object types implement interfaces, analogous to implementing interfaces in programming languages. Implementation requires mapping shared properties to object type properties and satisfying link type constraints. Allows application code to interact with any implementing objects through the interface definition as an API layer.
relevantParts: Implementation mechanics: property mapping, constraint satisfaction
Interface as API layer for applications

extractedEntities.name: Interface Implementation
extractedEntities.description: The process by which an object type declares conformance to an interface by mapping properties and satisfying link type constraints.

extractedEntities.name: Property Mapping
extractedEntities.description: The declaration mapping an object type's existing properties onto the required interface properties during interface implementation.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Constraint Satisfaction
extractedEntities.description: The process of selecting concrete link types on an object type that satisfy the interface's required link type constraints.

extractedEntities.name: Interface as API Layer
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where application code is written against the interface definition rather than individual object types, enabling automatic support for new implementing types.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application where interfaces are implemented on object types via the Interfaces tab.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Palantir tool where object type outputs can implement interfaces as part of data pipeline configuration.

extractedEntities.name: Dual API Name Access
extractedEntities.description: Objects of implementing types can be accessed using both their local API names (as concrete type) and the interface API names (when typed as interface).

keyInsights: Implementing an interface requires mapping existing object type properties onto required interface properties and satisfying all required link type constraints.
Objects of implementing types support dual access: via local API names when typed as the concrete object type, and via interface API names when typed as the interface.
Using interfaces as an API layer allows new object types to be added to applications simply by implementing the interface, without any code changes.
Interface implementation can be performed in both Ontology Manager (via the Interfaces tab) and Pipeline Builder (as part of object type output configuration).
Optional interface properties can be skipped during implementation, providing flexibility for types that do not have all characteristics.

title: Interfaces - Interface link types - Palantir
summary: Link type constraints define parameters for interface link types. Constraints specify: link target type (interface or object type), target entity, cardinality (ONE or MANY), and whether the link is required. All implementing object types must have a link satisfying these constraints if the link is required.
relevantParts: Required vs optional link constraints on implementing types

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Constraint
extractedEntities.description: Defines parameters for an interface link type including link target type, target entity, cardinality (ONE or MANY), and whether the link is required.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Link Type
extractedEntities.description: An object-to-object relationship defined at the interface level, common across all implementing object types, with an API name for code reference.

extractedEntities.name: Link Target Type
extractedEntities.description: Specifies whether an interface link points to another interface (abstract target) or a concrete object type (specific target).

extractedEntities.name: Interface Cardinality
extractedEntities.description: ONE or MANY cardinality for interface link types, analogous to one-to-one and one-to-many modeling respectively.

extractedEntities.name: Interface-to-Interface Link
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where both ends of a link type constraint are interfaces, modeling relationships between abstract types (e.g., Facility to Alert).

extractedEntities.name: Interface-to-Object Link
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where a link type constraint connects an interface to a concrete object type, enforcing a specific target (e.g., Facility to Airlines).

extractedEntities.name: Facility-Alert Interface Link
extractedEntities.description: Example of interface-to-interface linking where Facility interface links to Alert interface, implemented by Airport-to-Flight Alert concrete link.

extractedEntities.name: Facility-Airlines Concrete Link
extractedEntities.description: Example of interface-to-object linking where Facility interface links directly to the Airlines object type.

keyInsights: Link type constraints can target either interfaces (for abstract relationships between two polymorphic types) or concrete object types (for enforced specific targets).
Interface link cardinality supports ONE (one-to-one) or MANY (one-to-many), determining the multiplicity of the relationship.
When an object implements an interface with link type constraints, its concrete link types are used to fulfill the interface's abstract link requirements.
Interface-to-interface links enable modeling relationships between categories of things (e.g., any Facility to any Alert) without knowing specific types.
Required link type constraints must be satisfied by all implementing object types, while optional constraints provide flexibility for types without that relationship.

title: Object and link types - Shared properties - Overview - Palantir
summary: A shared property is a property that can be used on multiple object types, enabling consistent data modeling and centralized metadata management. When associated with a shared property, direct edits to inherited metadata are disabled on the object type. Shared properties are foundational to interfaces.
relevantParts: Centralized metadata management across object types
Relationship between shared properties and interfaces

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property reusable across multiple object types for consistent data modeling and centralized metadata management. Underlying object data is not shared, only the metadata.

extractedEntities.name: Property Metadata
extractedEntities.description: The schema-level information about a property (name, type, description, render hints) that is shared across object types when using shared properties.

extractedEntities.name: Centralized Metadata Management
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where shared properties allow updating property metadata in one place rather than on each object type individually.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property Locking
extractedEntities.description: When an object type property is associated with a shared property, direct edits to inherited metadata are disabled on the object type.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property Conversion
extractedEntities.description: Existing local properties on object types can be converted into shared properties, enabling post-hoc standardization.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Application where shared properties are created, managed, and associated with object types.

keyInsights: Shared properties share metadata (name, type, description) across object types but NOT the underlying data; each object type's data remains independent.
When a property is associated with a shared property, direct edits to inherited metadata are locked on the individual object type, enforcing consistency.
Existing local properties can be converted to shared properties retroactively, enabling incremental standardization of the ontology.
Shared properties are foundational to interfaces, providing the reusable property definitions that interfaces reference.
Shared properties are visually distinguished in the platform with a globe icon next to their name.

title: Object and link types - Value types - Overview - Palantir
summary: Value types are semantic wrappers around field types that include metadata and constraints, enhancing type safety and expressiveness. They capture domain-specific meaning (e.g., 'email' value type with regex constraint). Value types are scoped to a space and can be reused across object types and pipelines within that space.
relevantParts: Value types as semantic wrappers with constraints
Space-scoped reuse across object types and pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Value Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic wrapper around a field type that includes metadata and constraints enhancing type safety, expressiveness, and domain context. Scoped to a space and reusable across object types and pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Constraint
extractedEntities.description: Validation rules on value types (e.g., regex patterns) that enforce data quality and semantic correctness at the property level.

extractedEntities.name: Space
extractedEntities.description: An organizational unit in the platform that can hold a single ontology. Value types are scoped to and can only be used within their defining space.

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Typing
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of wrapping primitive types with domain-specific meaning and validation (e.g., 'email' value type on String with regex constraint).

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Versioning
extractedEntities.description: Value types are versioned to handle both breaking and non-breaking edits, since they are intended for reuse across multiple pipelines and object types.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Permissioning
extractedEntities.description: Value types have their own permission model to ensure users can apply them where needed across the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Email Value Type
extractedEntities.description: Example value type wrapping String with a regex constraint to validate email addresses, reusable across multiple object types and pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Palantir tool where value types enforce validation constraints on data flowing through data integration pipelines.

keyInsights: Value types bridge the gap between domain-agnostic primitive types and domain-specific semantics, capturing context and meaning beyond what column names provide.
Value types enforce validation constraints in both Pipeline Builder (data integration) and the Ontology, ensuring consistent data quality across the platform.
Value types are scoped to a Space and cannot be used across spaces, maintaining governance boundaries.
Value types are versioned and permissioned separately, enabling controlled evolution and reuse across multiple pipelines and object types.
Unlike other Ontology types (Object Type, Property, Link Type), value types are not available for the Default ontology and require a Space.

title: Functions - Functions on objects - Object sets - Palantir
summary: An object set represents an unordered collection of objects of a single type. Functions APIs support filtering (phrase, phrasePrefix, matchAllTokens), ordering (orderByRelevance), and retrieval (take, takeAsync). Filtering only works on properties with the Searchable render hint enabled. Supports Search Arounds to traverse link types.
relevantParts: ObjectSet as unordered collection of single-type objects
Filter methods: phrase, phrasePrefix, matchAllTokens, fuzzyMatchAllTokens
Searchable render hint required for filtering/ordering/aggregation

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: An unordered collection of objects of a single type, supporting filtering, ordering, aggregation, and traversal via Search Arounds.

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: Operation to traverse from one object set to a related object set via defined link types, enabling graph-like navigation.

extractedEntities.name: Object Search API
extractedEntities.description: The Objects.search() interface for initiating searches against any imported object type, returning object sets for further operations.

extractedEntities.name: Filter Methods
extractedEntities.description: String property filters: exactMatch (exact value), phrase (all tokens in order), phrasePrefix (prefix match on last token), matchAllTokens (all tokens in any order), matchAnyToken (any token).

extractedEntities.name: Searchable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: A property render hint that must be enabled for filtering, ordering, and aggregation to work on that property via the search APIs.

extractedEntities.name: takeAsync Method
extractedEntities.description: Asynchronous method for materializing large object sets beyond the 10,000 object synchronous limit, supporting up to 100,000 objects.

extractedEntities.name: Aggregation API
extractedEntities.description: Functions for computing aggregated values on object sets: count, max, min, sum, avg, approximateDistinct, exactDistinct, with optional groupBy.

extractedEntities.name: Functions
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's TypeScript-based Functions framework for authoring business logic that operates on Ontology objects and object sets.

extractedEntities.name: Search-Filter-Aggregate Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of chaining object search, filtering, ordering, and aggregation operations to derive analytical results from object sets.

keyInsights: Filtering, ordering, and aggregation only work on properties with the Searchable render hint enabled; this is a common gotcha for developers.
Object sets can be created from searches, lists of objects, object resource identifiers, or object set resource identifiers.
The synchronous take() method is limited to 10,000 objects; takeAsync() supports up to 100,000 objects for larger result sets.
Search Arounds traverse link types to navigate from one object set to related objects, enabling graph-like traversal in functions.
String filtering supports multiple modes: exactMatch, phrase (tokenized in-order), phrasePrefix (prefix on last token), matchAllTokens (any order), and matchAnyToken (any single token).
Aggregations support count, max, min, sum, avg, approximateDistinct, and exactDistinct with optional groupBy for dimensional analysis.

title: Functions - Overview - Palantir
summary: A function is code-based logic that takes input parameters and returns output, natively integrated with the Ontology. Functions can take objects and object sets as input, read property values, and be used across action types and applications. Queries are read-only subsets of functions exposed through the API gateway with no side effects.
relevantParts: Native integration with objects and object sets
Query functions as read-only subset of functions

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-based logic that takes input parameters and returns output, natively integrated with the Ontology. Functions include first-class support for reading properties, traversing links, and making Ontology edits. Executed server-side in an isolated environment.

extractedEntities.name: Query
extractedEntities.description: A read-only subset of Functions exposed through the API gateway with no side effects. Queries cannot modify Ontology state and are safe for external consumption.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be returned by Functions for use in applications like Workshop dashboards.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic type in the Ontology whose properties can be read and links traversed by Functions.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology entity that can be backed by Functions to express complex edits updating many objects.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A typed attribute of an Object Type whose values can be read and transformed by Functions.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A defined relationship between Object Types that Functions can traverse to navigate the Ontology graph.

extractedEntities.name: External Function
extractedEntities.description: A Function variant that queries external systems to enrich objects in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for building dashboards and operational applications. Functions return object sets and values for use in Workshop, including function-backed columns and charts.

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application where Functions run backend logic to return information displayed in the frontend.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Palantir analytics application where Functions compute custom metrics or aggregations for display.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Palantir data transformation application where Python functions can be used as sidecar containers.

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript Functions
extractedEntities.description: Functions can be authored in TypeScript, with v1 and v2 language versions supported.

extractedEntities.name: Python Functions
extractedEntities.description: Functions can be authored in Python, supporting use cases including sidecar containers in Pipeline Builder.

keyInsights: Functions are the primary mechanism for embedding code-based business logic into the Ontology, bridging data and action.
Queries are a read-only, side-effect-free subset of Functions specifically designed for safe external API exposure.
Functions provide first-class Ontology integration: reading properties, traversing links, and making edits — not just arbitrary code execution.
Function-backed Actions enable complex multi-object edits expressed as code rather than declarative rules.
External Functions extend the Ontology beyond Foundry by querying external systems to enrich object data.
Functions execute server-side in isolated environments, ensuring security and resource control.

title: Ontology SDK - Overview - Palantir
summary: Official overview of the Ontology Software Development Kit (OSDK), which provides accelerated development with ergonomic Ontology API access. OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata rather than actual Ontology contents, supports lazy loading, and generates strongly typed interfaces. Available for TypeScript (NPM), Python (Pip/Conda), Java (Maven), and OpenAPI for other languages.
relevantParts: OSDK as generated type-safe library per Ontology
Multi-language support: TypeScript, Python, Java, OpenAPI
Subset-based generation for relevant Ontology entities
Multi-language SDK for external Ontology access
Token-scoped security model for data access
Multi-language SDK: TypeScript, Python, Java, OpenAPI
Type-safe generated code from Ontology definitions
OSDK 2.0 performance improvements and lazy loading

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Software Development Kit that provides type-safe, generated code for accessing the Ontology from external development environments. Supports TypeScript (NPM), Python (Pip/Conda), Java (Maven), and OpenAPI for other languages.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for creating and managing OSDK applications, including bootstrapping and configuring Ontology access.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that serves as the backend for OSDK applications, providing high-scale queries, writeback, and granular governance controls.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic type in the Ontology for which the OSDK generates strongly-typed interfaces and query functions.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attributes whose names and descriptions are embedded in OSDK-generated code as metadata available in the editor.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types that are represented as typed accessors in the generated OSDK code.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology actions that are exposed as type-safe functions in the generated OSDK code for performing writebacks.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology entity supported by the OSDK for polymorphic access to Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Code Generation
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK generates strongly-typed code from Ontology metadata, scaling linearly with Ontology shape rather than contents. Supports lazy loading.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK 2.0 Scaling
extractedEntities.description: OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata rather than actual Ontology contents, and supports lazy loading of generated types.

extractedEntities.name: Scoped Token
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK uses a token scoped only to the ontological entities the application needs to access, combined with the user's own data permissions.

keyInsights: The OSDK treats Foundry as a backend, enabling external applications to leverage Ontology queries and writeback with granular governance.
OSDK 2.0's linear scaling with Ontology shape (not contents) is a critical architectural property enabling large-scale Ontology adoption.
Strong type-safety is achieved by generating code from the specific subset of the Ontology relevant to each application, not the entire Ontology.
Security is built into the OSDK architecture: tokens are scoped to specific ontological entities, layered on top of user-level permissions.
Centralized Ontology maintenance means application developers focus on building, not maintaining the data foundation.
The OSDK supports four language ecosystems (TypeScript, Python, Java, OpenAPI), making the Ontology accessible across the development landscape.

title: GitHub - palantir/osdk-ts: Typescript related OSDK libraries
summary: The official open-source TypeScript OSDK monorepo containing multiple packages for interacting with Palantir's Ontology. Includes the @osdk/client package for creating clients, @osdk/oauth for authentication, and foundry-platform-typescript for standalone Foundry API access. Actively maintained with regular releases through February 2026.
relevantParts: Generated TypeScript interfaces from Ontology types
Source code for Ontology integration patterns
Primary TypeScript SDK source code and packages
Monorepo structure with turbo.json build configuration

extractedEntities.name: osdk-ts
extractedEntities.description: The official open-source TypeScript OSDK monorepo hosted on GitHub, containing multiple packages for interacting with Palantir's Ontology via TypeScript.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/client
extractedEntities.description: The core OSDK client package for TypeScript. Used to create an authenticated client that provides typed access to Ontology entities.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/oauth
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK OAuth package providing createPublicOauthClient and createConfidentialOauthClient for authentication against Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/api
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK API types package defining the core type interfaces used by the generated SDK.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/foundry-sdk-generator
extractedEntities.description: The code generator that produces type-safe TypeScript bindings from Ontology definitions in Developer Console.

extractedEntities.name: foundry-platform-typescript
extractedEntities.description: A standalone package providing direct Foundry API access without needing to generate ontology-specific SDKs.

extractedEntities.name: @osdk/create-app
extractedEntities.description: CLI tool for quickly bootstrapping a frontend application using the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Public OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: OAuth client type for browser/frontend applications requiring client ID, Foundry URL, and redirect URL. No client secret needed.

extractedEntities.name: Confidential OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: OAuth client type for server-side/backend applications requiring client ID, client secret, and Foundry URL. Used with service users.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application used to create OSDK applications and generate SDKs, providing the client ID and configuration needed for OAuth.

keyInsights: The TypeScript OSDK is a mature open-source monorepo with 2,844+ commits and active maintenance, signaling Palantir's commitment to external developer ecosystem.
Two distinct OAuth flows (public and confidential) support both browser-based and server-side application architectures.
foundry-platform-typescript provides a no-code-generation path for developers who want direct Foundry API access without ontology-specific types.
The @osdk/foundry-sdk-generator bridges the gap between Ontology definitions and type-safe TypeScript code.
The monorepo architecture separates concerns: client, auth, API types, code generation, and app scaffolding are all independent packages.

title: The Ontology system - Palantir
summary: Architectural overview of the Ontology as a multimodal system with dozens of components, grouped into Language (semantic types, kinetic actions, logic), Engine (read/write architecture), and Toolchain. The Engine provides SQL queries, real-time subscriptions, atomic transactions, batch mutations, and streaming.
relevantParts: Engine capabilities: SQL, real-time, transactions, batch, streaming
Multimodal system with dozens of underlying components

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The system at the heart of Palantir's architecture, designed to represent the complex, interconnected decisions of an enterprise — not simply the data. Enables humans and AI agents to collaborate across operational workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Data-Logic-Action-Security Integration
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology models decisions through the four-fold integration of data (semantic objects/properties/links), logic (business rules, ML, LLMs), action (transactions to edits), and security (role-based, attribute-based access).

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Language
extractedEntities.description: The semantic layer of the Ontology system comprising types (Object Types, Properties, Links), kinetic actions, and logic. Defines the vocabulary for modeling enterprise decisions.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Engine
extractedEntities.description: The read/write architecture of the Ontology system providing SQL queries, real-time subscriptions, atomic transactions, batch mutations, and streaming capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Toolchain
extractedEntities.description: The development and management tools for building, maintaining, and evolving the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Semantic types representing real-world entities (nouns) that unify disparate data sources into coherent objects with properties and links.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attributes that describe Object Types, forming the adjectives in the Ontology's semantic vocabulary.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Defined relationships connecting Object Types in a web of semantic connections.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Kinetic operations (verbs) that model the full range of actions from simple transactions to complex multi-step updates written back to operational systems in real time.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Security Model
extractedEntities.description: Security woven into data, logic, and actions. Different scopes of access are relevant for different team members (e.g., production teams see global telemetry, warehouse associates have regional restrictions).

extractedEntities.name: Airline Flight Operations
extractedEntities.description: An airline models flights, aircraft, crew manifests, scheduling optimizers into their ontology to power day-of operations and longer-range planning.

extractedEntities.name: Hospital System Operations
extractedEntities.description: A hospital models patients, nurse schedules, medical supplies, bed capacities for real-time patient lifecycle management.

extractedEntities.name: Medical Manufacturing
extractedEntities.description: A medical manufacturer models plants, work orders, customer details, shipments, with differentiated access for production teams, warehouse associates, and supply chain analysts.

keyInsights: The Ontology is explicitly designed to model decisions, not data — this is the core architectural distinction from traditional data platforms.
The four-fold integration of data, logic, action, and security is the fundamental design principle of the Ontology system.
Semantics (nouns) must be paired with kinetics (verbs) to model decisions — Object Types alone are insufficient without Action Types.
The Ontology system is decomposed into three major subsystems: Language (what you model), Engine (how it executes), and Toolchain (how you build it).
Security is not a bolt-on concern but is woven into every layer — data, logic, and actions each have security implications.
Logic underlying actions can range from simple business rules to LLM-driven functions to complex multi-step orchestrations, reflecting real-world decision diversity.

title: Overview and getting started - Overview - Palantir
summary: The Ontology backend manages datasource feeding, schema definitions, querying/searching/aggregating objects, and orchestration of writes. Object Set Service (OSS) serves reads with filtering, aggregating, and loading. Object Storage V2 supports higher-scale Search Arounds (100K+ objects) and accurate aggregations via Spark-based query layer.
relevantParts: Backend responsibilities: datasource management, querying, write orchestration
Object Set Service (OSS) as query layer
Object Storage V2 Spark-based query execution
Separation of indexing and querying subsystems
Object Storage V2 architecture with separated indexing and querying
Object Data Funnel microservice for data write orchestration
Evolution from Phonograph (V1) to V2 architecture

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Metadata Service
extractedEntities.description: Overarching service (OMS) that defines the set of ontological entities that exist — including metadata for object types, link types, action types, and more.

extractedEntities.name: Object Set Service
extractedEntities.description: Service (OSS) responsible for serving reads from the Ontology, enabling searching, filtering, aggregating, and loading of objects.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Next-generation canonical data store for backing the Ontology. Supports higher-scale Search Arounds (100K+ objects) and accurate aggregations via Spark-based query layer.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's legacy Ontology backend component, predecessor to Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Object Data Funnel
extractedEntities.description: Service responsible for indexing data from datasources into the Ontology object databases.

extractedEntities.name: Functions on Objects
extractedEntities.description: Backend service that executes Functions in the context of Ontology objects.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: Lists of real-world entities saved for future reference and use across Foundry applications. Saved as resources for easy sharing and application integration.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose schema, datasource, and metadata are managed by the Ontology backend services.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attributes of Object Types stored and indexed by object databases for querying and filtering.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types whose metadata is defined in OMS and whose traversals are served by OSS.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Structured, controlled modifications to object data orchestrated through the Ontology backend.

extractedEntities.name: Microservices Architecture
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry Ontology backend uses a microservices architecture where multiple services collectively handle datasource management, querying, and write orchestration.

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: A query operation traversing Link Types between objects. Object Storage V2 supports scales of 100K+ objects for Search Arounds.

keyInsights: The Ontology backend is a composition of specialized microservices: OMS for metadata, OSS for reads, Object Storage for persistence, Data Funnel for indexing, and Functions on Objects for computation.
The Ontology Metadata Service (OMS) is the authoritative source of truth for what ontological entities exist and their schemas.
Object Set Service (OSS) is the single read path for all Ontology queries — it mediates between applications and the underlying object databases.
Object Storage V2 represents a generational leap: Spark-based query layer enables 100K+ object Search Arounds and accurate aggregations that were limited in V1.
The Ontology serves as a digital twin containing both semantic elements (objects, properties, links) and kinetic elements (actions, functions, dynamic security).
ObjectSets are first-class resources — saved lists of entities that can be shared and reused across applications.

title: Ontology Manager - Overview - Palantir
summary: Ontology Manager is the primary tool for building and maintaining the Ontology, from creating object types and action types to connecting data. Accessed via Workspace sidebar or URL. Supports guided step-by-step creation with metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and key configuration.
relevantParts: Primary management tool for all Ontology configuration
Guided creation workflow for object types
Primary UI for Ontology construction and maintenance

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The primary Palantir application (also called OMA) for building and maintaining the Ontology. Provides UI for creating object types, defining action types, connecting data, and investigating data updates.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology entity created and configured in Ontology Manager with metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and primary key configuration.

extractedEntities.name: Property

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between Object Types visualized and configured through the link type graph in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology actions configured through a dedicated view in Ontology Manager, defining structured modifications to objects.

extractedEntities.name: Function Type
extractedEntities.description: Functions configured and viewed through a dedicated function type view in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Branch
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Manager supports branching for creating and testing Ontology changes in isolation before merging, analogous to Git branches.

extractedEntities.name: Property Mapping
extractedEntities.description: The configuration that maps Object Type properties to columns in a backing datasource, defining how raw data becomes semantic Ontology properties.
