keyInsights: Ontology Manager is the single pane of glass for all Ontology configuration — object types, properties, links, actions, and functions are all managed here.
Object Type creation follows a guided workflow: metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and primary key configuration.
Ontology branching enables safe, isolated changes to the Ontology schema before deployment — critical for production Ontology governance.
Shared Properties enable consistent semantic representation across multiple Object Types, promoting Ontology reuse.
The Discover view provides personalized navigation with favorites, recent views, and group-based organization for large Ontologies.
Property mapping is the bridge between raw data (datasource columns) and semantic Ontology attributes (properties).

title: Ontology-Oriented Software Development - Palantir Blog
summary: Introduces ontology-oriented development as a new paradigm where programmers abstract away internal implementation details and interact with business concepts. The OSDK provides a toolkit for building APIs in the language of the business. Aligns business users and engineers around business representation rather than underlying components.
relevantParts: Alignment of business users and engineers
Ontology-oriented development paradigm for AI applications
OSDK integration with AIP Logic functions

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Oriented Development
extractedEntities.description: A new software development paradigm where programmers abstract away internal implementation details and interact with business concepts. The OSDK functions as a higher-level abstraction for authoring business logic over harmonized critical business concepts.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Software Development Kit that enables ontology-oriented development by providing a toolkit for building APIs in the language of the business.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The technology that centralizes business knowledge and encapsulates it within a shared system, enabling defragmentation of the enterprise by integrating isolated components into a holistic system.

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Defragmentation
extractedEntities.description: The architectural pattern of integrating isolated software components into a holistic system through the Ontology, replacing the fragmented vendor-driven enterprise architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Decision-Centric Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Software architecture centered on modeling and executing enterprise decisions rather than optimizing individual data components.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Business entities modeled in the Ontology (e.g., Aircraft, Flights, Mechanics) that developers interact with directly rather than through low-level data access.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Business operations modeled in the Ontology (e.g., reassigning mechanics, rerouting flights) that are invoked as type-safe functions in the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction enabling polymorphic access to Object Types, allowing applications to work with shared contracts across different entity types.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-based logic operating on Ontology entities that is shared and reusable across applications through the OSDK.

extractedEntities.name: Airline Maintenance Optimization
extractedEntities.description: An airline uses the Ontology to model aircraft, flights, mechanics, and maintenance schedules, enabling real-time reassignment of mechanics when warning lights trigger during routes.

keyInsights: Ontology-oriented development is positioned as a new paradigm alongside object-oriented and functional programming — abstracting business concepts rather than data structures.
The core thesis: enterprise software failure is not a technology problem but an architecture problem — optimizing components in isolation degrades system-level performance.
The OSDK makes the marginal cost of bespoke enterprise software development approach zero by reusing shared Ontology integration work.
Knowledge compounds in the Ontology: each new application benefits from all previous integration work, creating a flywheel effect.
The Ontology aligns business users and engineers around shared business representation rather than underlying component architectures.
Interfaces enable polymorphic programming against the Ontology, allowing code to work across different Object Types sharing a common contract.

title: Ontology: Finding meaning in data - Palantir Blog
summary: Foundational blog post explaining what Palantir means by 'ontology'. Data does not have inherent meaning; meaning is layered onto data by users. An ontology maps data to meaningful semantic concepts. Defines the nouns, verbs, and adjectives of an organization (e.g., Accounts, Transactions, Financial Products for a bank).
relevantParts: Philosophical foundation: meaning vs raw data
Ontology as mapping from data to semantic concepts
Nouns, verbs, adjectives metaphor for organizations

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A systematic mapping of data to meaningful semantic concepts. Exists outside the data itself to establish a framework that empowers data integration, application building, and user collaboration. Functions regardless of the specific data present.

extractedEntities.name: Three Levels of Abstraction
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology operates at three levels: (1) Class definitions (abstract object type schemas), (2) Instantiated objects (real data mapped to classes), and (3) Operational use (linked, created, deleted, property changes).

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Class definitions in the Ontology representing the nouns of an organization (e.g., Accounts, Transactions, Financial Products for a bank).

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Defined attributes that describe Object Type classes, forming the adjectives in the Ontology's semantic vocabulary.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Defined relationships connecting object classes in a web, enabling navigation between related entities.

extractedEntities.name: Instantiated Object
extractedEntities.description: A concrete representation of data mapped into a defined Object Type class. Can be created, deleted, linked, unlinked, and have properties changed.

extractedEntities.name: Data Agnosticism
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology is data-agnostic: while data may inform how the Ontology is structured, the Ontology itself functions regardless of the specific data present in the ecosystem.

extractedEntities.name: Banking Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A bank models Accounts, Transactions, and Financial Products as Object Types in the Ontology, with defined properties and relationships between them.

extractedEntities.name: Nouns-Verbs-Adjectives Model
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology defines the nouns (Object Types), verbs (Actions/Links), and adjectives (Properties) of an organization's semantic vocabulary.

keyInsights: Data does not have inherent meaning — meaning is layered onto data by users through the Ontology, making Ontology design a fundamentally human activity.
The Ontology exists outside the data itself, functioning as a schema-level framework that is independent of specific data contents.
Data agnosticism is a key design principle: the Ontology should function regardless of what data is present, enabling it to evolve independently of data sources.
Three levels of abstraction (class definitions, instantiated objects, operational use) mirror the class/instance/operation pattern in object-oriented programming.
The Nouns-Verbs-Adjectives model provides an intuitive mental framework for Ontology design that maps directly to natural language thinking about business domains.
A functional data ecosystem must incorporate Ontology concepts to be scalable and sustainable — without semantic meaning, data remains inert.

title: Connecting AI to Decisions with the Palantir Ontology - Palantir Blog
summary: The Ontology brings together data, logic, and action into a decision-centric model. Every decision comprises data (information), logic (evaluation), and action (execution). The Ontology integrates these into a scalable, dynamic foundation. LLMs and AIP agents can query and reason over the Ontology using natural language.
relevantParts: AI/LLM integration with Ontology via natural language
Ontology as foundation for human + AI decision-making
Ontology as the bridge between AI and enterprise decisions
Extensible tools paradigm for LLM-Ontology interaction
Decision data as training data for model fine-tuning

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Decision-centric software architecture integrating data, logic, and action into a scalable, dynamic, collaborative foundation reflecting real-time organizational conditions.

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform that enables LLMs and AI agents to query and reason over the Ontology using natural language, safely leveraging AI in enterprise operations.

extractedEntities.name: Decision-Centric Model
extractedEntities.description: Every decision comprises three elements: data (information used to decide), logic (process of evaluating), and action (execution of the decision). The Ontology integrates all three.

extractedEntities.name: Tools Paradigm
extractedEntities.description: Rather than fine-tuning LLMs on enterprise data, AIP provides LLMs with tools (Ontology queries, actions, functions) that the AI calls to interact with the real world through the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Semantic types that integrate all modalities of data (ERPs, IoT, unstructured, geospatial) into a full-scale representation surfaced in the language of the enterprise.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Kinetic operations ranging from simple transactions to multi-step orchestrations. The Ontology captures the full lifecycle including guardrails, side effects, and downstream implications.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Logic elements that can be simple business rules, ML models, LLM-driven functions, or complex multi-step orchestrations. Composed and evolved over time.

extractedEntities.name: Decision Data
extractedEntities.description: Data generated by end users as decisions are made, including context, options evaluated, and downstream implications. Distinct from traditional enterprise data.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Governance
extractedEntities.description: Security model woven into the Ontology ensuring that both human users and AI agents operate within defined access controls, guardrails, and audit trails.

extractedEntities.name: Real-time Subscriptions
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Engine provides real-time subscription capabilities for live updates to Ontology state changes.

extractedEntities.name: Atomic Transactions
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology Engine supports atomic transactions ensuring consistency when multiple objects are modified simultaneously.

keyInsights: The Ontology is designed to represent decisions, not data — this is the fundamental architectural distinction enabling AI integration.
AIP uses a tools paradigm rather than fine-tuning: LLMs call Ontology tools (queries, actions, functions) rather than being trained on enterprise data.
Decision Data is a novel concept: the context, options, and implications generated during decision-making become first-class data in the Ontology, enabling learning loops.
The three elements of any decision (data, logic, action) map directly to the Ontology's three pillars, making the Ontology a natural substrate for AI-driven decisions.
Security governance for AI is the same governance used for humans — the Ontology's access controls, guardrails, and audit trails apply equally to LLM agents.
Real-time capabilities (subscriptions, atomic transactions, streaming) are essential Engine features for operational decision-making where latency matters.

title: Object and link types - Properties - Base types - Palantir
summary: Documents the available property base types in the Ontology including standard types (string, boolean, integer, etc.) and specialized types like Timestamp, Geohash/Geopoint, Geoshape, Attachment, and Time Series. The base type determines the set of operations available in user applications and affects indexing behavior.
relevantParts: Complete reference of available property base types
Specialized types: geospatial, time series, attachments
Base type determines available operations in applications

extractedEntities.name: Base Type
extractedEntities.description: The fundamental data type of a property in the Ontology, determining the set of operations available for that property in user applications. Includes standard types (string, boolean, integer) and advanced types.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A named attribute on an Object Type defined by a base type, which determines available operations and indexing behavior.

extractedEntities.name: Vector
extractedEntities.description: An advanced property base type for storing vectors on objects for use in semantic search.

extractedEntities.name: Geopoint
extractedEntities.description: An advanced property base type representing geographic point coordinates on objects.

extractedEntities.name: Geoshape
extractedEntities.description: An advanced property base type representing geographic shapes (polygons, lines, etc.) on objects.

extractedEntities.name: Attachment
extractedEntities.description: A property base type for storing files on objects for use with functions on objects.

extractedEntities.name: Time Series
extractedEntities.description: A property base type for defining a property as a time series, representing data points indexed by time.

extractedEntities.name: Media Reference
extractedEntities.description: A property base type for referencing media files (images, videos, audio, documents) stored in media sets, containing mimeType and a reference with media set RID, view RID, and media item RID.

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Text
extractedEntities.description: A property base type for storing a string value encoded with Cipher for encrypted storage.

extractedEntities.name: Struct
extractedEntities.description: An advanced property base type allowing schema-based properties with multiple fields, created from struct type dataset columns.

extractedEntities.name: Media Set
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource that stores media items (images, videos, audio, documents) which can be referenced by media reference properties on objects.

keyInsights: Base types define not just storage format but also the set of operations available on a property in user applications and indexing behavior.
All base types except Vector and Time Series can be used in arrays to represent multiple values for a single property.
Map, Decimal, and Binary field types are valid field types but NOT valid base types for properties.
Media Reference is a composite type containing mimeType, media set RID, view RID, and media item RID, requiring both a backing dataset column and a configured media source.
Advanced types like Vector, Geopoint, Geoshape, and Time Series extend the standard type system for domain-specific data modeling.
Cipher Text provides encrypted string storage, indicating built-in support for field-level encryption in the Ontology.

title: Object and link types - Link types - Create a link type - Palantir
summary: Step-by-step guide for creating link types in the Ontology Manager. Covers selecting the two object types to link, choosing cardinality, configuring foreign key relationships for one-to-one/many-to-one links, and selecting datasources for many-to-many links.
relevantParts: Practical guide for creating link types
Foreign key and datasource selection process

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A named, typed relationship between two Object Types in the Ontology, defined with a specific cardinality and backed by either foreign keys, a join table dataset, or a backing object type.

extractedEntities.name: Cardinality
extractedEntities.description: The relationship multiplicity constraint for a Link Type: one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, or many-to-many, indicating how many instances of each Object Type can participate in the link.

extractedEntities.name: Foreign Key
extractedEntities.description: A property on one Object Type that references the primary key of another Object Type, used to back one-to-one and many-to-one cardinality link types.

extractedEntities.name: Join Table Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A dataset used to back many-to-many cardinality link types, containing the foreign key columns that map relationships between two Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Object-Backed Link
extractedEntities.description: A link type storage solution where an Object Type itself serves as the backing for many-to-one cardinality links, providing first-class support for links as objects.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry application for creating, configuring, and managing Ontology resources including Object Types, Link Types, Interfaces, and other Ontology elements.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: A unique identifier property on an Object Type that distinguishes individual object instances and is referenced by foreign keys in link types.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A fundamental Ontology concept representing a class of real-world entities (e.g., Aircraft, Flight), defined with properties, links, and actions.

keyInsights: Link types support three distinct backing mechanisms: foreign key properties (one-to-one/many-to-one), join table datasets (many-to-many), and object-backed links (many-to-one with first-class object support).
One-to-one cardinality is an advisory indicator of intended relationship semantics but is NOT enforced by the system.
Foreign key link types require that one Object Type's property refers to the primary key property of the other Object Type.
Object-backed links promote link data to first-class objects, enabling properties and metadata directly on the relationship.
The Link Type creation helper in Ontology Manager guides users through relationship type selection, resource definition, and naming configuration.

title: Action types - Parameters - Overview - Palantir
summary: Action type parameters define the user inputs required when taking an action. Parameters can reference object types, properties, and other Ontology elements. Each rule property is mapped to a parameter value by default, and parameter dropdowns can be filtered to restrict available options.
relevantParts: Parameter system for user-facing action inputs
Mapping between parameters and rule properties

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology element that defines how objects should change when an action is applied, consisting of parameters (inputs), rules (logic), and submission criteria.

extractedEntities.name: Parameter
extractedEntities.description: An input variable of an Action Type that transports values from user applications to rules. Each parameter has a type, visibility configuration, and editability settings.

extractedEntities.name: Rule
extractedEntities.description: The logic component of an Action Type that defines how objects should change when the action is applied, receiving values from parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Submission Criteria
extractedEntities.description: Conditions that must be met for an Action Type to be submitted, using parameter values to validate whether the action can proceed.

extractedEntities.name: Override
extractedEntities.description: Action Type configuration that allows one parameter's value to dynamically change the configuration of a subsequent parameter.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application builder product where Action Types can be invoked via forms and where parameter values like previous_status can be used as variables.

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's legacy application builder product that can invoke Action Types through its interface.

extractedEntities.name: Object Views
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's object visualization product that provides the interface for users to interact with Action Type parameters.

keyInsights: Parameters are the interface between Action Type rules and Foundry applications (Workshop, Slate, Object Views), acting as typed variables that carry external values.
Parameters can reference the current value of an object property before it is changed by the action, enabling before/after comparisons.
Each parameter can be individually configured for form visibility (exposed or hidden) and user editability (changeable or read-only).
Parameters support Overrides, allowing one parameter's value to dynamically alter the configuration of subsequent parameters.
Parameter values flow into rules, submission criteria, and overrides, making them the central data transport mechanism of Action Types.

title: Action types - Function-backed actions - Overview - Palantir
summary: Function-backed actions use code (TypeScript/Python functions) to define action logic instead of declarative rules. When a function rule is present, no other rule may be configured since function code alone handles everything. This provides maximum flexibility for complex business logic in mutations.
relevantParts: Function-backed actions as alternative to declarative rules
Exclusive nature of function rules (replaces all other rules)
Code-based approach for complex action logic

extractedEntities.name: Function-Backed Action
extractedEntities.description: An Action Type configured to call a TypeScript or Python function that defines the mutation logic, replacing simple declarative rules with arbitrary code for complex business logic. When a function rule is present, no other rule may be configured.

extractedEntities.name: Simple Rule
extractedEntities.description: Declarative action rules that allow creating, modifying, and deleting objects or links without code. Sufficient for basic mutations but limited in expressiveness.

extractedEntities.name: Function Rule
extractedEntities.description: An Action Type rule backed by a function. When a function rule is present, it exclusively handles all mutation logic and no other rules can coexist.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-defined logic in TypeScript or Python that can read any number of objects and modify objects, used to back Action Types for complex business logic.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology element defining how objects change when an action is applied, either through simple rules or function-backed logic.

extractedEntities.name: Function-Backed Action Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where code functions replace declarative rules in Action Types, enabling complex multi-object mutations, computed values, and cross-object link traversals during edits.

keyInsights: Function-backed actions are mutually exclusive with simple rules: when a function rule is present, no other rule type may be configured.

Key use cases for function-backed actions include: modifying linked objects together (e.g., closing an Incident and resolving all linked Alerts), computing values from multiple objects, and creating multiple object types with links in one action.
Function-backed actions are subject to both action type limits and function execution limits, imposing constraints on complexity.
Functions in function-backed actions can read any number of objects and modify objects freely, providing maximum flexibility for complex business logic.
Simple rules handle create, modify, and delete for objects and links, while function-backed actions handle everything else.

title: Action types - Actions on interfaces - Palantir
summary: Actions can operate on interfaces for polymorphic behavior. Interface-based rules include: create object(s) of interface, modify object(s) of interface, and delete object(s) of interface. This allows a single action type to work across any object type implementing a given interface.
relevantParts: Interface as abstraction layer for action types

extractedEntities.name: Interface Action Rule
extractedEntities.description: Action rules that operate on all Object Types implementing a given Interface, supporting create, modify, and delete operations on Interface shared properties.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Reference Parameter
extractedEntities.description: A specialized Action Type parameter constrained to a specific Interface, allowing users to select any object that implements that Interface, similar to an object reference parameter but polymorphic.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An abstract type definition in the Ontology that specifies shared properties and link constraints, enabling polymorphic behavior when multiple Object Types implement it.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Polymorphism
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where a single Action Type works across any Object Type that implements a given Interface, enabling generic CRUD operations on shared properties.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Parameter
extractedEntities.description: Auto-generated parameter in Interface create actions that indicates which concrete Object Type should be created, since the action only knows the Interface.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology element defining how objects change when applied, which can operate on specific Object Types or on Interfaces for polymorphic behavior.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property defined on an Interface that all implementing Object Types must (or optionally may) map to one of their local properties.

keyInsights: Interface action rules can only modify shared properties of the interface, not properties specific to individual implementing object types.
Create actions on interfaces auto-generate an 'Object Type' parameter so the user specifies which concrete type to instantiate.
Objects cannot be created without a primary key; all implementing object types must have a primary key assigned in the create rule or the submission will fail.
Modify actions on interfaces cannot modify primary key properties; if an interface property maps to a primary key on some implementing types, the action will fail for those types.
Interface reference parameters show objects of any type implementing the interface, enabling polymorphic object selection in forms and tables.
Interface actions apply the same submission criteria and permissions across all implementing object types.

title: Interfaces - Create an interface - Palantir
summary: Guide for creating interfaces in the Ontology Manager, including defining shared properties, configuring link type constraints, and setting metadata. Covers the process of building abstract type definitions that multiple object types can implement.
relevantParts: Configuration of shared properties and link constraints

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An abstract type definition in the Ontology that specifies shared properties and link type constraints. Object Types implement interfaces by mapping their local properties to interface properties.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Property
extractedEntities.description: A property defined on an Interface that can be either required (implementing Object Types must map a local property) or optional (mapping may be skipped).

extractedEntities.name: Link Type Constraint
extractedEntities.description: An optional constraint on an Interface that specifies a required or expected link to another Interface or Object Type, which implementing types must satisfy.

extractedEntities.name: Required Property
extractedEntities.description: An Interface property that mandates all implementing Object Types provide a mapping from a local property to the interface property.

extractedEntities.name: Optional Property
extractedEntities.description: An Interface property where implementing Object Types may skip the property mapping, useful in Marketplace packages to iterate on interfaces without introducing upgrade blockers.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry application for creating and managing Ontology resources where interfaces are defined, configured, and saved.

extractedEntities.name: Marketplace
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's package distribution system where interfaces can be packaged and installed, with optional properties helping avoid upgrade blockers.

keyInsights: Interface properties can be defined locally on the interface (recommended) or reference existing shared properties.
Required interface properties mandate that all implementing Object Types provide a property mapping, while optional properties allow skipping.
Optional properties are specifically useful for Marketplace packages to iterate on interfaces without introducing upgrade blockers for consumers.
Link type constraints on interfaces define expected relationships that implementing Object Types must satisfy with new or existing link types.
Interfaces must be saved to a project and then persisted to the Ontology via the Save action in Ontology Manager.

title: Interfaces - Extend an interface - Palantir
summary: Interfaces support inheritance: you can extend an interface to create a child interface that inherits properties of the parent, then add new specific properties to the child. This enables hierarchical type modeling similar to class inheritance in OOP.
relevantParts: Child interfaces inherit parent shared properties

extractedEntities.name: Interface Extension
extractedEntities.description: The mechanism by which one Interface inherits the shared properties and link type constraints of another Interface, enabling hierarchical type composition.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An abstract Ontology type that can extend any number of other interfaces, inheriting their shared properties and links while adding its own.

extractedEntities.name: Interface Inheritance
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where child interfaces extend parent interfaces to inherit properties and links, similar to class inheritance in object-oriented programming. An interface can extend multiple parents.

extractedEntities.name: Capability Interface Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where abstract object interfaces implement multiple capability interfaces through extension, composing specialized behaviors into more specific types.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A property inherited from a parent interface to a child interface through extension, maintaining consistent property definitions across the interface hierarchy.

keyInsights: Interface extension supports multiple inheritance: an interface can extend any number of other interfaces.
Extension is the primary mechanism for composing capability interfaces into more specific abstract object interfaces.
A child interface inherits both shared properties and link type constraints from the parent interface.
Removing an extension decouples the child from the parent, removing all inherited shared properties and link type constraints.
Interface extension enables hierarchical type modeling analogous to class inheritance in object-oriented programming.

title: Object and link types - Shared properties - Use shared properties on object types - Palantir
summary: When using a shared property on an object type, the property ID and API name remain unchanged to avoid breaking downstream workflows. While associated with a shared property, direct edits to metadata inherited from the shared property are disabled on the object type.
relevantParts: Backward compatibility when adopting shared properties
Practical usage patterns for shared properties

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: A reusable property definition that can be associated with multiple Object Types. When used, the Object Type inherits metadata from the shared property while retaining its original property ID and API name.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: An attribute on an Object Type that can optionally be associated with a Shared Property, inheriting centralized metadata while maintaining backward compatibility.

extractedEntities.name: Property ID
extractedEntities.description: The internal identifier of a property on an Object Type that remains unchanged when associating with a shared property, ensuring existing downstream workflows are not broken.

extractedEntities.name: API Name
extractedEntities.description: The programmatic name of a property used in SDKs and APIs that remains unchanged when associating with a shared property, ensuring backward compatibility.

extractedEntities.name: Type Class
extractedEntities.description: A classification tag on properties. When associated with a shared property, the resulting set of type classes is a union of those from both the property and its shared property.

extractedEntities.name: Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Configuration values that control how a property is displayed in UI applications. Using a shared property with different render hints overrides the local property's render hint configuration.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry application where shared properties are associated with, configured on, and detached from Object Type properties.

keyInsights: When associating a property with a shared property, the property ID and API name remain unchanged to avoid breaking existing downstream workflows and SDK integrations.
While associated with a shared property, direct edits to inherited metadata are disabled on the Object Type property, enforcing centralized control.
Type classes merge: the effective set of type classes is a union of those from both the local property and the shared property.
Shared property render hints override local property render hints, so render hint configuration must be validated before association.
Properties can be detached from shared properties, removing the association and restoring independent metadata control.

title: Object and link types - Value types - Value type constraints - Palantir
summary: Documents the constraint system for value types, including regex patterns, range constraints, and other validation rules that enforce data quality. Constraints defined on a value type are automatically applied wherever that value type is used.
relevantParts: Constraint types available for value types
Automatic constraint propagation to all uses
Data quality enforcement via type system

extractedEntities.name: Value Type
extractedEntities.description: A reusable type definition in the Ontology that wraps a base type with optional constraints, enforcing data validation wherever the value type is used.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Constraint
extractedEntities.description: An optional validation rule on a Value Type that enforces data quality, including enum, range, regex, RID, UUID, uniqueness, and nested constraints.

extractedEntities.name: Enum Constraint
extractedEntities.description: A Value Type constraint representing a static set of allowed values, applicable to String, Boolean, Decimal, Double, Float, Integer, or Short base types. String enums can be case-sensitive or case-insensitive.

extractedEntities.name: Range Constraint
extractedEntities.description: A Value Type constraint specifying minimum/maximum bounds or a range of allowed values, applicable to numeric types, dates, timestamps, strings (length), and arrays (size).

extractedEntities.name: Regex Constraint
extractedEntities.description: A String-specific Value Type constraint requiring the value to match a regex pattern, with optional substring matching mode.

extractedEntities.name: Uniqueness Constraint
extractedEntities.description: An Array-specific Value Type constraint requiring all elements of the array to be unique.

extractedEntities.name: Nested Constraint
extractedEntities.description: An Array-specific Value Type constraint that applies another value type constraint to each element of the array (e.g., regex on every string in an array).

extractedEntities.name: Struct Element Constraint
extractedEntities.description: A Struct-specific constraint that maps struct field identifiers to value type references, applying validation to individual fields within a struct.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Manager
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry application for creating and configuring Value Types with their associated constraints.

keyInsights: Value Type constraints provide a reusable data validation layer: constraints defined on a value type automatically apply everywhere that value type is used.
Constraints are type-aware: Enum applies to 7 base types, Range to 8 types (with semantic variations for strings and arrays), and Regex/RID/UUID are String-specific.
Array constraints include Uniqueness (all elements must be unique) and Nested (apply a value type constraint to each element), enabling recursive validation.
Struct element constraints map individual struct field identifiers to value type references, providing per-field validation within composite types.
String Enum constraints support both case-sensitive and case-insensitive matching modes.
Range constraints have different semantics depending on base type: numeric bounds for numbers, length for strings, and size for arrays.

title: Object and link types - Structs - Overview - Palantir
summary: A struct is an Ontology property base type allowing schema-based properties with multiple fields. Struct properties are created from struct type dataset columns. Nested structs are not supported. Structs are supported across Ontology Manager, Actions, Pipeline Builder, Workshop, and the OSDK.
relevantParts: Struct as composite property type with multiple fields

extractedEntities.name: Struct
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology property base type that allows schema-based properties with multiple fields (depth of one, 1-10 fields), created from struct type dataset columns. Supports 12 field types but cannot be nested.

extractedEntities.name: Struct Field
extractedEntities.description: An individual typed element within a Struct property, supporting 12 data types: Boolean, Byte, Date, Decimal, Double, Float, Geopoint, Integer, Long, Short, String, and Timestamp.

extractedEntities.name: Struct Query Semantics
extractedEntities.description: Structs are indexed like ElasticSearch object field types. In arrays of structs, conditions on different fields are evaluated independently across array elements, leading to potential cross-element false positives.

extractedEntities.name: Struct Full Name Example
extractedEntities.description: A Full Name struct property with First Name and Last Name fields, demonstrating how common composite attributes can be modeled as structs.

extractedEntities.name: Struct Address Example
extractedEntities.description: An Address struct property with Street, City, Postal Code, and Country fields, demonstrating multi-field composite property modeling.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for defining and editing struct types on Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application builder that can display and use struct properties as variables.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry data pipeline tool for defining and editing struct type dataset columns that back struct properties.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit that supports loading struct properties and searching by struct property values, though not all OSDK variants support structs.

keyInsights: Structs have a strict depth of one (no nesting), must have 1-10 fields, and support 12 field types including Geopoint.
Struct query semantics follow ElasticSearch object field indexing: in arrays of structs, conditions on different fields match independently across array elements, causing potential false positives.
Structs are NOT supported in Object Storage V1 (Phonograph) and can only be created from datasets and Restricted Views.
TypeScript v2 and Python functions support struct parameters and struct property edits; not all OSDK variants support struct properties.
Struct property fields can have different data sources as long as they are transformed into a single struct type column before Ontology definition.

title: Object Views - Overview - Palantir
summary: Object Views are customizable, reusable representations of object data serving as a central hub for all information related to an object. Two types exist: Full Object Views (comprehensive overview) and Panel Object Views (focused on critical data for specific workflows). Views include property data, object links, and related applications.
relevantParts: Object Views as reusable data representations
Full vs Panel Object View distinction
Views compose property data, links, and applications

extractedEntities.name: Object View
extractedEntities.description: A reusable representation of object data that serves as a central hub for all information related to an object, including property data, object links, and related applications. Available as Core or Custom views.

extractedEntities.name: Core Object View
extractedEntities.description: A standardized, out-of-the-box object representation that automatically reflects an Object Type's configuration without any manual setup. Available for all Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Object View
extractedEntities.description: A fully customizable object representation built using Workshop, configured for specific workflow needs. When set, it becomes the default view but Core Object Views remain accessible.

extractedEntities.name: Full Object View
extractedEntities.description: A form factor for Object Views providing a comprehensive, in-depth overview of all related information about an object.

extractedEntities.name: Panel Object View
extractedEntities.description: A form factor for Object Views designed for integration with other applications, focusing on the most critical data for a specific workflow.

extractedEntities.name: Patient Object View Example
extractedEntities.description: A custom full Object View for a Patient object showing demographics, vitals, linked procedures/prescriptions/diagnoses, and analytical trends from historical records.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application builder used to create Custom Object Views with fully customizable representations for specific workflows.

keyInsights: Object Views have two independent dimensions: type (Core vs Custom) and form factor (Full vs Panel), creating four possible combinations.
Core Object Views are auto-generated from Object Type configuration and remain accessible even after Custom Object Views are configured.
Custom Object Views are built using Workshop, enabling fully customizable representations tailored to specific workflow needs.
Panel Object Views are designed for embedding in other applications, showing only the most critical data, while Full Object Views show comprehensive information.
Users can toggle between Core and Custom Object Views at any time, ensuring the auto-generated view always serves as a fallback.

title: Functions - Publish and call query functions through API gateway - Palantir
summary: Query types represent logic that can be run in the Ontology, created using Query Functions. Queries are read-only, cannot have side effects, and can be exposed through the API gateway for external consumption. Queries can be searched and managed in Ontology Manager.
relevantParts: Query functions as API gateway-exposed read logic
API gateway architecture for exposing Functions
Query function publication and external access patterns

extractedEntities.name: Query
extractedEntities.description: A read-only subset of Functions that can be optionally exposed through the API gateway. Queries cannot have side effects such as modifying the Ontology or altering external systems.

extractedEntities.name: Query Function
extractedEntities.description: A function decorated with @Query (TypeScript v1), exported config with apiName (TypeScript v2), or @function decorator (Python) that defines read-only logic exposed through the API gateway.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Code-defined logic in TypeScript or Python that operates on the Ontology, with Queries being the read-only subset and Actions being the write subset.

extractedEntities.name: Query Decorator TypeScript v1
extractedEntities.description: The @Query decorator from @foundry/functions-api with an apiName parameter, used to expose TypeScript v1 functions as read-only queries through the API gateway.

extractedEntities.name: Query Config TypeScript v2
extractedEntities.description: An exported config object with apiName property from the function file, used to expose TypeScript v2 functions as read-only queries through the API gateway.

extractedEntities.name: Query Decorator Python
extractedEntities.description: The @function decorator from functions.api with api_name parameter, used to expose Python functions as read-only queries through the API gateway.

extractedEntities.name: API Name Validation Rules
extractedEntities.description: Query API names must be lowerCamelCase, under 100 characters, without leading numbers, and unique among all Ontologies imported into the repository.

extractedEntities.name: API Gateway
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's API gateway service that exposes Query functions and Action types for external consumption via REST endpoints.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application where query types can be searched and managed as Ontology resources.

keyInsights: Queries are strictly read-only functions: they cannot modify the Ontology or alter external systems. Actions should be used for write operations through the API gateway.
Three distinct syntax patterns exist for defining queries: @Query decorator (TypeScript v1), exported config object (TypeScript v2), and @function decorator (Python).
API names for queries must be lowerCamelCase, under 100 characters, have no leading numbers, and be globally unique across all Ontologies in the repository.
TypeScript v2 queries use @osdk/client and @ontology/sdk for client access and object type imports, while v1 uses @foundry/functions-api and @foundry/ontology-api.
Queries are first-class Ontology resources that can be searched and managed in Ontology Manager alongside Object Types and Action Types.

title: Functions - TypeScript v2 - Ontology edits - Palantir
summary: TypeScript v2 functions can perform Ontology edits using the Edits type and createEditBatch from @osdk/functions. Functions must explicitly declare edited entities. Edits include Object and Link types. Struct properties can be edited using TypeScript interfaces matching struct fields. Function-backed Actions provide permissions and metadata.
relevantParts: TypeScript v2 edit API: Edits type, createEditBatch
Struct property editing via TypeScript interfaces
Programmatic Ontology edit patterns via Functions

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Edit
extractedEntities.description: A mutation operation on the Ontology performed within a TypeScript v2 function, including creating objects, updating properties, creating/deleting links, and deleting objects. Must be configured as a function-backed Action to take effect.

extractedEntities.name: Edits Type
extractedEntities.description: A type system from @osdk/functions for declaring which entities a function can edit. Supports Edits.Object<T>, Edits.Interface<T>, and Edits.Link<T, linkName>, combined with the | operator for multiple entity types.

extractedEntities.name: createEditBatch
extractedEntities.description: Function from @osdk/functions that creates an edit batch to track all Ontology modifications in a function. Provides update, create, delete, addLink, and removeLink methods.

extractedEntities.name: Edit Batch Update Method
extractedEntities.description: Method on edit batch for modifying object properties. Accepts either an object instance or a reference by API name and primary key. Property changes are not immediately reflected when reading the object in the same function execution.

extractedEntities.name: Edit Batch Create Method
extractedEntities.description: Method on edit batch for creating new objects. Requires specifying the object type and all required properties. Can generate unique primary keys using createPrimaryKey().

extractedEntities.name: Function-Backed Action
extractedEntities.description: An Action Type configured with a function that performs Ontology edits. Required for edits to actually be applied; running an edit function outside of an Action will not modify any data.

extractedEntities.name: Edits.Interface Type
extractedEntities.description: Type from @osdk/functions for declaring edits to interface-typed objects, enabling polymorphic edit operations across all object types implementing an interface.

extractedEntities.name: Edits.Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Type from @osdk/functions for declaring link creation and deletion between objects, parameterized by the source object type and link name.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit providing the @osdk/functions and @osdk/client packages used for TypeScript v2 function development and Ontology edits.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A fundamental Ontology type representing entities that can be created, updated, and deleted through edit batch operations.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A relationship between Object Types that can be created and deleted through edit batch addLink and removeLink operations.

keyInsights: Ontology edit functions MUST be configured as function-backed Actions for edits to take effect; running edit functions outside of Actions will not modify any data.
The Edits type system requires explicit declaration of all entities a function will edit, combining Object, Interface, and Link edit types with the | operator.
Edit batch update method accepts either an in-memory object instance or a reference by API name and primary key, enabling edits without loading objects.
Property changes made via edit batch are NOT immediately reflected when reading the object later in the same function execution -- a critical caveat for sequential logic.
Searching for objects immediately after editing them may return unexpected results due to eventual consistency in Ontology indexing.
Struct properties can be edited using TypeScript interfaces matching struct fields, enabling structured data mutation within edit functions.

title: Functions - Functions on objects - Objects and links - Palantir
summary: Describes how functions interact with objects and links in the Ontology. Properties of each object type are converted to fields on generated TypeScript interfaces. Links can be traversed as ObjectSets to avoid loading linked objects into memory. Search Around APIs are generated for linked object sets.
relevantParts: TypeScript interface generation for object types
Search Around API generation for links

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript-based logic that interacts with Ontology objects and links, enabling programmatic access to properties, link traversals, and search-around operations

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology entity whose properties are converted to fields on a generated TypeScript interface, accessible via dot notation in Functions

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A characteristic of an object type, converted to a TypeScript field that may return undefined if no concrete value is set

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A relationship between object types converted to fields on TypeScript interfaces, supporting 1-to-1, 1-to-many, and many-to-many cardinalities

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A lazy collection of objects that can be traversed via links without loading individual object instances into memory

extractedEntities.name: SingleLink
extractedEntities.description: Generated TypeScript type for the '1' side of a link, providing get() and getAsync() methods to retrieve the linked object

extractedEntities.name: MultiLink
extractedEntities.description: Generated TypeScript type for the 'many' side of a link, providing all() and allAsync() methods to retrieve an array of linked objects

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: APIs generated on ObjectSets when links are created, enabling navigation from one object set to linked object sets without loading individual objects

extractedEntities.name: ReadOnlyArray
extractedEntities.description: Immutable array type used for array properties and link traversal results, requiring explicit copy to modify

extractedEntities.name: Lazy Link Traversal
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of traversing links as ObjectSets to avoid loading linked object instances into memory, improving performance for large datasets

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform that hosts the Ontology and generates TypeScript APIs for object types and their links

keyInsights: Object type properties are code-generated into TypeScript interfaces with dot-notation access, and may return undefined requiring explicit null handling
Array properties use ReadOnlyArray to enforce immutability semantics -- modifications require creating a copy first
Link traversal supports both eager loading (get/all methods) and lazy ObjectSet-based traversal via Search Around APIs to avoid memory overhead
Search Around APIs are automatically generated when links are created in the Ontology, enabling ObjectSet-to-ObjectSet navigation
Link types support three cardinalities: 1-to-1, 1-to-many, and many-to-many, each with different generated TypeScript types (SingleLink vs MultiLink)

title: Models in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Describes how machine learning and AI models integrate with the Ontology. Models can take objects and object sets as input, read property values, and produce outputs that feed back into the Ontology. Modeling efforts build on each other over time, reducing duplicated work across use cases.
relevantParts: ML/AI model integration with Ontology primitives
Models as consumers and producers of Ontology data
Compounding value of shared modeling efforts
Model registration and deployment within the Ontology
Modeling results as Ontology object properties
Abstraction of ML complexity through business concepts

extractedEntities.name: Model
extractedEntities.description: A machine learning or AI model integrated into the Ontology, deployed for live inference and mapped to Ontology concepts for interpretability and reuse

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose properties can serve as inputs and outputs for ML models, enabling interpretable modeling results

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be passed as input to ML models for batch or live inference

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of object types used by models as input features or output targets, making ML results interpretable as real-world concepts like forecasts or classifications

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Wrapper logic published around a deployed model to orchestrate complex inference and expose it to end-user applications

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the data foundation, model evaluation tools, and deployment capabilities for operationalizing AI/ML

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: End-user application where deployed model functions can be consumed for live inference

extractedEntities.name: Vertex
extractedEntities.description: End-user application for consuming deployed model functions alongside Workshop

extractedEntities.name: Digital Twin
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology becomes a single source of truth for both data and logic, enabling enterprise-wide simulation of future changes using ML models

extractedEntities.name: Direct Model Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Configuration that deploys a model for live inference, making it callable via wrapper functions in the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Batch Inference
extractedEntities.description: Using a model in Code Repositories to produce datasets that back Ontology objects, as opposed to live inference

keyInsights: Models are integrated into the Ontology by mapping their inputs/outputs to object type properties, making ML results interpretable as real-world concepts like forecasts, estimates, or classifications
The end-to-end ML operationalization workflow is: create model, configure deployment, publish wrapper function, consume in user-facing applications
Modeling efforts achieve economies of scale because forecasts and predictions produced for one use case can immediately serve subsequent use cases through the shared Ontology
The Ontology becomes a digital twin encoding both data and organizational logic/expectations, enabling enterprise-wide simulation and future-state prediction
Models can operate in two modes: live inference (via direct model deployment + functions) and batch inference (via Code Repositories backing object type datasets)

title: Derived properties - Palantir
summary: Derived properties are calculated at runtime based on other properties or links. Types include linked property/aggregation (from 1:1 or 1:N links) and column math (combining multiple properties). Computed in real time, they may impact module loading times. Currently a beta feature with expanding capabilities.
relevantParts: Runtime-computed properties from other properties or links
Two types: linked property/aggregation and column math

extractedEntities.name: Derived Property
extractedEntities.description: A property calculated at runtime based on other properties or links, including aggregations on linked objects, available for filtering, sorting, and aggregation within the same request

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A stored characteristic of an object type that can serve as the basis for derived property calculations

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship between object types used by derived properties to access and aggregate values from linked objects

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity on which derived properties are defined, with support currently limited to OSv2-indexed types

extractedEntities.name: withProperties Operation
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript OSDK operation for requesting derived properties, available in @osdk/client 2.2.0-beta.x or later

extractedEntities.name: Linked Property Aggregation
extractedEntities.description: Type of derived property that aggregates or selects values from 1:1 or 1:N linked objects at runtime

extractedEntities.name: Column Math
extractedEntities.description: Type of derived property that combines multiple properties of the same object using mathematical expressions

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK providing TypeScript client for accessing derived properties via the withProperties operation

extractedEntities.name: Derived Property Security
extractedEntities.description: Security model where derived properties use the security of all objects involved in the calculation, never exposing information a user could not otherwise see

keyInsights: Derived properties are computed at runtime (not stored) and can be based on other properties, link traversals, or aggregations on linked objects
Two main types of derived properties exist: linked property/aggregation (from 1:1 or 1:N links) and column math (combining multiple properties on the same object)
Derived properties inherit the security model of all objects involved in their calculation, ensuring no information leakage
Currently a beta feature with limitations: no OSv1 support, no text search, no struct properties in OSDK queries (workaround: use $select to exclude structs)
Derived properties can chain into further operations -- once computed they can be used for additional filtering, sorting, or aggregation within the same request

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Statuses - Palantir
summary: Every Ontology resource has a developmental status: active, experimental, deprecated, or example. Object types also support 'endorsed' status (beta) for core trusted resources. Status changes cascade: changing an object type from active to experimental marks all its properties experimental too.
relevantParts: Lifecycle status system: active, experimental, deprecated, example, endorsed
Status cascade from object type to child properties
Endorsed status for vetted core object types

extractedEntities.name: Status
extractedEntities.description: Metadata indicating the developmental state of an Ontology resource: active, experimental, deprecated, example, or endorsed (object types only)

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that supports all status values including the special endorsed status, with cascading status changes to child properties

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristic of an object type whose status can be automatically cascaded when the parent object type's status changes

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship between object types that carries its own developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource for defining data modification operations that carries developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction mechanism that carries developmental status metadata

extractedEntities.name: Resource Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for managing Ontology resources through developmental stages: experimental to active to deprecated, with endorsed as a higher trust level

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Owner Role
extractedEntities.description: Role required to directly apply the endorsed status to an object type; other users must submit proposals for owner approval

extractedEntities.name: Endorsed Status
extractedEntities.description: Beta status for object types only, signifying a core trusted resource vetted by ontology owners, with automatic visibility set to prominent and protections on API name changes

extractedEntities.name: Deprecated Status Metadata
extractedEntities.description: Additional metadata on deprecated resources including deprecation reason, expected deletion deadline, and replacement resource reference

keyInsights: Every Ontology resource (object types, properties, link types, actions, interfaces) has a developmental status that communicates its readiness for production use

Five status values exist: active (in production), experimental (under development), deprecated (slated for deletion), example (notional/training), and endorsed (core trusted resource, object types only)
Status changes cascade: changing an object type from active to experimental automatically marks all its properties as experimental
Endorsed status is restricted to object types and requires the Ontology Owner role to apply directly; other users must submit proposals
Deprecated resources carry rich metadata: deprecation reason, expected deletion deadline, and a reference to the replacement resource
Endorsed status automatically sets visibility to prominent, increasing discoverability, and inherits the same operational protections as active status (e.g., deletion restrictions)

title: Object and link types - Metadata - Render hints - Palantir
summary: Render hints communicate information about Ontology property usage to Object Storage and user applications. Examples include sortable (enables sorting), searchable (enables filtering/aggregation), and visibility (controls display prominence). Some render hints require reindexing to take effect, affecting performance.
relevantParts: Render hints as metadata controlling application behavior
Performance implications: some hints require reindexing

extractedEntities.name: Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Metadata on Ontology properties that communicates usage information to Object Storage and user applications, controlling indexing, display, and interaction behavior

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristic of an object type to which render hints are applied, controlling how the property is indexed and displayed

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose properties carry render hints, with some hints requiring a full reindex to take effect

extractedEntities.name: Sortable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables sorting on a property in applications like timelines and charts; adds a raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Searchable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables filtering and aggregation on a property; prerequisite for low-cardinality filtering; adds raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Selectable Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Enables aggregation on string properties; adds raw index and requires reindex

extractedEntities.name: Visibility Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Controls display prominence of a property in applications: prominent (shown first), normal, or hidden

extractedEntities.name: Low Cardinality Render Hint
extractedEntities.description: Indicates few possible values for a property, enabling specific filter widgets; requires the Searchable hint and adds raw index

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage system that uses render hints to optimize indexing behavior and column storage

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: User application that uses render hints to determine property display, filtering, and sorting capabilities

keyInsights: Render hints are a metadata system that bridges Ontology property definitions and their storage/display behavior in applications and Object Storage
Some render hints add raw indexes in Object Storage V1, causing two columns to be counted per property -- deselecting them improves reindex performance
Render hints fall into two categories: those applied immediately when saved, and those requiring a full reindex of the backing datasource to take effect
The Searchable hint is a prerequisite for Low Cardinality, creating a dependency chain between render hints
Visibility render hint controls prominence in applications: prominent properties appear first, hidden properties are not shown at all, normal is the default
Render hints like Identifier optimize reindex performance by indicating that numerical keys do not need number formatting or range filtering

title: Object and link types - Object types - Create an object type - Palantir
summary: Step-by-step guide for creating object types including metadata (display name, ID, description), backing datasource selection, property mapping, and key configuration. Primary way is via guided helper. All object types must have a title key and unique primary key. A single datasource can only back one object type.
relevantParts: Object type creation workflow in Ontology Manager
Required elements: title key, primary key, backing datasource
Single datasource per object type constraint

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity created via a guided helper in Ontology Manager, requiring metadata, backing datasource, property mappings, and key configuration

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier property required for every object type, ensuring each object instance is uniquely identifiable

extractedEntities.name: Title Key
extractedEntities.description: Required property designating the human-readable display name for objects of a type, shown in user applications

extractedEntities.name: Backing Datasource
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry dataset or external source that provides the underlying data for an object type; a single datasource can only back one object type

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attribute of an object type, automatically mapped from backing datasource columns or manually configured

extractedEntities.name: API Name
extractedEntities.description: Programmatic identifier for an object type used in code, assignable after creation and distinct from the display name

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Groups
extractedEntities.description: Categorization mechanism for organizing object types within the Ontology, making it easier to filter and find relevant types

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application providing the guided helper interface for creating, configuring, and managing object types and their metadata

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform hosting datasets that serve as backing datasources for object types

extractedEntities.name: Guided Object Creation
extractedEntities.description: Step-by-step wizard pattern for creating object types: choose datasource, set metadata, create properties, configure keys, generate actions, save

keyInsights: Every object type requires at minimum a primary key (unique identifier) and a title key (human-readable display name)
A single Foundry datasource can only back one object type, enforcing a 1:1 mapping between backing data and object type definitions
Backing datasources cannot contain MapType or StructType columns, constraining the data shapes that can directly back object types
When a backing datasource is selected, its columns are automatically mapped to properties, but any can be discarded during the configuration step
Object types can be created without an existing datasource -- an empty dataset is generated at a chosen location for permissions management
The guided creation workflow follows a fixed sequence: datasource selection, metadata, properties, key configuration, action generation, save location, and commit

title: Object and link types - Object types - Metadata reference - Palantir
summary: Reference for object type metadata including display name, plural display name, description, ID, icon, color, groups, and status. Documents all configurable metadata fields that define how an object type appears and behaves across the platform.
relevantParts: Complete object type metadata field reference

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity represented by comprehensive metadata including ID, RID, icon, display names, description, groups, API name, visibility, status, index status, and writeback configuration

extractedEntities.name: Object Type ID

extractedEntities.name: Object Type RID

extractedEntities.name: API Name

extractedEntities.name: Visibility

extractedEntities.name: Index Status
