extractedEntities.name: Writeback Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Metadata indicating whether the object type has a writeback dataset and whether end-user edits are enabled or disabled

extractedEntities.name: Status
extractedEntities.description: Developmental state metadata: active, experimental, or deprecated, with experimental as the default for new object types

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Characteristics of an object type that form part of its full metadata specification

keyInsights: Object types have a dual identity system: a human-readable ID (e.g., 'employee') for configuration and an auto-generated RID for platform-level error messages and references
Visibility is a three-level system (prominent, normal, hidden) that controls discoverability in user applications, separate from the developmental status
New object types default to experimental status, requiring explicit promotion to active for production use
Writeback configuration is a distinct metadata field that controls whether end users can edit objects of a type, independent of the object type's datasource
API name is the programmatic identifier distinct from the display name, enabling code-stable references even when display names change

title: Object edits and materializations - How user edits are applied - Palantir
summary: When an Action is applied, edits are immediately applied to the index in object databases and periodically flushed to persistent storage as Foundry datasets. Transactionality guarantees avoid data correctness issues. Writeback datasets read edits and reflect the most up-to-date object state.
relevantParts: Action application flow: immediate index update, periodic flush
Writeback datasets as persistent edit storage
User edit lifecycle from Action to persistent dataset
Immediate index update with periodic dataset flush
Integration between Actions service and object databases

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology mechanism for applying data modifications to objects, links, or object sets, triggering immediate index updates and periodic persistent storage flushes

extractedEntities.name: Writeback
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism for persisting user edits from object databases into Foundry datasets, enabling the most up-to-date object state to be read

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity whose instances can be edited through Actions, with version control ensuring transactional consistency

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes of objects that can be modified through Action-based edits, subject to version control

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Service
extractedEntities.description: Service that receives modification instructions from Actions, stores them in an offset-tracked queue supporting simultaneous edits

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Object storage system that tracks Funnel offsets for object types and many-to-many link types, applying edits to live indexed data with read-after-write consistency

extractedEntities.name: Schema Migration Framework
extractedEntities.description: Object Storage V2 capability for migrating user edits, including a 'drop all edits' instruction to reset objects to their input datasource state

extractedEntities.name: Entity Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism ensuring transactional consistency when applying Actions by checking version consistency of both object type definitions and object instances

extractedEntities.name: Optimistic Concurrency
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where front-end consumers pass object version on Action submission, and the server applies the Action only if versions match, using compare-and-swap

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage without schema migration support, where removing writeback dataset configuration deletes all existing user edits

extractedEntities.name: Materialization
extractedEntities.description: Process of periodically flushing in-memory edits from the object database index to persistent Foundry datasets via the Funnel service

keyInsights: When an Action is applied, edits are immediately applied to the object database index and periodically flushed to persistent Foundry datasets via the Funnel service
Object Storage V2 guarantees read-after-write consistency: any object read occurring after a user modification will contain the user edits
There is no mechanism to undo a single user edit -- corrections require additional user edits or object recreation
Object Storage V2 provides a schema migration framework with a 'drop all edits' instruction for resetting to input datasource state, useful for transitioning from testing to production
Version control uses optimistic concurrency: the front-end passes the object version seen by the user, and the server uses compare-and-swap to ensure the Action applies to the correct version
Object Storage V1 (Phonograph) and V2 have different version control behaviors, with V2 providing more robust transactional guarantees

title: Ontologies - Overview - Palantir
summary: An ontology stores ontological resources including object types, link types, action types, and interfaces. Ontologies can be private (single organization) or shared (multiple organizations). Mapped 1:1 with a space. When a new space is created, a corresponding ontology is simultaneously created.
relevantParts: Ontology as container for all ontological resources

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Top-level artifact that stores ontological resources (object types, link types, action types, interfaces, shared properties, groups), mapped 1:1 with a space

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology resource representing a real-world entity, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource defining relationships between object types, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource defining data modification operations, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource for abstraction and polymorphism, contained within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource enabling property definitions to be reused across multiple object types within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Object Type Group
extractedEntities.description: Organizational unit for categorizing object types within an ontology

extractedEntities.name: Space
extractedEntities.description: Foundry organizational unit mapped 1:1 with an ontology; when a space is created, a corresponding ontology is simultaneously created with the same organization markings

extractedEntities.name: Organization Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism controlling which organizations can access ontological entities; private ontologies belong to one organization, shared ontologies span multiple

extractedEntities.name: Shared Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where an ontology is shared among multiple organizations, enabling safe cross-organization data and workflow sharing

keyInsights: An ontology is the top-level container for all Ontology resources: object types, link types, action types, interfaces, shared properties, and object type groups
Ontologies are mapped 1:1 with spaces -- creating a new space automatically creates a corresponding ontology with the same organization markings
Ontologies can be either private (single organization) or shared (multiple organizations), enabling controlled cross-organization collaboration
Shared ontologies inherit the markings of shared spaces, ensuring that only users of specified organizations can access ontological entities
Shared properties are an explicit Ontology resource type, enabling property definitions to be reused across multiple object types

title: Foundry Branching - Overview - Palantir

Provides unified experience for modifications across multiple applications on a single branch. Branches are intended to be short-lived and small to avoid conflicts.

Modifying large object types on a branch can incur significant additional compute and storage costs
Foundry Branching and release management are complementary: branching for development/testing isolation, release management for managing versions across environments
A review process with per-resource approval policies ensures changes are finalized and approved before merging into the Main branch

title: Why create an Ontology? - Palantir
summary: Explains the strategic value of building an Ontology: shared source of truth for decision-making, data discovery, viewing local decisions in global context. Goes beyond data cataloging to define a robust foundation for end-user workflows with rich metadata and granular security.

Shared source of truth across organization

keyInsights: The Ontology provides five key organizational benefits: connectivity at scale, interpretability, economies of scale, decision capture, and powering operational AI/ML
The Ontology goes beyond a data catalog by defining a shared language that abstracts away technical concepts like datasets and joins for non-technical users
The Ontology is bidirectional: used not only to read data but to write back and capture decisions, making it an active decision-making system
Operating from standard data lakes leads to unmanageable complexity over time, while the Ontology provides a well-defined system that scales with data growth
The Ontology enables cross-functional collaboration by providing a common language, eliminating lengthy reconciliation processes to confirm everyone sees the same data

title: Understanding Palantir's Ontology: Semantic, Kinetic, and Dynamic Layers Explained
summary: Third-party analysis breaking down the three Ontology layers. Semantic layer defines the conceptual model (entities, relationships, properties). Kinetic layer operationalizes the model with data feeds, action types, and functions. Dynamic layer introduces business rules, access control, and lifecycle management.
relevantParts: Clear delineation of each layer's responsibilities
Kinetic layer: actions and workflow execution
Dynamic layer: AI-driven decision-making on top of Ontology
Three-layer Ontology architecture model: semantic, kinetic, dynamic
Accessible framework for understanding Ontology concepts

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Core ontology layer defining the conceptual model: what entities exist, how they relate, and what properties they have -- answering 'what are the things that matter'

extractedEntities.name: Kinetic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Ontology layer connecting the conceptual model to actual data sources via ETL pipelines, enabling traceable and auditable data lineage from source to insight

extractedEntities.name: Dynamic Layer
extractedEntities.description: Ontology layer introducing behavior through business rules, access control, lifecycle management, and workflow enforcement

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Entities defined in the semantic layer representing real-world concepts like Person, Vehicle, Transaction, Organization

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attributes defined in the semantic layer such as name, timestamp, representing characteristics of entities

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships defined in the semantic layer connecting entities, e.g., Person owns Vehicle

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Part of the kinetic layer operationalizing the ontology model with data feeds and executable functions

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Part of the kinetic layer enabling complex logic and data orchestration around the ontology model

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform where engineers build the kinetic layer and end users interact through the dynamic layer

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: Palantir platform that also uses the ontology concept with semantic, kinetic, and dynamic layers

extractedEntities.name: Three-Layer Ontology Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern organizing the ontology into semantic (conceptual model), kinetic (data connection), and dynamic (behavior/rules) layers

keyInsights: The Palantir Ontology is organized into three distinct layers: semantic (what exists), kinetic (connecting to real data), and dynamic (business rules and behavior)
The semantic layer defines entities, relationships, and properties as a shared language that reconciles fragmented data concepts across sources
The kinetic layer maps raw data (SQL tables, CSVs, APIs) to ontological entities via ETL pipelines with traceable data lineage
The dynamic layer introduces active behavior: business rules, access control, and lifecycle management (e.g., Suspect to Investigated to Cleared)
Different roles interact with different layers: analysts use semantic, engineers build kinetic, end users interact through dynamic
The layered approach enables the ontology to be not just a passive model but an active, adaptive system

keyInsights: Ontologies are themselves a form of artificial intelligence, encapsulating human knowledge into structured, machine-processable formats that enable inference and pattern recognition
Foundational ontologies serve as reusable base patterns that can be extended for specific use cases, preventing each modeling project from starting from scratch
The ontology design process involves: problem definition, data collection, preparation/cleaning, analysis (clustering, association rules, decision trees), and interpretation of results
Knowledge graphs underlying ontologies bridge the gap between raw data and meaningful insights by encoding both entities and semantic relationships
Ontologies enable AI agents to understand context, preferences, and nuances by providing a scaffold of human knowledge that aligns with human reasoning
The foundational ontology pattern reduces bias in logical models by continuously validating them against data-derived relationships

title: Palantir's Ontology, Kimball's Star Schema, and Model-Driven Data Engineering: A Comparative View
summary: Comparative analysis of Palantir's Ontology approach against traditional data modeling paradigms like Kimball's star schema. Palantir's Ontology is described as a highly normalized, business-oriented abstraction layer that models data as business objects with relationships as first-class citizens.
relevantParts: Comparison with traditional data modeling approaches

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Highly normalized, business-oriented abstraction layer in Foundry that models data as business objects with relationships as first-class citizens

extractedEntities.name: Star Schema

extractedEntities.name: Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationships between business objects treated as first-class citizens in the Ontology, unlike foreign keys in traditional data modeling

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the Ontology as its central semantic layer for business-oriented data modeling

keyInsights: Palantir's Ontology is a highly normalized approach compared to Kimball's denormalized star schema, trading reporting performance for semantic richness
In the Ontology, relationships between business objects are first-class citizens rather than foreign keys, enabling richer graph-like data navigation
The Ontology centers on business objects (Customer, Account, Contract) while star schema centers on facts and dimensions, representing fundamentally different modeling philosophies
A model-driven data engineering approach can bridge the gap by combining the ontology's business semantics with dimensional modeling's pragmatic performance
The Ontology's normalized structure aligns data around business concepts rather than query patterns, making it more adaptable to changing analytical needs

keyInsights: The Ontology is conceptualized as the API of an organization — a shared layer between engineers, business users, and AIP agents composed of intuitive business concepts

title: Object and link types - Properties - Mandatory control properties - Palantir

Enables fine-grained mandatory controls on Ontology properties
Controls propagate with data through the system

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Control Property

extractedEntities.name: Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager

keyInsights: Mandatory control properties enforce access control at the property level within an Object Type, restricting access to all properties in the same datasource

Mandatory control properties require Object Storage V2 and are not available on V1 (Phonograph)
A marking-backed restricted view must be created before configuring mandatory control properties
Markings and organizations can be combined on the same property: user must satisfy ALL markings AND at least ONE organization
For multi-datasource Object Types, each datasource needs its own mandatory control property configuration

title: Object permissioning - Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) - Palantir
summary: Multi-datasource object types (MDOs) are backed by multiple datasources. Only column-wise MDOs are supported (not row-wise). A specific property must come from one datasource only, except the primary key which must exist in every datasource for joining. Only datasets or restricted views can be used.
relevantParts: Primary key as join key across datasources
Multiple datasources backing a single object type
Primary key as the join mechanism across datasources
Column-wise MDOs enable column-level access controls
Different property subsets from different datasources with different permissions
Supports use cases requiring segregated column access

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Datasource Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Property

extractedEntities.name: Column-Wise MDO

extractedEntities.name: Row-Wise MDO

extractedEntities.name: Column-Level Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern using column-wise MDOs to restrict property visibility based on datasource permissions

keyInsights: MDOs only support column-wise joins (not row-wise unions); row-wise use cases should use restricted views instead
Each property in an MDO must come from exactly one datasource, but the primary key must exist in every datasource for joining
If a user lacks Viewer permission on a backing datasource, properties from that datasource appear as null but the object type schema remains visible
MDOs require Object Storage V2 and support user edits and materializations
Only Foundry datasets or restricted views can serve as MDO backing datasources; streaming sources are not supported
Property multiplicity (overlapping columns from multiple datasources) is not supported in column-wise MDOs

title: Action types - Side effects - Overview - Palantir

keyInsights: Action type side effects are triggered after primary action rules execute, maintaining a clear separation between Ontology modifications and external integrations

The 'decision orchestration' pattern arises when an external system (not Foundry) is the source of truth, and side effects enable Foundry to integrate with and write to those systems

title: Polymorphic Interface with Unique Primary Keys - Best Practice? - Palantir Developer Community
summary: Community discussion about best practices for implementing polymorphic interfaces when object types have unique primary key requirements. Explores practical challenges of interface-based polymorphism in Ontology design.
relevantParts: Primary key uniqueness across interface implementations

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction enabling polymorphic modeling where multiple object types share common properties like task_id, status, and priority through interface implementation

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship mechanism used to connect objects via interface-level references through toInterfaceRid for polymorphic linking

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Unique identifier for object instances enforced locally per object type, not globally across the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Interface Polymorphism
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of using a shared interface (e.g., Task) across multiple object types to enable polymorphic references and links from other object types like SLA

extractedEntities.name: toInterfaceRid
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism for creating polymorphic references through interface RIDs, enabling a single link type (e.g., from SLA) to reference any implementing object type

keyInsights: Foundry enforces primary key uniqueness only locally per object type, not globally across implementing types, which creates a challenge for interface-level polymorphic references
The toInterfaceRid mechanism enables polymorphic linking from a single object type (e.g., SLA) to any object type implementing a shared interface (e.g., Task)
The community-reported challenge of global uniqueness across interface implementations suggests this is an unresolved design tension in Ontology's polymorphism model
Real-world polymorphic modeling (Incident, Case, Problem sharing a Task interface) requires careful primary key design to avoid collisions across implementing types

extractedEntities.description: An operation that computes summary statistics (count, min, max, sum, average) over properties of objects within an ObjectSet. Supports grouping by property values including range and exact grouping.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A typed attribute on an object type that holds data values. Properties are the target of aggregation functions and groupBy clauses.

extractedEntities.name: AggregateObjectSetRequestV2
extractedEntities.description: V2 API request body for aggregating objects. Contains an objectSet definition, a list of AggregationV2 functions, optional groupBy clauses, and an accuracy setting (REQUIRE_ACCURATE or ALLOW_APPROXIMATE).

extractedEntities.name: AggregateObjectsResponseV2
extractedEntities.description: V2 API response for object set aggregation. Returns data as a list of response items each containing metrics (name/value pairs) and group keys, plus accuracy and optional compute usage.

extractedEntities.name: AggregationV2
extractedEntities.description: Individual aggregation function specification in the V2 API. Specifies a field (property), a name for the result metric, and a type (min, max, avg, sum, count).

extractedEntities.name: AggregationGroupByV2
extractedEntities.description: Grouping specification for aggregations. Supports 'range' type with startValue/endValue and 'exact' type for property-based grouping of aggregation results.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth Scope api:ontologies-read
extractedEntities.description: OAuth2 operation scope required for third-party applications to read ontology data through the aggregate endpoint and other read operations.

extractedEntities.name: API V2
extractedEntities.description: Version 2 of the Palantir REST API providing enhanced ontology operations including aggregation, object set manipulation, and query capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The semantic type definition for objects in the Ontology, specifying name, properties, and behavior. Referenced in aggregation via the 'base' objectSet type.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Branch
extractedEntities.description: An experimental feature allowing objects to be aggregated from a specific branch of the Ontology rather than the default branch.

keyInsights: The V2 aggregation API supports five aggregation functions: count, min, max, sum, and average, applied to object properties within an ObjectSet.
Aggregation results can be grouped by property values using either 'range' grouping (with start/end values) or 'exact' grouping for discrete property values.
The API provides an accuracy toggle (REQUIRE_ACCURATE vs ALLOW_APPROXIMATE) allowing clients to trade precision for performance in large-scale aggregations.
Compute usage tracking is available as an opt-in feature for OSDK applications, measuring query cost in compute-seconds.
The aggregation endpoint supports experimental Ontology branches and transactions, allowing aggregation against non-default data states.
The objectSet definition in the request uses a union type, supporting multiple ways to define the set of objects to aggregate (e.g., 'base' type specifying an object type directly).

title: Query basics - API Reference - Palantir
summary: API reference for query fundamentals in the Ontology. A query type represents logic that can be run in the Ontology. Covers query execution, parameter passing, and result handling through the API gateway.

extractedEntities.name: Query Type
extractedEntities.description: A named logic definition in the Ontology that can be executed to compute results. Query types are created using Query Functions and represent reusable computational units.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A code-backed logic unit in the Ontology. Query Functions are a specific category used to create Query Types that can be invoked through the API.

extractedEntities.name: API Gateway
extractedEntities.description: The Palantir API gateway that exposes ontology operations including query execution, parameter passing, and result handling for external consumption.

extractedEntities.name: API V1
extractedEntities.description: Version 1 of the Palantir REST API. The query basics documentation is part of the V1 API reference.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The central semantic layer in Palantir Foundry that organizes data into typed objects, relationships, and executable logic including query types.

keyInsights: Query Types are the Ontology's mechanism for encapsulating executable logic that can be invoked through the API, separating computation definition from execution.
Query Types are created using Query Functions, establishing a link between the Functions framework and the Query API surface.
The Query API supports parameter passing and result handling, enabling parameterized computation over ontology data.

title: Ontologies - Usage - Ontology query compute - Palantir
summary: The Ontology is a data backend that maps file-based data to organization-centric objects and serves high-speed queries for exploration, analysis, operational editing, scenario analysis, and more. Data is stored in multi-modal storage backends that can be flexibly queried in a single request.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The semantic representation of an entity in the Ontology, defining its name and properties. Distinguished from the object set which contains the actual object instances.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: The collection of actual object instances corresponding to an object type. The size corresponds to the number of dataset rows plus objects created/deleted by actions.

extractedEntities.name: Query Type
extractedEntities.description: A category of operations executed against object sets in the Ontology, including filters, aggregations, Search Arounds, and writeback operations. Each requires compute to execute.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1 (Phonograph)
extractedEntities.description: First-generation object storage using distributed indices in a horizontally scalable cluster. Uses index traversal and pruning to evaluate up to 1000x fewer records. Measures compute via 'hits' (physical record evaluations).

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2 (OSv2)
extractedEntities.description: Enhanced object storage with an optimized indexing format for high-speed queries. Measures compute differently from V1 with its own cost model.

extractedEntities.name: Filter
extractedEntities.description: A query type that considers a full object set and applies filtering criteria to produce a smaller output set.

extractedEntities.name: Aggregation
extractedEntities.description: A query type that takes an input object set and runs an aggregating function (sum, avg, etc.) on a property for all objects in the set.

extractedEntities.name: Search Around
extractedEntities.description: A query type that takes an incoming object set and runs a secondary filter on another object set based on a property of the incoming set, traversing relationships.

extractedEntities.name: Compute-Seconds
extractedEntities.description: The unit of measurement for Ontology query compute usage. Includes a fixed minimum overhead plus additional scaling compute based on query complexity.

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for exploring and querying objects in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application-building framework for operational users that can execute Ontology queries.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that executes Ontology queries for data exploration and analysis.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data backend platform that maps file-based data to organization-centric objects and serves high-speed queries across multi-modal storage backends.

keyInsights: The Ontology distinguishes between Object Types (semantic definitions) and Object Sets (actual object instances), with object set size determined by dataset rows plus action-created/deleted objects.
Four core query types exist: Filters (reduce sets), Aggregations (compute statistics), Search Arounds (traverse relationships), and Writeback Operations (modify property values).
Object Storage V1 (Phonograph) uses distributed indices with a pruning mechanism that can evaluate up to 1000x fewer records than a full scan, measuring compute via 'hits'.
Object Storage V2 (OSv2) is an enhanced indexing format optimized by Palantir for high-speed queries with a different cost model than V1.
The Ontology stores data in multi-modal storage backends that can be flexibly queried in a single request, abstracting away the underlying storage complexity.
Query compute is measured in compute-seconds, consisting of a fixed minimum overhead plus scaling compute based on query complexity.
Seven Foundry applications consume Ontology queries: Object Explorer, Workshop, Quiver, Slate, Vertex, Foundry Rules, Foundry Machinery, and Object APIs (OPIs).

title: Time series - Sensor object types use case - Create sensor object types with Ontology Manager - Palantir
summary: Guide for creating sensor object types in the Ontology, a pattern where sensor objects hold time series data linked to parent/root objects. Provides flexibility for per-object time series data. Covers two approaches: direct time series properties (TSPs) on objects vs linked sensor object types.
relevantParts: Sensor object type pattern for time series data
Two time series approaches: direct TSP vs linked sensors

extractedEntities.name: Sensor Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A specialized object type pattern for representing sensors that hold time series data. Sensor objects are linked to parent/root objects (e.g., Flight Sensor linked to Flight) providing per-object time series data flexibility.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A typed entity definition in the Ontology. In the sensor pattern, there is a root object type (e.g., Flight) and a sensor object type (e.g., Flight Sensor) linked together.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: A typed relationship between two object types with defined cardinality. In the sensor pattern, a many-to-one link connects sensor objects to their root object using a foreign key.

extractedEntities.name: Time Series Property
extractedEntities.description: A special property type on an object that references time series data. Can be set as the default time series property for automatic display in Quiver. Configured via the Capabilities tab in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: An attribute on an object type. In the sensor pattern, properties include a primary key (unique_sensor_flight_id), a title property, and a foreign key (flight_id) for linking.

extractedEntities.name: Sensor Object Pattern
extractedEntities.description: An architectural pattern where sensor objects hold time series data and are linked to parent/root objects via many-to-one relationships. Provides flexibility for per-object time series data versus direct time series properties on root objects.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for creating and configuring object types, properties, link types, and capabilities including time series properties.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for building data pipelines including time series syncs that feed data into sensor object type time series properties.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that automatically displays default time series properties configured on object types.

extractedEntities.name: IoT Sensor Monitoring
extractedEntities.description: Use case where sensor objects (e.g., flight sensors) hold time series readings and are linked to physical entities (e.g., flights) for monitoring and analysis.

keyInsights: The Sensor Object Pattern separates time series data into dedicated sensor objects linked to root objects, providing per-object granularity versus attaching time series directly to root objects.
Link types between sensor and root objects use many-to-one cardinality with foreign key mapping (e.g., flight_id on sensor maps to primary key on flight).
Time series properties are configured via the Capabilities tab in Ontology Manager and require a time series sync from Pipeline Builder as the data source.
A default time series property can be set on a sensor object type so it automatically appears in Quiver for exploration.
The sensor object type toggle in the time series configuration section enables special sensor-related behavior and link configuration.
Primary keys, title properties, and foreign keys are all configured during object type creation, establishing the structural foundation for linking.

title: Object and link types - Value types - Value type versions - Palantir
summary: Value types support versioning to manage evolution of semantic type definitions over time. Versions allow updating constraints, metadata, and behavior while maintaining backward compatibility with existing property definitions.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type
extractedEntities.description: A semantic type definition in the Ontology that adds meaning and constraints on top of base types. Value types are versioned to handle breaking and non-breaking edits to their definitions.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Version
extractedEntities.description: A specific version of a value type consisting of metadata (name, description, apiName) and constraints (validation rules). New versions are created when constraints change.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Metadata
extractedEntities.description: The mutable portion of a value type version including name, description, and apiName. Can be changed at any time without creating a new version.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Constraints
extractedEntities.description: The immutable validation rules of a value type version. Updating constraints creates a new version. The base type metadata is also immutable once set.

extractedEntities.name: Value Type Versioning
extractedEntities.description: A versioning pattern for value types where metadata is mutable but constraints are immutable. Breaking changes to constraints should trigger deprecation and creation of a new value type rather than a new version.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: An attribute on an object type that can be typed with a value type. Properties are consumers of value types and receive automatic updates when non-breaking version changes propagate.

keyInsights: Value types have two distinct mutable surfaces: metadata (name, description, apiName) which can change freely, and constraints (validation rules) which are immutable per version.
Non-breaking constraint changes create a new version that automatically propagates to all consumers across the Ontology.

Breaking constraint changes on value types with existing consumers should be handled by deprecating the current value type and creating an entirely new one to avoid runtime errors and data inconsistencies.
The base type metadata (the underlying primitive type) is immutable once set, ensuring type safety across versions.
Value types without consumers can have their constraints changed freely, as there are no downstream dependencies to break.

title: Ontologies - Shared ontologies - Palantir
summary: A shared ontology enables resources from multiple organizations to be used in a workflow, whereas a private ontology contains resources from a single organization. Shared ontologies allow safe cross-organization collaboration on data and workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A special kind of ontology that allows multiple organizations to collaborate on shared workflows. Lives under a dedicated shared space and enables resources from multiple organizations to be used together.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A private ontology belonging to a single organization containing resources from that organization alone. Contrasted with shared ontologies which span multiple organizations.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Space
extractedEntities.description: A dedicated collaboration area created for cross-organization workflows. A shared ontology is automatically created when a new shared space is created.

extractedEntities.name: Organization Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security markings associated with organizations that are applied to shared ontologies. The shared ontology holds the same organization markings as its shared space.

extractedEntities.name: Role Grants
extractedEntities.description: Permission grants applied to shared spaces and their associated shared ontologies, controlling what actions different roles can perform.

extractedEntities.name: Cross-Organization Collaboration
extractedEntities.description: An architectural pattern enabling multiple organizations to securely collaborate on shared workflows using shared ontologies and shared spaces with proper security controls.

keyInsights: Shared ontologies are automatically created when a shared space is created, inheriting the shared space's organization markings and role grants.
A private ontology contains resources from a single organization, while a shared ontology enables cross-organization resource usage within workflows.
Security for shared ontologies is derived from the shared space, ensuring consistent access controls across the collaborative environment.
The shared ontology pattern enables multi-organization collaboration while maintaining security boundaries through markings and role grants.

title: Palantir Foundry 101 - Unit8
summary: Third-party introductory guide to Palantir Foundry covering the platform's core components including the Ontology. Provides an outside perspective on how Foundry's data integration, transformation, and Ontology layers work together for enterprise use cases.
relevantParts: Third-party introduction to Foundry and Ontology

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's operational platform consisting of a data layer and an operational layer. Encapsulates data processing, analytics, BI, and ML needs for technical and non-technical users.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's most common data abstraction. Datasets have a unique creation path (immutable provenance) ensuring traceable data lineage. Created by uploading files, importing from external systems, or transforming existing datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's semantic layer enabling business users to make data-driven decisions. Maps technical datasets to business-meaningful objects and relationships.

extractedEntities.name: Contour
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's interactive data analysis tool similar to Jupyter Notebooks. Allows quick data exploration with board-level cells, branching analysis, and output dataset creation.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data application creator for building operational applications. Referenced as one of the key services in the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry service for tracking the history and provenance of data through transformation pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's production-grade data transformation service using PySpark or Spark SQL code. Backed by Git repositories, accepting inputs and writing to a single output.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Immutable Provenance
extractedEntities.description: A constraint where each dataset has a unique creation path that cannot be modified using different upstream transformations. Ensures complete traceability of how any dataset was created.

extractedEntities.name: Two-Layer Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's architectural pattern consisting of a data layer (ingestion, transformation, storage) and an operational layer (applications, ontology, decision-making).

keyInsights: Foundry is structured as two distinct layers: a data layer (ingestion, transformation, storage) and an operational layer (applications, ontology, decision-making).
Datasets in Foundry have immutable provenance — each dataset has a unique creation path that cannot be modified with different upstream transformations, ensuring full traceability.
The Ontology serves as the bridge between technical data (datasets) and business-meaningful concepts, enabling non-technical users to make data-driven decisions.
Foundry uses Spark as its runtime engine for data transformations, accessible through Code Repositories (production-grade PySpark/SQL) and Contour (interactive analysis).
Contour provides Jupyter Notebook-like interactive data exploration with board-level cells, branching, and the ability to save analysis outputs as new datasets.
All Foundry services share datasets as a common language, with each service speaking to the same underlying data abstractions.

title: Geospatial and geotemporal - Use geospatial data in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Guide for integrating geospatial data into the Ontology using geopoint and geoshape property types. Supports point, line, and polygon geometries. Objects can have georectified images attached with bounds. Integrates with Map application for visualization.

extractedEntities.name: Geopoint Property
extractedEntities.description: A property type for point geometries specified as latitude/longitude strings (WGS 84 CRS) or Geohash strings. Objects with geopoint properties are indexed for geospatial search.

extractedEntities.name: Geoshape Property
extractedEntities.description: A property type for polygon and line geometries specified as GeoJSON Geometry strings. Supports LineString, Polygon, MultiLineString, MultiPolygon, MultiPoint, and Point types. Must comply with RFC 7946 and WGS 84 coordinates.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A typed attribute on an object type. Geospatial property types (geopoint, geoshape) are special property types that enable spatial indexing and search.

extractedEntities.name: Map
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application providing immersive geospatial analysis and investigation capabilities including time series overlay, link analysis, and simulations.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application-building framework that includes a robust map widget for geospatial visualization of ontology objects.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool that has superseded the geospatial-tools library for loading, transforming, and wielding geospatial data.

extractedEntities.name: GeoJSON Geometry
extractedEntities.description: The wire format for geoshape properties. Must be a valid GeoJSON Geometry (not Feature/FeatureCollection/GeometryCollection), use WGS 84 coordinates, and follow RFC 7946 including right-hand winding order for polygons.

extractedEntities.name: Geospatial Search Index
extractedEntities.description: Automatic indexing applied to objects with geopoint or geoshape properties enabling spatial queries and filtering.

extractedEntities.name: Geospatial Analysis
extractedEntities.description: Use case for visualizing, exploring, and analyzing geospatial data in the Ontology using Map, Workshop, Slate, Quiver, Object Explorer, and Contour applications.

keyInsights: The Ontology supports two geospatial property types: geopoint (for points as lat/lon or Geohash) and geoshape (for lines and polygons as GeoJSON).
Objects with geopoint or geoshape properties are automatically indexed for geospatial search, enabling spatial queries without additional configuration.
Geoshape properties must contain valid GeoJSON Geometry strings compliant with RFC 7946, using WGS 84 coordinates and proper winding order for polygons.
Pipeline Builder has superseded the legacy geospatial-tools library as the recommended approach for loading and transforming geospatial data.
Six Foundry applications support geospatial capabilities: Map (primary), Workshop, Slate, Quiver, Object Explorer, and Contour.
Geopoint supports two input formats: direct latitude/longitude coordinates and Geohash strings (which are converted to points using the bottom-left corner of the Geohash rectangle).

title: Media sets (unstructured data) - Using media in the Ontology - Palantir
summary: Media reference object properties allow efficient display of media in Ontology-based applications. Provides optimizations like faster previews in Workshop/Object Explorer and tiling for geospatial imagery in Map. Bridges unstructured media data with structured Ontology objects.
relevantParts: Media reference properties for unstructured data
Integration of unstructured media with Ontology objects

extractedEntities.name: Media Reference Property
extractedEntities.description: A special property type on object types that references media items in a media set. Enables efficient display of media in Ontology-based applications with optimizations like faster previews and geospatial tiling.

extractedEntities.name: Media Set
extractedEntities.description: A collection of unstructured media files (images, documents, audio) that can be referenced by ontology objects through media reference properties.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A typed attribute on an object type. Media reference is a specialized property type that bridges unstructured media with structured ontology objects.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A typed entity definition that can have media reference properties in its Capabilities tab to associate unstructured media data.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that provides optimized interactive previews for media reference properties on objects.

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that provides optimized previews for media reference properties.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology SDK that provides media capabilities for building applications with Foundry as the backend.

extractedEntities.name: Media Operations
extractedEntities.description: Operations available on media reference properties via Functions on Objects, including: reading raw media items, OCR on documents, text extraction, audio transcription, and reading metadata.

extractedEntities.name: Unstructured Data Ontologization
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of bridging unstructured media data (images, documents, audio) with structured Ontology objects through media reference properties, enabling search, analysis, and application integration.

keyInsights: Media reference properties bridge unstructured media data with structured Ontology objects, enabling media display in Ontology-based applications.
Media reference properties support programmatic operations via Functions on Objects: raw reading, OCR, text extraction, audio transcription, and metadata reading.
Media files uploaded in action forms are only committed to the backing media set upon successful form submission, preventing orphaned files from failed submissions.
Media reference lists are not supported as a property type — only single media references per property.
Multiple media sets backing a single media reference property are strongly discouraged as media uploads in actions are not fully supported in that configuration.
The OSDK exposes media capabilities for building external applications that leverage media stored in Foundry.

title: Ontology SDK - OSDK references - Unsupported types for OSDK - Palantir
summary: Documents which Ontology types are not yet supported by the OSDK, providing insight into the type system boundaries. Helps understand which Ontology primitives can and cannot be accessed through generated SDK code.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology SDK that generates client-side code for TypeScript, Python, and Java packages representing object types, action types, and functions from the Ontology. Has type system limitations across language targets.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A typed entity definition in the Ontology. OSDK generates code for object types but skips unsupported property types during code generation.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: A typed action definition in the Ontology. OSDK cannot create packages if action types use unsupported parameter types.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A code-backed logic unit in the Ontology. OSDK fails to generate packages if functions use unsupported input or output types.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Unsupported Property Types
extractedEntities.description: Property types not supported by OSDK code generation. TypeScript: Cipher, Marking, Vectors. Python adds: Media, Struct. Java adds: TimeSeries.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Unsupported Parameter Types
extractedEntities.description: Action parameter types not supported. Python: ObjectSet, Marking, MarkingList. Java: Struct, Interface.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Unsupported Function Types
extractedEntities.description: Function input/output types not supported across SDKs. Common unsupported types include: Principal, User, Notification, OntologyEdit, ClassificationMarking, GeoShape, TimeSeries, TwoDimensionalAggregation.

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: An encrypted property type in the Ontology that is unsupported by all OSDK language targets (TypeScript, Python, Java).

extractedEntities.name: Marking
extractedEntities.description: A security classification property type in the Ontology that is unsupported by TypeScript and Python OSDK targets.

extractedEntities.name: Struct
extractedEntities.description: A complex structured property type in the Ontology. Unsupported in Python and Java OSDK property generation, though supported in TypeScript.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology concept for polymorphic type abstractions. Unsupported as an action parameter type in the Java OSDK.

keyInsights: OSDK code generation handles unsupported types differently: unsupported properties are silently skipped with a log error, while unsupported action/function types cause complete package generation failure.
Cipher is universally unsupported across all three OSDK language targets (TypeScript, Python, Java), reflecting the security sensitivity of encrypted properties.
TypeScript OSDK has the broadest type support, with only Cipher, Marking, and Vectors unsupported for properties. Python and Java have significantly more limitations.
The Python OSDK has the most unsupported function types, lacking support for ObjectSet, GeoShape, TimeSeries, TwoDimensionalAggregation, and many others as both inputs and outputs.
Action types with OAuth 2.0 webhook authentication are unsupported across all SDKs because users cannot authorize outbound applications through the SDK without first going through Foundry.
The type system gaps reveal the Ontology's internal richness — types like OntologyEdit, Principal, ModelGraph, and MandatoryControl exist internally but are not yet exposed through generated SDKs.

title: Pipeline Builder - Overview - Palantir
summary: Documentation for Pipeline Builder, which leverages Spark and Flink with a next-generation transformation backend acting as intermediary between logic creation and execution. Supports expressions (column-level) and transforms (table-level), with user-defined functions (UDFs) for custom code. Designed to output datasets, objects, streams, time-series, and exports.
relevantParts: Pipeline Builder architecture using Spark and Flink compute engines
Supports batch, incremental, and streaming pipeline types
Compute-agnostic Build framework with integrated security and data lineage
Spark and Flink-based data transformation architecture
Intermediary backend between logic and execution
Multiple output types: datasets, objects, streams, time-series

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's primary application for data integration, enabling point-and-click pipeline building with a next-generation transformation backend acting as intermediary between logic creation and execution.

extractedEntities.name: Expressions
extractedEntities.description: Column-level transformations in Pipeline Builder that operate on individual values within a dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Transforms
extractedEntities.description: Table-level operations in Pipeline Builder that modify dataset structure (joins, filters, aggregations, unions).

extractedEntities.name: User-Defined Functions (UDFs)
extractedEntities.description: Custom code functions in Pipeline Builder for computations that built-in expressions and transforms do not cover.

extractedEntities.name: Apache Spark
extractedEntities.description: Distributed compute engine leveraged by Pipeline Builder for batch data transformation processing.

extractedEntities.name: Apache Flink
extractedEntities.description: Stream processing engine leveraged by Pipeline Builder for real-time streaming pipeline execution.

extractedEntities.name: Transformation Backend
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder's next-generation backend that acts as a middle layer between logic creation and execution, writing transform code, performing integrity checks, and identifying refactoring errors.

extractedEntities.name: Compute-Agnostic Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder abstracts the execution engine: the same pipeline logic can execute on Spark, Flink, or other engines without user modification.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entities that Pipeline Builder can output to, feeding the Ontology from transformed data.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Outputs
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder can output to datasets, objects, streams, time-series, and exports — connecting raw data to the Ontology and beyond.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipelines
extractedEntities.description: Real-time latency pipeline execution capability in Pipeline Builder, enabling continuous data processing.

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder's transformation backend acts as an intelligent intermediary: it writes code, checks integrity, and identifies errors before execution — not just a visual wrapper.
The compute-agnostic architecture means Pipeline Builder logic is portable across Spark, Flink, and other engines without rewriting.
Pipeline Builder bridges the gap between no-code users and code-first engineers through a layered approach: point-and-click UI, expressions, transforms, and UDFs.
Outputs include objects (feeding the Ontology), streams, time-series, and exports — making Pipeline Builder a gateway from raw data to semantic Ontology.
Type-safe functions flag errors immediately rather than at build time, reducing the feedback loop for pipeline authors.
Automatic build path pruning avoids unnecessary computation by trimming disconnected transform paths.

title: Overview - Data Integration - Palantir
summary: Foundry provides a highly configurable set of data connectivity and integration tools that extend far beyond typical ETL/ELT solutions. The extensible data connection framework establishes connections with all types of source systems (structured, unstructured, semi-structured) using batch, micro-batch, or streaming transfer approaches. Designed to reduce the cost of data integration over time.
relevantParts: Comprehensive overview of Foundry's data integration architecture
Extensible framework for all source system types
Pipeline Builder as the primary data integration application

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's highly configurable data connectivity and integration toolset extending far beyond typical ETL/ELT solutions. Designed to reduce the cost of data integration over time through capabilities that act as a force multiplier.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for fast, flexible, and scalable delivery of data pipelines with integrated robustness and security.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection Framework
extractedEntities.description: Extensible framework establishing connections with all types of source systems (structured, unstructured, semi-structured) using batch, micro-batch, or streaming approaches.

extractedEntities.name: Compute-Agnostic Build Framework
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's Build framework provides fully integrated security and data lineage while enabling mixing and matching of third-party compute runtimes.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Full lineage tracking of data versions throughout the integration pipeline, from source through transformation to Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Health Checks
extractedEntities.description: Rigorous release process for production pipelines including health checks guaranteeing only fully compliant data is deployed.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The end destination for integrated data: raw data is connected, transformed, and ultimately mapped into Object Types in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Security
extractedEntities.description: Granular security for collaborative management of data extraction, with branching of data sync configurations and integrated governance.

keyInsights: Foundry data integration is explicitly designed to reduce cost over time — a force multiplier philosophy rather than one-time ETL.
The compute-agnostic Build framework is a foundational architectural pattern: security and lineage are integrated regardless of which compute engine runs.
The data integration layer spans four capabilities: connecting, transforming, managing pipelines, and quality assurance — each with dedicated tooling.
Pipeline health checks enforce that only compliant data reaches production, providing a quality gate before Ontology ingestion.
Data lineage from source to Ontology is a first-class capability, enabling full auditability of how raw data becomes semantic objects.

title: Data Connection - Overview - Palantir
summary: Data Connection is the application used to synchronize data from external systems into Foundry. It supports batch, streaming, media, and CDC syncs, virtual tables, and manages associated resources including source credentials. Designed to make it simple to explore data sources and sync new data for use case development.
relevantParts: Central hub for all external data synchronization
Supports batch, streaming, media, CDC sync modes
Virtual tables for querying without data movement

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Palantir application for synchronizing data from external systems into Foundry. Supports batch, streaming, media, CDC syncs, virtual tables, and manages source credentials.

extractedEntities.name: Raw Data Ingestion
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection philosophy that data should be ingested as-is from its most raw source with no external preprocessing, making the Foundry pipeline the single source of all transformations.

extractedEntities.name: Batch Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for periodic bulk data transfer from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for continuous real-time data ingestion from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: CDC Sync
extractedEntities.description: Change Data Capture sync mode that captures only incremental changes from source systems.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Tables
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection capability enabling query-time access to external data without copying it into Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Media Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection sync mode for ingesting unstructured media files (images, video, PDFs) from external systems.

extractedEntities.name: Webhook Writeback
extractedEntities.description: Outbound Data Connection capability enabling writeback to external systems via webhooks.

extractedEntities.name: Robustness Through Simplicity
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection uses simple functions (filesystem, database syncs) to pull data in small batches with low-complexity queries, combined with automatic retries and health monitoring.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The downstream destination for data synchronized through Data Connection, after transformation in the pipeline layer.

keyInsights: Data Connection deliberately ingests data as-is with no preprocessing, making Foundry the single source of truth for all transformations.
Five sync modes (batch, streaming, media, CDC, virtual tables) cover the full spectrum of data ingestion patterns.
Virtual Tables enable query-time federation — accessing external data without copying it into Foundry, useful for compliance or latency requirements.
Robustness is achieved through simplicity: small batches, low-complexity queries, automatic retries, and health monitoring rather than complex resilience patterns.
Data Connection also supports outbound connections via webhooks and data exports, making it bidirectional not just ingest-only.
Extensibility is a core design principle: new source systems can often reuse existing plugins with minor changes.

title: On dataset versioning in Palantir Foundry - Palantir Blog
summary: Palantir blog post explaining dataset versioning in Foundry. Datasets use immutable versions (like Git commits) enabling concurrent access. Four transaction types exist: SNAPSHOT, APPEND, UPDATE, and DELETE. The system combines dataset branching with Git branching to support versioning over both logic and data simultaneously.
relevantParts: Immutable dataset versions with Git-like semantics
Four transaction types: SNAPSHOT, APPEND, UPDATE, DELETE
Concurrent access safety through dataset branching

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The fundamental data representation in Foundry. Datasets use immutable versions (analogous to Git commits) enabling concurrent read/write access without conflicts.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that replaces the current view entirely with a new set of files. The basis of batch pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that adds new files without modifying existing ones. The basis of incremental pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: UPDATE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that modifies specific existing files within the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: DELETE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type that removes specific files from the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: Git-for-Data
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's dataset versioning model parallels Git: transactions are analogous to commits, dataset branches parallel Git branches, enabling versioning over both logic and data simultaneously.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Branching
extractedEntities.description: Datasets support branching that isolates changes, enabling concurrent development on different versions of the same dataset without interference.

extractedEntities.name: Immutable Versions
extractedEntities.description: Each committed dataset transaction creates an immutable version, ensuring atomic reads and preventing mixed-version inconsistencies.

extractedEntities.name: Dual Branching
extractedEntities.description: Foundry combines dataset branching (data versioning) with Git branching (logic versioning) to support versioning over both data and transformation code simultaneously.

keyInsights: Dataset versioning through immutable transactions solves the fundamental problem of concurrent read/write access in collaborative data engineering.
Four transaction types (SNAPSHOT, APPEND, UPDATE, DELETE) provide precise control over how datasets evolve, analogous to different Git operations.
Dataset branching enables sandbox development: data engineers can experiment on isolated branches without affecting production data or other engineers' work.
Dual branching (data + logic) is Foundry's unique capability: versioning both the transformation code and the data it produces simultaneously.
Without versioning, exported data suffers from non-deterministic mixed versions during concurrent access — a subtle but critical correctness issue.
APPEND transactions are the foundation of incremental pipelines, processing only new data rather than recomputing entire datasets.

title: Core Concepts - Datasets - Palantir
summary: Defines the dataset as the most essential representation of data in Foundry. A dataset is fundamentally a wrapper around a collection of files stored in a backing file system. Datasets are updated over time using transactions which represent modifications to files within a dataset, with each version being immutable.
relevantParts: Foundational definition of datasets in Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The most essential representation of data in Foundry, from when it lands through when it is mapped into the Ontology. A wrapper around a collection of files in a backing file system with integrated permission, schema, and version control.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset View
extractedEntities.description: The current state of a dataset as seen by users — the result of all committed transactions up to the latest version.

extractedEntities.name: Transaction Lifecycle
extractedEntities.description: A dataset transaction follows a lifecycle: OPEN (files can be written), COMMITTED (changes visible in view), or ABORTED (changes ignored).

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Replaces the entire current dataset view with a new set of files. The basis of batch pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Adds new files to the current view without modifying existing files. Cannot overwrite existing files. The basis of incremental pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: UPDATE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Modifies specific existing files within the dataset. Supports row-level operations.

extractedEntities.name: DELETE Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Removes specific files from the dataset view.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Schema
extractedEntities.description: Metadata describing the columns of structured (tabular) datasets, stored alongside the dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology entity that datasets are ultimately mapped into, with datasets providing the backing data for Object Type properties.

extractedEntities.name: Git-for-Data
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transactions are analogous to Git commits — atomic, immutable changes that together form the version history of a dataset.

keyInsights: Datasets are the fundamental abstraction in Foundry — everything from landing to Ontology mapping flows through datasets.
A dataset is fundamentally a wrapper around files, not a database table — this enables support for structured, unstructured, and semi-structured data.
The transaction model (OPEN, COMMITTED, ABORTED) provides atomicity guarantees: either all changes apply or none do.
Four transaction types map to distinct pipeline patterns: SNAPSHOT for batch, APPEND for incremental, UPDATE for row-level modifications, DELETE for removals.
Datasets bridge the gap from raw data to Ontology: data lands as files, gets structured through schemas, and is ultimately mapped into Object Types.
Foundry datasets provide integrated permission management, schema management, and version control — capabilities absent from raw file systems.

title: Data Lineage - Overview - Palantir
summary: Data Lineage is an interactive tool providing a holistic view of how data flows through the Foundry platform. It presents the graph of datasets and transforms to show inputs and outputs, enables managing builds and schedules, and tracks downstream usage including Workshop Apps and Ontology Objects.
relevantParts: Visual graph of dataset and transform relationships
Build management and scheduling from lineage view
Tracks downstream Ontology and application usage
Tracks data flow and transformation history across the platform
Pipeline mode enforces strict provenance control via build system
Supports security through marking propagation along lineage

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Interactive tool in Foundry that provides a holistic view of how data flows through the platform, showing pipeline graphs of datasets and transforms with their inputs, outputs, and downstream usage.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A foundational data resource in Foundry that stores tabular or file-based data, serves as input/output for transforms, and can back Object Types in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: A data processing step in Foundry that takes one or more datasets as input and produces output datasets, forming the nodes and edges of the pipeline graph.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform that provides data integration, transformation, Ontology building, and operational capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Snapshot
extractedEntities.description: A shareable view of a pipeline's state captured at a point in time within Data Lineage, used for collaboration between teammates.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application building tool that consumes Ontology objects and is tracked as downstream usage in Data Lineage.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept representing a category of real-world entities, backed by datasets and tracked as downstream consumers in Data Lineage.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Graph
extractedEntities.description: The directed acyclic graph of datasets and transforms that represents data flow through the Foundry platform, visualized and managed through Data Lineage.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Coloring
extractedEntities.description: A visualization feature in Data Lineage that colors datasets based on attributes like out-of-date status to aid pipeline monitoring.

keyInsights: Data Lineage provides visibility into the full data flow graph, tracking both upstream ancestors and downstream descendants of any dataset.
Downstream tracking extends beyond data transforms to include Workshop Apps and Ontology Objects, bridging the gap between raw data pipelines and operational applications.
Pipeline snapshots enable collaborative pipeline management by allowing users to share specific views of the pipeline state with teammates.
Data Lineage supports drill-down from pipeline-level views to dataset-level details including schema, last build time, and generating code.

title: Data Protection and Governance - Palantir
summary: Covers data governance as the management of enterprise data across the entire lifecycle from ingestion to access to deletion. Data protection and governance are central to Foundry, with markings implementing mandatory access controls and Sensitive Data Scanner detecting sensitive information automatically.
relevantParts: Data governance lifecycle from ingestion to deletion
Markings as mandatory access controls on data
Sensitive Data Scanner for automated detection
Lifecycle approach to data governance from ingestion to deletion
Integration of PBAC, markings, organizations, and Cipher
Tools for managing sensitive/personal data compliance

extractedEntities.name: Data Protection
extractedEntities.description: The range of technical and organizational means in Foundry to ensure processing of personal data is limited to what is necessary and proportional to achieve a legitimate processing outcome.

extractedEntities.name: Data Governance
extractedEntities.description: The management of enterprise data across the entire lifecycle of processing in Foundry, from ingestion to access to deletion.

extractedEntities.name: Fair Information Practice Principles
extractedEntities.description: Foundational privacy principles (FIPPs) that guide personal data handling in Foundry, including collection limitation, data quality, purpose specification, use limitation, and accountability.

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's implementation of mandatory access controls that define eligibility criteria restricting visibility and actions to users who meet those criteria.

extractedEntities.name: Sensitive Data Scanner
extractedEntities.description: An automated tool in Foundry that detects sensitive information within datasets to support data protection and governance compliance.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform providing data integration, transformation, analysis, and operationalization with security and governance at its core.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lifecycle Management
extractedEntities.description: The end-to-end management of data from ingestion through access to deletion, ensuring compliant processing at every stage.

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism that restricts data use to specified legitimate purposes, aligning with the FIPPs Purpose Specification Principle.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: An attribute of an Object Type that may contain sensitive or personal data subject to data protection and governance controls.

keyInsights: Data protection and data governance are central, first-class concepts in Foundry, not afterthoughts layered on top of the platform.

Foundry implements the Fair Information Practice Principles (FIPPs) as foundational guidelines for handling personal data, covering collection limitation, data quality, purpose specification, use limitation, and accountability.
Data governance spans the entire data lifecycle from ingestion to access to deletion, providing systematic means of understanding what data an organization has.
The distinction between data protection (technical and organizational means) and data governance (lifecycle management) reflects a dual approach to regulatory compliance.
Sensitive Data Scanner provides automated detection of sensitive information, reducing the burden of manual data classification.

title: Platform Overview - Architecture - Palantir
summary: Official documentation covering Palantir's core architecture consisting of three platforms: AIP, Foundry, and Apollo, functioning as an enterprise operating system. The Ontology is the central software architecture representing enterprise decisions, not just data. Includes Kubernetes-based build system, zero-copy data lake access, and comprehensive security with role-, classification-, and purpose-based access controls.
relevantParts: Defines the three-platform architecture (AIP, Foundry, Apollo)
Explains Ontology as the central architectural abstraction
Covers microservices architecture of the Ontology backend

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform that connects AI with enterprise data and operations, forming one of three core platforms alongside Foundry and Apollo.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform for data integration, transformation, Ontology building, and operational capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's mission control platform for autonomous software deployment, managing the delivery and operation of the other platforms.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The central software architecture of Palantir's platform that represents enterprise decisions, combining data, logic, and actions rather than being merely a data model.

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Operating System
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's architectural pattern where AIP, Foundry, and Apollo together function as an operating system for enterprise operations, decisions, and AI.

extractedEntities.name: Role-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Discretionary access control mechanism based on roles assigned to users for specific resources.

extractedEntities.name: Classification-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control mechanism based on data classifications (markings) that restrict visibility regardless of role.

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism that restricts data use based on specified legitimate purposes.
