keyInsights: Palantir's architecture consists of three core platforms — AIP, Foundry, and Apollo — that together function as an enterprise operating system.
The Ontology is described as representing enterprise decisions, not just data, making it a decision-centric architecture rather than a traditional data model.
Security is implemented through three complementary access control mechanisms: role-based (discretionary), classification-based (mandatory markings), and purpose-based.
Apollo provides autonomous software deployment capabilities, managing how the other platforms are delivered and operated.
The platform uses Kubernetes-based build systems and supports zero-copy data lake access for performance.

title: Data Connection - Architecture - Palantir
summary: Architecture documentation for Foundry's Data Connection system, covering three connection patterns: direct connections for publicly accessible systems, agent proxy connections for private networks (recommended), and agent worker connections (legacy). Agents use unidirectional outbound HTTPS connections.
relevantParts: Three architecture types for data connection
Agent proxy for private network connectivity
Agent proxy vs agent worker architecture

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data connectivity system that provides multiple architecture patterns for connecting to external data systems, including direct connections, agent proxies, and agent workers.

extractedEntities.name: Direct Connection
extractedEntities.description: A Data Connection architecture where a Foundry worker container directly communicates with publicly accessible external systems via encrypted network requests with egress policies.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Proxy Connection
extractedEntities.description: The recommended Data Connection architecture for private networks where an agent acts as a network tunnel via websocket, while a Foundry worker handles all computation and data processing.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker Connection
extractedEntities.description: A legacy Data Connection architecture where the agent itself executes tasks and sends results to Foundry over unidirectional outbound HTTPS connections.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker
extractedEntities.description: An isolated compute container in Foundry that handles authenticated encrypted network requests for data connections, with scalable compute resources.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection Agent
extractedEntities.description: A component installed on private networks that establishes unidirectional outbound HTTPS connections to Foundry, enabling connectivity to systems not publicly accessible.

extractedEntities.name: AES-256-GCM Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Server-side encryption standard used for storing external system credentials in Foundry's Data Connection, ensuring credentials can only be decrypted by authorized containers.

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Platform-managed network egress rules that control which external systems a Foundry worker or agent proxy can communicate with.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform providing the infrastructure for data connection architectures.

keyInsights: Data Connection provides three distinct architecture patterns: direct connection for public systems, agent proxy (recommended) for private networks, and agent worker (legacy) for historical use cases.
Agent proxy is the recommended pattern for private network access because it separates the networking role (agent as tunnel) from computation (Foundry worker), improving scalability and security.
All connection types use AES-256-GCM encryption for credential storage, with decryption restricted to containers triggered by authorized users.
Agents use unidirectional outbound HTTPS connections, meaning no inbound firewall rules are needed on the customer's private network.
Egress policies provide granular, in-platform control over which external systems can be reached, rather than relying solely on network-level firewall rules.

title: Core Concepts - Streams - Palantir
summary: Explains Foundry streams as a data representation with both a persistent hot buffer and cold storage. Streams provide the same primitives as datasets (branching, version control, permissions, schema management) while also offering low-latency data access. Data is archived from hot to cold storage every few minutes.
relevantParts: Stream architecture with hot buffer and cold storage
Same governance primitives as datasets but with low latency
Archiving process from hot to cold storage

extractedEntities.name: Stream
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry data representation that combines a persistent hot buffer for low-latency access with cold storage backed by a file system, providing the same primitives as datasets (branching, version control, permissions, schema) while enabling real-time data access.

extractedEntities.name: Hot Buffer
extractedEntities.description: The low-latency storage layer of a Foundry stream that receives records as they flow in, providing at-least-once semantics for ingestion and optional exactly-once semantics for processing.

extractedEntities.name: Cold Storage
extractedEntities.description: The persistent file-system-backed storage layer of a Foundry stream where data is archived from the hot buffer every few minutes, making it available as a standard Foundry dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The standard Foundry data resource that serves as the cold storage view of a stream after archiving, enabling any Foundry application to operate on streaming data.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Archiving
extractedEntities.description: The process of transferring data from a stream's hot buffer to cold storage every few minutes, making streaming data available as a standard dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Transaction Model
extractedEntities.description: Unlike datasets which have transaction boundaries, streams treat each row as its own transaction with per-row state tracking, enabling push-based transformations without batching.

extractedEntities.name: High Throughput Stream Type
extractedEntities.description: A stream configuration optimized for large data volumes per second that may introduce non-zero latency as a tradeoff for higher throughput.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform providing the infrastructure for stream processing.

keyInsights: Streams are a dual-layer data representation combining hot buffer (low-latency) and cold storage (standard dataset), bridging real-time and batch processing in a single abstraction.
Streams provide the same primitives as datasets — branching, version control, permissions, schema management — ensuring consistent governance regardless of data velocity.
The per-row transaction model in streams eliminates the need for batch boundaries, enabling push-based transformations and granular state tracking.
Data is archived from hot to cold storage every few minutes, meaning any Foundry application can operate on streaming data even without real-time capabilities.
Stream types allow tuning the latency-throughput tradeoff, with high throughput configurations suited for large-volume ingestion at the cost of some latency.

title: Core Concepts - Change Data Capture (CDC) - Palantir
summary: Foundry supports syncing, processing, and storing data from CDC feeds. CDC is used to stream real-time updates from relational databases, leveraging Debezium. Components throughout the platform (connectors, streams, pipelines, Ontology) all work natively with changelog data. Supports before/after change tracking for audit trails and SCD Type 2.
relevantParts: CDC integration using Debezium for database log reading
Native changelog support across connectors, streams, pipelines, Ontology
Before/after change tracking for audit trails

extractedEntities.name: Change Data Capture
extractedEntities.description: An enterprise data integration pattern for streaming real-time updates from relational databases to Foundry, using changelog metadata (primary keys, ordering columns, deletion columns) to resolve change history into current state.

extractedEntities.name: Changelog Metadata
extractedEntities.description: The required attributes on CDC data consisting of primary key columns, ordering columns (numeric for relative ordering), and a boolean deletion column, used to resolve changelogs to current state.

extractedEntities.name: CDC Resolution Strategy
extractedEntities.description: The algorithm that groups data by primary key, finds the entry with the largest ordering value, and removes deleted records to collapse a changelog into the current view of the source system.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry application that supports full CDC stream processing and partial CDC streams with backfill capability.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept that supports CDC indexing for both batch- and stream-backed objects, enabling real-time Ontology updates.

extractedEntities.name: Stream
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry data representation that provides full CDC support for both live and archive views of changelog data.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's connectivity system that supports syncing CDC data from source systems using Debezium and similar technologies.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's application builder that provides auto-refresh capability for frequently updating objects backed by CDC data.

extractedEntities.name: Debezium Integration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's integration with the open-source Debezium platform for capturing change data from relational databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server.

extractedEntities.name: SCD Type 2 Audit Trail
extractedEntities.description: A use case where CDC before/after change tracking enables maintaining Slowly Changing Dimension Type 2 history for audit trails.

keyInsights: CDC is supported natively across the entire Foundry stack — connectors, streams, pipelines, Ontology, and Workshop — enabling end-to-end real-time data flows from source database to operational application.
Changelog metadata requires three components: primary key columns for record identity, ordering columns for temporal sequencing, and a deletion column for tracking removals.
The CDC resolution strategy is a deterministic algorithm that collapses change history into current state by grouping by primary key, selecting the latest ordering value, and filtering deletions.
Foundry's Ontology supports CDC indexing for both batch- and stream-backed objects, meaning Object Types can reflect real-time changes from source databases.
CDC supports before/after change tracking enabling audit trails and SCD Type 2 historical tracking patterns.

title: Core Concepts - Virtual Tables - Palantir
summary: Virtual tables act as pointers to tables in source systems outside Foundry, abstracting away the underlying source system and storage formats. They can be combined with Foundry datasets, support BigQuery, Databricks, Snowflake, and open table formats. Update detection monitors changes in source systems.
relevantParts: Query external data without moving it into Foundry
Supports BigQuery, Databricks, Snowflake, cloud storage
Update detection for triggering dependent pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Table
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource that acts as a pointer to a table in an external source system, abstracting away the underlying storage format and enabling federated queries without first storing data in Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Federated Query
extractedEntities.description: An architectural pattern where Virtual Tables enable querying external data systems directly without copying data into Foundry, supporting flexible architectures where storage remains external.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Table Locator
extractedEntities.description: The identifier within a Virtual Table definition that specifies the exact table in the source system, such as database, schema, and table name.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The standard Foundry data resource that can be combined with Virtual Tables in workflows, representing data stored within Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: BigQuery
extractedEntities.description: Google's cloud data warehouse supported as a Virtual Table source in Foundry with support for tables, views, and materialized views.

extractedEntities.name: Databricks
extractedEntities.description: A data platform supported as a Virtual Table source in Foundry with Unity Catalog integration for Iceberg tables.

extractedEntities.name: Snowflake
extractedEntities.description: A cloud data platform supported as a Virtual Table source in Foundry with Horizon Catalog support for Iceberg tables.

extractedEntities.name: Iceberg Catalog
extractedEntities.description: A catalog service required to load Virtual Tables backed by Apache Iceberg, with Foundry supporting AWS Glue, Polaris, Unity Catalog, and Object Storage catalogs depending on the source.

extractedEntities.name: Update Detection
extractedEntities.description: A Virtual Table capability that monitors changes in external source systems, enabling Foundry to detect when underlying data has been modified.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform that orchestrates virtual tables with security, permissions, and workflow capabilities.

keyInsights: Virtual Tables abstract away the source system and storage format, enabling seamless workflows that combine data from different external systems and Foundry datasets.
Virtual Tables support creating new virtual tables as outputs from Foundry transformations, enabling fully external storage architectures where Foundry handles only orchestration and security.
Supported sources include BigQuery, Databricks, Snowflake, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake, and Google Cloud Storage, with varying levels of Iceberg catalog support.
Virtual Tables are governed by the same Foundry security and permissions model as native datasets, ensuring consistent access control regardless of where data physically resides.
The federated query pattern enables organizations to avoid data consolidation while still building unified workflows across heterogeneous data platforms.

title: Core Concepts - Iceberg Tables - Palantir
summary: Apache Iceberg is available in Foundry as both managed and virtual Iceberg tables. Foundry natively implements the Iceberg REST Catalog specification and supports third-party catalogs like Databricks Unity Catalog. Benefits include interoperability, automated compaction, DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE INTO support, and incremental changelog consumption.
relevantParts: Managed vs virtual Iceberg table types
Open format interoperability with third-party catalogs

extractedEntities.name: Iceberg Table
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource type based on the Apache Iceberg open table format, available as both managed tables within Foundry and virtual tables via external catalogs, providing interoperability, compaction, row edits, and changelog support.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Iceberg REST Catalog
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's native implementation of the Apache Iceberg REST Catalog specification, enabling third-party tools to read and write Foundry Iceberg tables via a standard API.

extractedEntities.name: Iceberg Compaction
extractedEntities.description: Automated support for compacting small files in Iceberg tables without affecting incremental reads, improving query performance over time.

extractedEntities.name: Iceberg Row Edits
extractedEntities.description: Support for DELETE, UPDATE, and MERGE INTO statements on Iceberg tables, enabling conditional row modifications without re-snapshotting the entire table.

extractedEntities.name: Iceberg Changelogs
extractedEntities.description: The ability to incrementally consume row deletions and updates from Iceberg tables, supporting streaming and CDC use cases.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The standard Foundry data resource that Iceberg tables offer as an alternative to, with different tradeoffs around interoperability and features.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Table
extractedEntities.description: A pointer to an external table that can reference Iceberg tables in third-party catalogs like Databricks Unity Catalog.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform that natively implements the Iceberg REST Catalog specification.

keyInsights: Foundry natively implements the Iceberg REST Catalog specification, making it a first-class participant in the broader Iceberg ecosystem rather than merely consuming Iceberg data.

Iceberg tables provide four key benefits over standard datasets: interoperability with third-party tools, automated compaction, row-level edits (DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE INTO), and incremental changelog consumption.
The row edit capability (DELETE, UPDATE, MERGE INTO) eliminates the need to re-snapshot entire tables for conditional modifications, significantly improving efficiency for large tables.
Iceberg tables serve as a bridge between Foundry and external data platforms, with support for catalogs like Databricks Unity Catalog, AWS Glue, and Snowflake Horizon Catalog.
Iceberg tables are currently in Beta, with features and coverage expected to expand over time.

title: Indexing - Overview - Palantir
summary: Documents the object indexing system, where the Object Data Funnel microservice orchestrates data writes into the Ontology via OSv2. Covers funnel batch and streaming pipelines, hydration of search nodes, and incremental object indexing for improved performance.
relevantParts: Object Data Funnel architecture for Ontology writes
Incremental object indexing and hydration process

extractedEntities.name: Object Indexing
extractedEntities.description: The process of making tabular or other forms of data in Foundry datasources available for faster retrieval through specialized databases, managed by the Object Data Funnel service.

extractedEntities.name: Object Data Funnel
extractedEntities.description: The microservice (Funnel) that orchestrates funnel pipelines to create and modify object instances in the Ontology, ensuring up-to-date data and metadata in Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: An indexing mechanism that processes data in batches to create and update object instances in the Ontology, suitable for periodic data refreshes.

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: An indexing mechanism that processes data in real-time streams to create and update object instances in the Ontology, suitable for low-latency requirements.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: The current-generation object storage system (OSv2) in Foundry that serves as the target for indexed object data, replacing the legacy Phonograph system.

extractedEntities.name: Phonograph
extractedEntities.description: The legacy Object Storage V1 system that preceded OSv2, with separate documentation for its indexing process.

extractedEntities.name: Search Node Hydration
extractedEntities.description: The process of populating search index nodes with object data during indexing, enabling fast retrieval operations on Ontology objects.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept whose instances are created and maintained through the object indexing process.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform providing the infrastructure for object indexing.

keyInsights: Object indexing is the critical bridge between raw tabular data in Foundry datasources and the Ontology's object model, making data available through specialized retrieval databases.
The Object Data Funnel service is the orchestrator that manages the entire indexing lifecycle, creating and modifying object instances while ensuring data and metadata consistency.
Two indexing mechanisms exist — batch and streaming pipelines — allowing users to choose based on their latency requirements, cost considerations, and datasource landscape.
Object Storage V2 (OSv2) is the current-generation storage system, having replaced the legacy Phonograph (V1) system, indicating ongoing platform evolution.
Search node hydration is a distinct step in the indexing process, populating specialized search indices for fast object retrieval.

title: Concepts - Restricted Views - Palantir
summary: Documentation on Restricted Views that limit dataset access to only rows a user has permission to see. Restricted views use granular policies based on user attributes, group memberships, and data values to filter rows. They are read-only to prevent schema alterations that could expose restricted data.
relevantParts: Restricted views enforce row-level security based on user attributes
Read-only to prevent schema changes that could leak restricted data
Granular policies compare user attributes against data columns

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry security mechanism that limits dataset access to only the rows a user has permission to see, built on top of a backing dataset using granular policies based on user attributes, group memberships, and data values.

extractedEntities.name: Granular Policy
extractedEntities.description: A policy consisting of rules and logical operators that compare user attributes, column values, and specific values to determine which rows are visible to each user in a Restricted View.

extractedEntities.name: User Attributes
extractedEntities.description: Properties of the user viewing data (such as group memberships or organization) used in granular policies to determine row-level access in Restricted Views.

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access controls in Foundry that provide coarse-grained file and transaction-level access restrictions, complemented by Restricted Views for finer-grained row-level control.

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Discretionary access controls in Foundry that grant permissions to users on resources, with the Owner role required to create Restricted Views.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept that can be backed by a Restricted View to control which objects individual users can see based on row-level policies.

extractedEntities.name: Row-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: The security pattern implemented by Restricted Views where different users see different subsets of rows from the same underlying dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: The backing data resource for a Restricted View, providing the raw rows that are filtered by the granular policy.

extractedEntities.name: Branch-Based Customer Access
extractedEntities.description: A use case where sales representatives are limited to viewing only customers assigned to their branch, implemented through Restricted Views with user attribute-based policies.

keyInsights: Restricted Views provide row-level security on top of the coarser markings and roles mechanisms, enabling per-user data filtering based on user attributes and data values.
Restricted Views are read-only to prevent schema alterations that could expose restricted data, enforcing immutability of the security boundary.
Granular policies compare user attributes (group memberships, organization) against column values, enabling dynamic row filtering without creating separate datasets per user group.
A Restricted View can serve as the backing data source for an Object Type, meaning Ontology-level access control can be driven by row-level policies on the underlying data.
Policies require UUIDs rather than names for users, groups, and organizations to prevent access control bypass through entity renaming.

title: Concepts - Markings - Palantir
summary: Documentation on Markings, Foundry's implementation of mandatory access controls. Markings define eligibility criteria that restrict visibility and actions, propagate through data lineage and file hierarchy, and travel with the data unlike role-based access. Removing markings requires the Expand Access permission on the marking itself.
relevantParts: Mandatory access controls independent of roles
PII and sensitive data restriction patterns
Markings are mandatory controls that propagate through data lineage
Markings travel with data, unlike role-based access which is location-based
Removing markings requires special Expand Access permission, independent of Owner role

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's implementation of mandatory access controls that define binary eligibility criteria restricting visibility and actions. Markings propagate through both file hierarchy and data dependencies, traveling with the data unlike role-based access.

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control paradigm implemented by Markings where access restrictions are centrally managed and cannot be removed by resource owners without the Expand Access permission on the marking itself.

extractedEntities.name: Discretionary Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control paradigm implemented by Roles where any user with the Owner role on a resource can grant roles to other users without centralized restrictions.

extractedEntities.name: Marking Propagation
extractedEntities.description: The mechanism by which Markings automatically flow to derived resources through two paths: file hierarchy inheritance (parent folders/Projects to children) and data dependency inheritance (upstream datasets to downstream outputs).

extractedEntities.name: Expand Access Permission
extractedEntities.description: A centrally managed permission on a Marking itself that is required to remove (un-mark) a Marking from a resource, ensuring data protection officers maintain control.

extractedEntities.name: Data Marking
extractedEntities.description: A Marking inherited through data dependencies (upstream datasets), distinct from file hierarchy markings, representing that the data itself carries the classification.

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Discretionary access controls in Foundry that expand access through data sharing workflows, contrasted with Markings which restrict access through mandatory eligibility criteria.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept whose backing data inherits Markings, with binary all-or-nothing access regardless of user roles.

extractedEntities.name: PII Marking
extractedEntities.description: A common use case where a PII Marking restricts access to personally identifiable information to only users who have completed required trainings, with the marking propagating to all derived datasets.

keyInsights: Markings implement mandatory access controls that are binary (all-or-nothing) and conjunctive (boolean AND) — a user must be a member of ALL markings on a resource to have any access.
Markings propagate through two distinct inheritance paths: file hierarchy (parent to child) and data dependency (upstream to downstream), ensuring derived data inherits source restrictions.
The critical distinction between Markings (mandatory) and Roles (discretionary) is that Markings restrict access centrally while Roles expand access through owner-driven sharing.
Removing a Marking requires the Expand Access permission on the marking itself, not just ownership of the resource — even Owners cannot remove markings without this central authorization.
Data markings travel with the data through the pipeline, meaning classifications are preserved regardless of where data is moved or transformed, providing end-to-end data protection.

title: Foundry Technical Overview v4 (PDF) - Palantir
summary: Official Palantir technical overview PDF for Foundry, providing a comprehensive architectural description of the platform including data integration, transformation, Ontology, and operational capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's comprehensive data operations platform providing data integration, transformation, Ontology building, and operational capabilities as described in the official technical overview.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The central software architecture of Foundry that models enterprise entities, relationships, and operations as a semantic layer over underlying data.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core Ontology concept representing a category of real-world entities with properties and relationships.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology concept defining typed relationships between Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology concept defining executable operations that modify objects and enforce business logic.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology concept providing reusable computation logic that can be referenced by actions, interfaces, and AI workflows.

keyInsights: The Foundry Technical Overview v4 PDF is the official architectural reference document for the platform, though its binary content cannot be directly extracted from raw PDF encoding.
Foundry's architecture encompasses data integration, transformation, Ontology, and operational capabilities as an integrated stack.
The Ontology is positioned as the central layer connecting raw data to operational decision-making through Object Types, Link Types, Action Types, and Functions.

title: What is a data pipeline? - Palantir
summary: Explains the concept of a data pipeline in Foundry as the flow of data from a source system through intermediate datasets to produce high-quality curated datasets. These curated datasets can be structured into the Ontology or serve as the foundation for machine learning and analytical workflows.
relevantParts: Defines the end-to-end data pipeline concept in Foundry
Shows how pipelines connect raw data to Ontology and analytics

extractedEntities.name: Data Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Flow of data from a source system through intermediate datasets to produce high-quality curated datasets that can be structured into the Ontology or serve ML/analytical workflows

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool enabling users to quickly set up pipelines with a point-and-click interface, supporting both technical and less-technical users

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Data platform providing the digital view of objective reality within an organization through data integration

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity backed by curated datasets produced through data pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Ownership
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where each production pipeline has designated person(s) overseeing regular, reliable data flow to power business processes

extractedEntities.name: Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for periodic data processing at scheduled intervals

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for processing only new or changed data since the last run

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type for continuous real-time data processing

extractedEntities.name: Build Scheduling
extractedEntities.description: Configuration of recurring data transformation runs, from weekly/daily to hourly or more frequent cadences

extractedEntities.name: Data Expectations
extractedEntities.description: Quality checks on pipeline inputs and outputs at every step, recommended during initial pipeline definition stages

keyInsights: Data pipelines serve to provide a digital view of objective reality by syncing data from source systems, imposing common schema, and combining datasets
Three pipeline types available in Foundry: batch, incremental, and streaming — choice depends on data scale, latency requirements, and maintenance complexity
A production-quality pipeline requires ownership, build scheduling, data quality checks, security governance, and documentation
Pipeline Builder enables both technical users (rapid pipeline building) and less-technical users (point-and-click forms) to create pipelines
Curated datasets produced by pipelines can either be structured into the Ontology or serve as foundation for ML and analytical workflows

title: Pipeline Builder - Core Concepts - Palantir
summary: Covers Pipeline Builder's core concepts including graph and form-based interfaces, type-safe functions that flag errors immediately, strict output checks to prevent unintentional downstream breaks, and version control via pipeline branches similar to Git code branches.
relevantParts: Type-safe functions and strict output checks for data quality

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for building data pipelines with graph and form-based interfaces, supporting datasets, virtual tables, and Ontology components as outputs

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Building block of a pipeline — a wrapper around a collection of files stored in a backing file system, representing data from landing to Ontology mapping

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Branch
extractedEntities.description: Version control mechanism in Pipeline Builder operating like Git branches, allowing isolated iteration without affecting the main pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Function definition accepting inputs (datasets) and producing outputs, forming the links between datasets in a pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Output
extractedEntities.description: Result of transforms: can be datasets, virtual tables, or Ontology components (object types, link types, time series)

extractedEntities.name: Build Schedule
extractedEntities.description: Recurring build configuration that can trigger at specific times, cadences, or based on upstream resource status updates

extractedEntities.name: Data Expectations
extractedEntities.description: Quality checks on outputs and intermediate transforms supporting primary key and row count expectation types

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as a Pipeline Builder output

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as a Pipeline Builder output

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing the dataset, branching, and version control infrastructure used by Pipeline Builder

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder uses Git-like branching for version control: users iterate on branches and propose merges back to Main, just like code pull requests
Pipeline outputs are not limited to datasets — they can be virtual tables or Ontology components including object types, link types, and time series
Transforms are function definitions accepting inputs and producing outputs, forming the logical links in a pipeline graph
Data expectations currently support two types: primary key validation and row count checks on pipeline outputs
Build schedules can be time-based, cadence-based, or event-driven (triggered by upstream resource updates)

title: Pipeline Builder - Transforms - Overview - Palantir
summary: Documents the transforms available within Pipeline Builder, enabling users to apply data transformations without specialized programming language knowledge. Pipeline Builder integrates various programming languages beneath a streamlined builder interface.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry data transformation tool providing a general model for describing transformations with an intermediate layer between writing tools and execution engines

extractedEntities.name: Expression
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder data transformation that takes columns as input and outputs a single column (e.g., Split string, Cast, Concatenate strings, Length)

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder data transformation that takes an entire table as input and returns an entire table (e.g., Pivot, Filter, Drop columns)

extractedEntities.name: Join Transform
extractedEntities.description: Combines two datasets with at least one matching column, with configurable join types for matching/excluding rows

extractedEntities.name: Union Transform
extractedEntities.description: Combines two datasets to include all rows, requiring all inputs to have the same schema

extractedEntities.name: User-Defined Function
extractedEntities.description: Custom code (Python recommended, Java available) that can be versioned and upgraded, for cases where built-in transforms are insufficient

extractedEntities.name: No-Code Data Transformation
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline Builder's approach of abstracting programming languages behind a streamlined builder interface for data transformation

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology component that can be produced as output from Pipeline Builder transforms

keyInsights: Pipeline Builder uses a general intermediate layer between transformation writing and execution, supporting multiple output types including datasets, Ontology objects, streams, and time-series
Two transformation categories exist: expressions (column-in, column-out) and transforms (table-in, table-out), unified in the same configuration interface
Expressions can be embedded within transforms (e.g., Length expression inside a Filter transform) for composable data manipulation
User-defined functions (Python recommended) provide escape hatch for complex logic but should be used sparingly in favor of optimized built-in transforms
Pipeline Builder decouples transformation logic from specific execution engines, allowing the same transformation model to target batch, streaming, or Ontology outputs

title: Data Connection - Core Concepts - Palantir
summary: Explains Data Connection's core concepts including sources, connectors (source types), syncs, and the relationship between them. A source represents a connection to an external system, set up based on a particular connector, with a broad range of connectors available to support common enterprise data systems.
relevantParts: Defines sources, connectors, and sync primitives

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for connecting to external systems, managing sources, credentials, workers, and syncs

extractedEntities.name: Source
extractedEntities.description: A single connection to an external system including configuration to specify/locate the target and credentials for authentication

extractedEntities.name: Connector
extractedEntities.description: Source type defining the kind of external system to connect to (e.g., Postgres, S3, REST API). Also called source type

extractedEntities.name: Sync
extractedEntities.description: Configuration for bringing specific sets of data from a source into Foundry as datasets

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker
extractedEntities.description: Default compute worker running source capabilities in Foundry's containerized, scalable job execution environment

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker
extractedEntities.description: Advanced compute worker running on customer-provided host via agent, for specific file-based syncs or micro-batching with on-premise systems

extractedEntities.name: Credential
extractedEntities.description: Encrypted secret value (password, token, API key) required to authenticate with external systems, stored securely in the platform

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Network policy controlling outbound connections from Foundry worker sources to external systems

extractedEntities.name: External Transform
extractedEntities.description: Code-based connectivity option for systems without dedicated connectors, using the generic or REST API source

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform hosting Data Connection and providing the compute infrastructure for data syncing

keyInsights: Data Connection supports two compute models: Foundry worker (preferred, containerized, scalable) and Agent worker (for on-premise systems not accessible over the Internet)
Sources are configured based on connectors (source types) with a broad range available for common enterprise data systems
For systems without dedicated connectors, generic connector or REST API source can be used with code-based options like external transforms and compute modules
All credentials are encrypted and stored securely, with some sources supporting secretless authentication via OpenID Connect or cloud identity
Foundry worker sources benefit from containerized, scalable job execution and now support both direct egress and agent proxy networking

title: Data Connection - Sources - Set up a source - Palantir
summary: Step-by-step guide for setting up a data source in Foundry, covering network configuration, credentials management via dedicated service accounts, and source creation through the Data Connection interface. Explains the difference between Foundry worker and agent worker connection types.
relevantParts: Practical guide for configuring external data sources
Worker types: Foundry worker vs agent worker

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application where sources are created and configured to connect external systems to Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Source Setup
extractedEntities.description: Four-step process: validate network connection, provision credentials, configure agent drivers (if agent worker), create source in Data Connection UI

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Worker Source
extractedEntities.description: Connection type running compute in Foundry, supporting direct egress and agent proxy networking

extractedEntities.name: Agent Worker Source
extractedEntities.description: Connection type running compute on customer-provided host via agent, requiring two network paths: agent-to-external-system and agent-to-Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Agent
extractedEntities.description: Single point of validated entry from customer network to Foundry, handling data reading and sending with traffic flowing only outbound from agent

extractedEntities.name: Service Account
extractedEntities.description: Dedicated credentials provisioned specifically for Foundry source authentication, separate from personal accounts

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Network policy for Foundry worker sources enabling direct connection or agent proxy connections

extractedEntities.name: Sync
extractedEntities.description: Configuration for bringing specific data into Foundry from a connected source, entirely configurable through Data Connection UI

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform receiving data from external systems through configured sources

keyInsights: Source setup requires network validation, credential provisioning, optional agent configuration, and UI-based source creation — syncs are then entirely configurable through Data Connection
Agent worker networking requires two separate paths: agent-to-external-system and agent-to-Foundry, with traffic only flowing outbound from the agent
Dedicated service accounts should be provisioned for Foundry authentication rather than using personal credentials
Foundry worker sources now support both direct egress policies and agent proxy policies, making 'direct connection' naming obsolete
Source types include Postgres databases, S3 buckets, Linux filesystems, SAP instances, REST APIs, and many more enterprise data systems

title: Core Concepts - Branching - Palantir
summary: Explains dataset branching in Foundry, which provides foundational semantics for versioning data. Foundry combines dataset branching with Git branching to support branching over logic and data simultaneously, allowing different users to write concurrently to their own branches.
relevantParts: Dataset branching for parallel data development
Integration of dataset and code branching

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Branch
extractedEntities.description: Version control mechanism for datasets analogous to Git branches — a pointer to the most recent transaction, enabling concurrent work without interference

extractedEntities.name: Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Unit of dataset change analogous to a Git commit, forming a linear sequence on a branch ordered by commit time

extractedEntities.name: Commit
extractedEntities.description: Record of changes (additions and removals) made within a branch, tracked for clear history

extractedEntities.name: Pull Request
extractedEntities.description: Signal to team that branch changes are ready for review and merging back to master, enabling code review workflow

extractedEntities.name: Data Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Pattern applying software version control best practices to data pipelines: branch, commit, pull request, review, merge

extractedEntities.name: Logic and Data Branching
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's combination of Git-based code branching with dataset branching for simultaneous versioning of both logic and data

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform implementing dataset branching alongside Git-like version control for code, enabling branching over logic and data simultaneously

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool supporting branching workflow for data pipeline development

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application implementing Git-like version control for data transformation code, designed for one active developer per branch

keyInsights: Foundry uniquely combines dataset branching with Git-based code branching, enabling simultaneous versioning of both logic and data
Dataset branches are analogous to Git branches: datasets, branches, and transactions map to Git repositories, branches, and commits respectively
The branching workflow follows standard Git patterns: create branch, make commits, create pull request, review, merge to master
Code Repositories is designed for one active developer per branch — concurrent edits on the same branch risk overwrites
Branching enables safe experimentation: users work in isolation and merge only validated changes back to the production pipeline

title: Why Data Pipeline Version Control Matters - Palantir Blog
summary: Palantir blog post covering data pipeline version control, explaining why tracking code and data together is essential. Versioned output datasets enable safely scheduling transformation jobs across the data platform without risk of data corruption.
relevantParts: Rationale for combined code+data version control
Safe scheduling of transformation jobs with versioned outputs

extractedEntities.name: Data Pipeline Version Control
extractedEntities.description: Practice of tracking changes to both code (transformation logic) and data together, enabling safe scheduling, auditability, and understanding which code+data combination produced which outcome

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform launched in 2016 with heavy focus on data pipeline tooling and version control as a key component

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's earlier platform featuring the Revisioning Database, a framework applying version control concepts to graph databases

extractedEntities.name: Revisioning Database
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's first technologies applying version control concepts to graph databases in the Gotham platform

extractedEntities.name: Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Collection of data files (spreadsheets, documents, images, etc.) that form the nodes of a data pipeline

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Software code/logic that changes a dataset — a pipeline can have hundreds of transforms applying incremental changes

extractedEntities.name: Metadata Tracking
extractedEntities.description: Tracking source system origin, security policies, developer changes alongside data, often more important than tracking data changes themselves

extractedEntities.name: Role-Based Data Views
extractedEntities.description: Version control enables users with different roles to see different versions of the same data file based on their respective authorizations

extractedEntities.name: Versioned Output Dataset
extractedEntities.description: Dataset with version history enabling safe scheduling of transformation jobs across the platform without risk of data corruption

keyInsights: Version control for data pipelines tracks both code changes (transformation logic) and data changes simultaneously, with metadata often more important than the data changes themselves
Palantir's version control obsession began with the Revisioning Database in Gotham, applying VC concepts to graph databases before extending to Foundry
Metadata captured includes source system origin, security policies, and developer attribution — informing much of the end-user experience including role-based data visibility
Versioned output datasets enable safe scheduling of transformation jobs across the platform without risk of data corruption from concurrent writes
Understanding which combination of code and data produced which outcome is the key value proposition of data pipeline version control

title: Ontology SDK - Python OSDK - Palantir
summary: Documentation for the Python OSDK enabling Python developers to access Ontology objects, perform filtering, aggregations, and execute actions. Provides programmatic access to the full Ontology data model from Python environments.
relevantParts: Python SDK for Ontology data access
Filtering, aggregation, and action execution capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Python OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Python SDK for accessing Ontology objects, performing filtering, aggregations, and executing actions programmatically from Python environments.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit providing type-safe, code-generated client libraries for interacting with the Ontology data model across multiple languages.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for generating Ontology-specific SDK documentation and managing developer configurations.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet

extractedEntities.name: Action Type

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2

extractedEntities.name: Phonograph
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage backend with a 10,000 result limit per query, superseded by Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Page Token Pagination
extractedEntities.description: SDK pagination mechanism using nextPageToken for iterating through large result sets with configurable page sizes up to 10,000.

extractedEntities.name: Code-Generated SDK
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where SDK client code is auto-generated from Ontology schema definitions, providing type-safe access to Object Types, Properties, and Actions.

extractedEntities.name: Aggregation API
extractedEntities.description: SDK capability to compute aggregate values (count, min, max, sum, avg, approximateDistinct) over ObjectSets with optional groupBy clauses.

keyInsights: Python OSDK provides programmatic access to the full Ontology data model including objects, filtering, aggregations, and action execution.
Object Storage V2 removes the 10,000 result limit that existed with the legacy Phonograph backend, enabling unlimited queries.
SDK documentation can be auto-generated specific to a customer's Ontology via Developer Console, providing tailored code examples.
Pagination uses a page token pattern with configurable page sizes up to 10,000 objects per page.
The SDK exposes aggregation operations (count, min, max, sum, avg, approximateDistinct) with groupBy support over ObjectSets.
Object responses include a __rid (resource identifier) and __primaryKey alongside all typed properties.

title: Data Lineage - Graphs - Explore artifacts and ontology entities - Palantir
summary: Documents how Data Lineage tracks not just datasets but also Ontology entities, showing the connection between raw data artifacts and their downstream Ontology representations.

Connection between data artifacts and Ontology objects

title: Building Pipelines - Types of Pipelines - Palantir
summary: Describes the three primary pipeline types in Foundry: batch pipelines (fully recompute on each run), incremental pipelines (process only new/changed data), and streaming pipelines (run continuously). Each type has different latency, cost, and use case characteristics.

Tradeoffs between latency, cost, and completeness

extractedEntities.name: Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type that fully recomputes every dataset on each run, offering low complexity but high latency and compute cost, with low resilience to data scale changes.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type that processes only new/changed data since the last run, offering low latency and compute cost with medium complexity and high resilience to data scale.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Continuously-running pipeline that processes data as it arrives, offering very low latency but high complexity and compute cost with high resilience to data scale.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing three pipeline types with different latency, complexity, cost, and scale resilience tradeoffs.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type used by incremental pipelines where only new data is added to existing datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Progression Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Recommended approach of starting with batch pipelines for validation, then extending to incremental as scale demands, keeping streaming for real-time requirements.

keyInsights: Three pipeline types exist with clear tradeoff dimensions: batch (simple/expensive), incremental (moderate/efficient), streaming (complex/real-time).
Batch pipelines should be the starting point for pipeline development, with incremental extension as data scale validates the use case.
Incremental pipelines offer the best balance of latency (minutes) and compute cost for growing datasets, but require understanding of transactions.
Streaming pipelines provide the lowest latency (seconds) but carry the highest complexity and continuous compute costs.
Python or Java are recommended for batch pipelines that may need future incremental support, as SQL does not support incremental computation.
Foundry also offers 'faster' variants of batch and incremental pipelines that sacrifice some expression support for improved performance.

title: Building Pipelines - Incremental Pipelines - Overview - Palantir
summary: Incremental pipelines process only new data that has changed since the last run, avoiding unnecessary computation on unchanged rows/files. extractedEntities.description: Foundry service that provides incremental sync capabilities for bringing data into Foundry incrementally.

Long-running incremental pipelines accumulate many small files from APPEND transactions, requiring Spark partitioning management for sustained performance.
Incremental syncs in Data Connection are the first step in building an end-to-end incremental pipeline.
Unit testing is strongly recommended before production deployment to validate behavior under both incremental and snapshot scenarios.

title: Indexing - Funnel Batch Pipelines - Palantir
summary: Details how funnel batch pipelines orchestrate efficient indexing of data from Foundry datasources and user edits into OSv2. Covers live pipelines for production updates and replacement pipelines for schema changes requiring full reindexing.

Schema change handling via replacement pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Internal Foundry job pipeline that orchestrates efficient indexing of data from datasources and user edits into Object Storage V2 through a four-stage process.

extractedEntities.name: Changelog Stage
extractedEntities.description: First stage of Funnel batch pipeline that computes data differences for all datasources when new data arrives, creating intermediate changelog datasets with APPEND transactions.

extractedEntities.name: Merge Changes Stage
extractedEntities.description: Second stage that joins changelog datasets and user edits by primary key to merge all changes into a unified dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Indexing Stage
extractedEntities.description: Third stage that converts merged change rows into format compatible with configured object databases (e.g., OSv2 index files).

extractedEntities.name: Hydration Stage
extractedEntities.description: Final stage where object databases prepare indexed data for querying by downloading index files to search node disks, enabling the Object Type to be queried.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's canonical object database that stores indexed Object Type data, receiving updates through Funnel batch pipeline hydration.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity whose data is indexed into OSv2 by Funnel batch pipelines, combining datasource data and user edits.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Property used by the Merge Changes stage to join changelog datasets and user edits, ensuring all changes for the same object are consolidated.

extractedEntities.name: Live Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Funnel pipeline variant that runs whenever datasources update and every six hours for user edit persistence, keeping object types current in production.

extractedEntities.name: Replacement Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Funnel pipeline variant that performs full reindexing when schema changes require it, rebuilding the entire object database.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept that generates user edits which are merged into Funnel pipelines, with edits applied immediately to indexes and persisted every six hours.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that displays hydration progress and manages Object Type configurations affected by Funnel pipelines.

keyInsights: Funnel batch pipelines are a four-stage internal process: Changelog, Merge Changes, Indexing, and Hydration, orchestrating data flow from datasources to OSv2.
User edits from Actions are merged with datasource changes by primary key, applied immediately to indexes but persisted to Foundry datasets every six hours.
Live pipelines run on datasource updates and every six hours for user edit persistence; replacement pipelines run for schema changes requiring full reindexing.
Hydration is the final step where OSv2 search nodes download index files to disk, making the Object Type queryable; progress is visible in Ontology Manager.
All intermediate datasets (changelog, merged, index) are owned and controlled by Funnel and not accessible to users.
The changelog stage uses incremental computation semantics with APPEND transactions to efficiently capture only data differences.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry frontend application that uniquely supports live data refresh for streaming object types, unlike other Foundry frontends.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for integrating external data streams into Foundry for use as input datasources.

extractedEntities.name: Most Recent Update Wins Strategy
extractedEntities.description: OSv2 streaming conflict resolution strategy where every stream is treated as a changelog stream and the latest update overwrites previous state.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Record Limits
extractedEntities.description: Streaming object types enforce a 1MB maximum record size and 250 maximum properties per object type.

extractedEntities.name: Changelog Stream Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Streaming pattern where events are treated as a changelog with most-recent-update-wins semantics. Requires ordered input to guarantee correctness.

keyInsights: OSv2 streaming uses a most-recent-update-wins strategy treating all streams as changelog streams, meaning out-of-order events result in incorrect Ontology data.
Streaming object types have significant limitations: no user edits, no multi-datasource object types (MDOs), and no live data refresh outside Workshop.
Sub-15-second Ontology availability is achievable for streaming data by bypassing batch infrastructure entirely.
Streaming object types are limited to 250 properties and 1MB record size, requiring smaller ontology models for larger records.
Workshop is the only Foundry frontend that supports live data refresh for streaming object types; all other applications require manual refresh.
Stream datasources can also be configured for many-to-many link types, not just object types.

title: Data Connection - Exports - Overview - Palantir
summary: Data Connection supports exporting datasets and streams from Foundry to external systems including data warehouses and data lakes. Supports table exports (INSERT/API calls), streaming exports, and file exports, with snapshot and incremental export modes.
relevantParts: Bidirectional data flow: export from Foundry to external systems
Table, streaming, and file export types

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that supports bidirectional data flow, including exporting datasets and streams from Foundry to external systems like data warehouses and data lakes.

extractedEntities.name: Table Export
extractedEntities.description: Export type that writes Foundry dataset rows to external tables using INSERT statements or API calls, supporting both snapshot and incremental modes.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Export
extractedEntities.description: Export type that sends Foundry streaming data to external streaming platforms in real-time.

extractedEntities.name: File Export
extractedEntities.description: Export type that sends Foundry files to external storage systems like Amazon S3.

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security classifications applied to data. Exports require explicit configuration of which Markings are allowed to be exported to each source, enforced by the Information Security Officer role.

extractedEntities.name: Information Security Officer
extractedEntities.description: A default Foundry role that must enable exports per source and configure which Markings and Organizations may be exported.

extractedEntities.name: Organizations
extractedEntities.description: Organizational security boundary in Foundry. Data with specific Organization markings must have those Organizations explicitly allowed for export.

extractedEntities.name: Snapshot Export Mode
extractedEntities.description: Export mode that exports the complete state of a dataset at a point in time to the external system.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Export Mode
extractedEntities.description: Export mode that exports only new or changed data since the last export to the external system.

extractedEntities.name: Bidirectional Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where Foundry both imports data from and exports data to external systems, enabling Foundry to serve as a data processing hub within a broader data platform.

extractedEntities.name: ML Inference Export
extractedEntities.description: Exporting machine learning inference results from Foundry batch deployments to external systems for operationalizing ML projects across the organization.

extractedEntities.name: Operational Data Writeback
extractedEntities.description: Writing operational data captured from end users in the Ontology to external systems for analysis outside Foundry.

keyInsights: Data Connection exports enforce security through Markings: every exportable Marking and Organization must be explicitly allowed per source by an Information Security Officer.
Three export types exist: table exports (row-level INSERT/API), streaming exports (real-time), and file exports (bulk file transfer), each supported by different source types.
Exports support both snapshot (full state) and incremental (delta only) modes, with the choice depending on the source type capabilities.
As of June 2025, exports with no new data are marked as success instead of failure, a behavioral change that may impact dependent workflows.
Legacy export tasks are being replaced by updated file, streaming, and table export capabilities, with legacy tasks no longer recommended.
Export credentials must have write access on the target external system (e.g., s3:PutObject for S3 destinations).

title: Object Edits and Materializations - Materializations - Palantir
summary: Materializations (formerly writeback datasets in OSv1) capture user edits made through Actions back into persistent Foundry datasets. In OSv2, materializations are optional. Supports automatic propagation of user edits with a few-minutes latency.
relevantParts: Materialization as the bridge between Ontology edits and datasets
Optional in OSv2 vs required in OSv1

extractedEntities.name: Materialization
extractedEntities.description: A persistent Foundry dataset that captures the latest state of each object by combining data from input datasources and user edits. Renamed from 'writeback dataset' in the OSv1-to-OSv2 transition. Optional in OSv2.

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Dataset
extractedEntities.description: OSv1 (Phonograph) equivalent of materializations. Required in OSv1 to enable user edits on object types or many-to-many link types with join tables.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity. In OSv2, user edits can be enabled without materializations by toggling the Edits configuration in Ontology Manager.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism through which user edits are applied to objects in the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attribute of an Object Type. OSv2 allows materializing only a subset of properties from an object type.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V1
extractedEntities.description: First-generation object storage (Phonograph) where writeback datasets were required for user edits. Uses scheduled builds for data freshness.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Second-generation object storage where materializations are optional and supports automatic propagation of user edits with few-minutes latency.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for managing object type configurations including the Datasources and Materializations tabs.

extractedEntities.name: Automatic Edit Propagation
extractedEntities.description: OSv2 feature that automatically propagates user edits to materialized datasets with a latency of a few minutes, incurring additional compute cost.

extractedEntities.name: Materialized Object Dataset
extractedEntities.description: A dataset type created from materializations that contains the latest state of objects combining input datasources and user edits.

extractedEntities.name: Object Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: A type of materialization output that provides a restricted view of object data with various configurations.

extractedEntities.name: Downstream Pipeline Integration
extractedEntities.description: Building downstream Foundry pipelines that require the latest state of each object including user edits via materialized datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Bulk Ontology Download
extractedEntities.description: Enabling downloads of Ontology data containing the latest state of all objects for an object type via materialized datasets.

keyInsights: Materializations (formerly writeback datasets) are optional in OSv2, a major simplification from OSv1 where they were required to enable user edits.
OSv2 supports automatic propagation of user edits to materialized datasets with a few-minutes latency, replacing OSv1's manual build scheduling approach.
Multiple materialized datasets can be created per object type in OSv2, each materializing only a subset of properties.
Two main use cases drive materialization creation: downstream pipeline integration needing latest object state, and bulk Ontology data downloads.
Edit history preservation is a separate concern during OSv1-to-OSv2 migration and incurs additional compute costs.
OSv2 materialized datasets update automatically and cannot be built manually from Dataset Preview, unlike OSv1 writeback datasets which relied on manual schedules.

title: Architecture Center - Interoperability - Palantir
summary: Documents Foundry's interoperability capabilities. All data is stored in original format (CSV, Iceberg, Parquet) and accessible through standard interfaces (REST, JDBC, S3-compatible). All transformed data is accessible in open formats like Apache Iceberg and Apache Parquet by default.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform built on open data standards, storing data in original formats (CSV, Iceberg, Parquet) and accessible through standard interfaces (REST, JDBC, S3-compatible).

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform designed to interoperate with data, logic, AI, workflow, and security systems.

extractedEntities.name: Multimodal Data Plane
extractedEntities.description: MMDP - infrastructure enabling integration with enterprise data platforms including Virtual Tables for avoiding data duplication and fully orchestrated pushdown compute.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that can seamlessly work with existing compute investments via MMDP pushdown compute.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK enabling programmatic access to Ontology elements through REST APIs and JSON-driven authoring paradigms.

extractedEntities.name: Platform SDK
extractedEntities.description: SDK providing access to dataset metadata and all metadata accessible across Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Tables
extractedEntities.description: Framework for leveraging existing data assets within external data platforms (Databricks, Snowflake, BigQuery) without data duplication.

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Interoperability
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology pushes beyond traditional semantic definitions to include granular definitions for objects, links, actions, and functions accessible through REST APIs.

extractedEntities.name: Open Data Standards
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where all data is stored in original format and transformed data is accessible in open formats like Apache Iceberg and Apache Parquet by default.

extractedEntities.name: Pushdown Compute
extractedEntities.description: MMDP capability where compute is pushed down to existing external data platforms rather than moving data into Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: S3-Compatible Access
extractedEntities.description: Standard interface for accessing Foundry data through S3-compatible protocols.

extractedEntities.name: JDBC Access
extractedEntities.description: Standard interface for accessing Foundry data through JDBC connections.

keyInsights: Foundry is built on open data standards: all data stored in original format (CSV, Iceberg, Parquet) and accessible through REST, JDBC, and S3-compatible interfaces.
The Multimodal Data Plane (MMDP) enables zero-copy integration with external platforms like Databricks, Snowflake, and BigQuery through Virtual Tables.
Pushdown compute allows Pipeline Builder to execute transformations on external compute infrastructure rather than requiring data movement into Foundry.
The Ontology provides semantic interoperability through REST APIs and JSON-driven authoring, enabling bidirectional synchronization with external semantic modeling tools.
Four interoperability dimensions exist: data (open formats), metadata (catalogs and lineage), semantic (Ontology APIs), and operational (workflow orchestration).
All Ontology elements are accessible programmatically through REST APIs, enabling deep integration with external enterprise systems.

title: Resource Guides - Streaming - Palantir
summary: Resource guide for streaming data integration in Foundry, covering architecture decisions, best practices, and patterns for implementing streaming data pipelines from ingestion through to Ontology availability.
relevantParts: Best practices for streaming data architecture

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application supporting streaming syncs from external streaming platforms into Foundry streaming datasets via agent architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool used to transform live streaming data, producing streaming dataset outputs usable in real time throughout Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform supporting end-to-end streaming workflows from ingestion through transformation to Ontology integration.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Stream
extractedEntities.description: Streaming dataset type in Foundry that enables low-latency, high-throughput data flow for real-time decision-making.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Proxy
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism for pushing records from an external stream directly into a Foundry stream, as an alternative to pull-based Data Connection syncs.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Sync
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection capability that pulls records from external streaming platforms into Foundry using unidirectional connections via the agent architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Stream Push
extractedEntities.description: Alternative ingestion method where records are pushed directly from external sources into a Foundry stream via the stream proxy.

extractedEntities.name: Flink in Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Apache Flink-based streaming compute engine used by Foundry for processing streaming data transformations.

extractedEntities.name: End-to-End Streaming Workflow
extractedEntities.description: Complete streaming pattern from data source connection through transformation and monitoring to Ontology integration.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Keys
extractedEntities.description: Key concepts for partitioning and ordering streaming data within Foundry streaming pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Stateful Transforms
extractedEntities.description: Streaming transformation capability that maintains state across streaming records for complex event processing.

keyInsights: Foundry supports two streaming ingestion methods: pull-based via Data Connection agent architecture, and push-based via the stream proxy.
Foundry connects to a wide range of streaming platforms including Apache Kafka, Amazon Kinesis, Amazon SQS/SNS, Google Pub/Sub, IBM MQ, RabbitMQ, MQTT, and Solace.
Pipeline Builder transforms streaming data outputs into streaming datasets usable in real time throughout Foundry, including Ontology integration.
The end-to-end streaming workflow follows five phases: core concepts, overview/architecture, data source connection, transformation, and monitoring.
Apache Flink is the underlying streaming compute engine in Foundry, supporting stateful transforms and streaming keys for complex event processing.
Streaming profiles and compute usage management are important operational considerations for production streaming deployments.

Supports three repository types: Transforms (Python/Java/SQL data transformations), Functions (TypeScript/Python business logic with Ontology access), and Model development. Features IntelliSense, linting, and Ontology-aware autocomplete.
relevantParts: Impact analysis for code change management
Web-based IDE architecture with Git integration
Three repository types: Transforms, Functions, Model

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Aware Autocomplete

extractedEntities.name: Functions Preview
