keyInsights: Function Interfaces are the key abstraction enabling BYOM (Bring Your Own Model): any LLM that implements the ChatCompletion interface becomes usable across AIP Logic, Pipeline Builder, Agent Studio, and Workshop.

The BYOM architecture uses a three-layer approach: Data Connection source defines the external endpoint, a webhook wraps the API call securely, and a TypeScript function implements the ChatCompletion interface.
Custom LLMs can be hosted on-premise, in the customer's own cloud, or fine-tuned on another platform -- the Function Interface abstraction decouples model location from platform consumption.

Two integration methods exist: webhook-based (TypeScript calls webhook directly) and direct source (REST API source in Data Connection with fetch calls), both producing the same ChatCompletion-compatible function.
Network policies and bearer token authentication in Data Connection ensure that all external LLM API calls are explicitly authorized and secured at the platform level.

keyInsights: Language models are integrated into functions via auto-generated strongly-typed code bindings (e.g., the Gpt41 class with typed methods), providing compile-time safety for LLM interactions.

Three distinct API methods are generated per model: createChatCompletion (text), createChatVisionCompletion (multimodal with images), and createChatCompletionStreamed (streaming), covering the major LLM interaction modes.
The import mechanism uses a Model Imports side panel that generates code bindings automatically via task runner, abstracting away the complexity of model endpoint management.
Functions using language models follow a system prompt + user prompt pattern, with the function wrapping the LLM call and returning structured output that the Ontology can consume.
Both TypeScript and Python are supported for authoring LLM-powered functions, with the same model import and code generation workflow available in both languages.

keyInsights: AIP Logic functions are composed of blocks that chain together, with each block taking input, producing output, and performing a discrete operation -- creating a visual programming model for AI workflows.

LLMs in AIP Logic do not have direct access to tools; they can only request tool use, and AIP Logic executes those tool calls within the invoking user's permissions -- enforcing the security boundary.

The tool taxonomy in AIP Logic has four categories: Apply Actions (write to Ontology), Call Function (execute logic), Query Objects (read from Ontology), and Calculator (compute) -- covering CRUD + compute.
The k-LLM philosophy means AIP Logic supports any available LLM in the platform, avoiding vendor lock-in and enabling model selection per block based on task requirements.
Block types beyond LLM include loops, conditionals, apply action, execute function, and create variable -- making AIP Logic a full visual programming environment, not just an LLM wrapper.

Trigger-based automation for object creation events

keyInsights: AIP Logic + Automate creates event-driven AI: Automate monitors an Object Set, triggers AIP Logic per new object, and the AI either auto-applies or stages Actions for human review.
The human-in-the-loop pattern stages AI-generated Actions as proposals with a 24-hour review window, enabling human validation before Ontology changes are committed.
Agent Decision Logs provide full LLM reasoning transparency, allowing reviewers to inspect why the AI proposed a specific Action before approving it.
Two automation modes exist: automatic execution (AI applies Ontology edits directly) and staged proposals (AI suggests edits for human approval), configurable per automation.
AIP Logic functions must output an Ontology edit (Action) and accept an object as input for the Automate integration to work, establishing a clear contract between AI and automation.

keyInsights: Palantir's responsible AI framework has eight concrete themes: equitable, robust, interpretable, privacy-preserving, safe, secure, accountable, and aligned -- each with specific platform capabilities.

Bias detection operates at two levels: data-level (Sensitive Data Scanner, Data Health Monitoring) and model-level (subset evaluation, AIP Evals), providing defense-in-depth against unfair discrimination.
Responsible AI is positioned as integral to system design rather than a compliance afterthought, with capabilities embedded throughout the development lifecycle rather than bolted on.
AIP Evals serves double duty: it validates functional correctness of AI logic and enables ethical evaluation by testing across diverse demographic groups and input variations.
The implementation workflow progresses from data-level fairness assessment to model-level bias testing to mitigation strategies, creating a systematic process for ethical AI development.

extractedEntities.description: Security controls governing every aspect of the AI system including what LLMs can access, how they interact with data, and what actions they can take.

extractedEntities.name: No Data Retention by Third Parties

extractedEntities.name: No Model Retraining on Customer Data
extractedEntities.description: Guarantee that no customer data submitted in prompts or completions is used for retraining third-party-hosted models.

extractedEntities.name: Least Privilege for LLMs
extractedEntities.description: Agent Studio principle where LLMs are granted access only to the specific Ontology resources, tools, and data necessary for their assigned task.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform whose security infrastructure (access controls, encryption, auditing) is inherited by AIP as the foundation for AI security.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity protected by AIP's security measures including access controls that govern what LLMs can query and modify.

extractedRelationships.source: No Data Retention by Third Parties
No Model Retraining on Customer Data

keyInsights: AIP security is built on inheritance: all of Foundry's existing access controls, encryption, and auditing carry forward to AI interactions, rather than building a separate security model.
Third-party LLM providers have zero access to customer data -- no retention, no retraining, no personnel access -- enforced through both technical and contractual guarantees.
Agent Studio enforces least privilege for LLMs: each agent is configured with access only to the specific Object Types, properties, and actions needed for its task.
AIP supports private model deployments for customers who want retraining, with full governance tools for auditing, interrogating, and monitoring model performance -- separate from third-party models.
Geographic restrictions and regional endpoints help satisfy data residency requirements, with specific regions (US, UK, EU) available depending on model provider capabilities.

Lowe's exemplifies the partnership's impact: moving from weekly single-node supply chain optimization to continuous dynamic optimization at global scale.
NVIDIA NeMo Retriever is available through multiple Foundry tools (Pipeline Builder, Code Workspaces, Ontology Toolchain), enabling embedding-based workflows across development modalities.

Legacy mode (workshop-configured agents) is being sunset in favor of Agent Studio-configured agents, which provide versioning, metrics, session history, and a dedicated configuration UI.

title: The Context Advantage: How Palantir AIP Operates the Modern Enterprise - Towards AI
summary: Third-party analysis of how AIP uses context advantage to operate enterprises. Compares AIP's Ontology-grounded approach to ChatGPT's general approach, explaining how AIP uses the Ontology like ChatGPT uses training data but grounded in enterprise-specific reality for accurate, actionable insights.
relevantParts: Context advantage concept for enterprise AI

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform that gains its competitive advantage through Ontology-grounded context rather than model size, enabling accurate enterprise reasoning by passing structured Ontology objects as variables to LLMs.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that carries meaning, relationships, and constraints; when passed to AIP, it provides the structured context that grounds LLM reasoning in enterprise reality.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology relationship connecting objects (e.g., asset to events, shipment to orders) that provides the relational context AIP uses for reasoning.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object attribute carrying data values that form the factual grounding for AIP reasoning, replacing the need for LLMs to infer context from text alone.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Grounded AI Reasoning
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where Ontology objects are passed to AIP Logic as variables that define what the model should consider, keeping reasoning focused, reliable, and grounded in structured enterprise data.

extractedEntities.name: Context Advantage
extractedEntities.description: Architectural principle that AI effectiveness comes not from model capability but from the quality and structure of context provided to the model, with the Ontology serving as the context source.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology computation where AIP Logic reasons within boundaries defined by passed Ontology objects, producing recommendations grounded in structured relationships.

extractedEntities.name: Supply Chain Shortage Resolution
extractedEntities.description: Use case where AIP receives shortage part objects, affected production orders, and supplier performance history to reason about and recommend alternative suppliers.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform where the Ontology lives, structuring data into objects with meaning, relationships, and constraints that provide the grounding layer for AIP.

keyInsights: The 'context advantage' thesis: enterprise AI effectiveness depends not on model size or speed but on the quality of structured context provided to the model -- the Ontology is that context layer.
Ontology objects passed to AIP Logic function as variables analogous to ChatGPT conversation context, but grounded in structured, governed enterprise data with relationships and constraints attached.
AIP does not scan the entire Ontology; it reasons within explicit boundaries defined by the objects passed as inputs, keeping reasoning focused and computationally tractable.
The Ontology provides three layers of context: meaning (what the object represents), relationships (how it connects to other objects), and constraints (what limits or dependencies apply).

The supply chain use case demonstrates that AIP's value comes from passing structured objects (shortage part, production orders, supplier performance) rather than requiring the LLM to infer these relationships from text.

title: Palantir AIP: The Next-Generation Enterprise AI System - From Semantic Ontology to Autonomous Operations - AI Monks (Medium)
summary: In-depth Medium analysis of Palantir AIP covering the progression from semantic Ontology to autonomous operations. Discusses how the Ontology serves as the semantic layer, kinetic layer, and dynamic layer that together enable AI-driven autonomous enterprise operations.
relevantParts: Three-layer Ontology model: semantic, kinetic, dynamic
Progression from data representation to autonomous operations
AI-driven enterprise autonomy via the Ontology

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform described as a next-generation enterprise AI system that progresses from semantic Ontology through kinetic operations to autonomous enterprise operations.

extractedEntities.name: Semantic Layer
extractedEntities.description: The foundational Ontology layer where data is structured into meaningful objects with relationships, serving as the knowledge representation that AI systems reason over.

extractedEntities.name: Kinetic Layer
extractedEntities.description: The operational layer where AI reasoning translates into real-world actions through Action Types, enabling the Ontology to drive actual enterprise operations beyond passive data modeling.

extractedEntities.name: Dynamic Layer
extractedEntities.description: The autonomous operations layer where AI agents continuously process events, make decisions, and execute actions with minimal human intervention, building on semantic and kinetic layers.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity forming the semantic layer's building blocks, carrying meaning and relationships that AI uses for reasoning.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attributes that encode the factual knowledge in the semantic layer.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology relationships that create the semantic graph connecting Object Types in the semantic layer.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology write mechanism forming the kinetic layer, translating AI decisions into real-world enterprise operations.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Executable logic that bridges the semantic and kinetic layers, enabling AI-driven reasoning to produce operational outputs.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform providing the data foundations and processing pipelines that underpin all three layers of the AIP architecture.

keyInsights: The Ontology serves three distinct architectural layers in AIP: semantic (knowledge representation), kinetic (operational action), and dynamic (autonomous processing) -- forming a progression from understanding to action.

Most enterprise AI deployments fail because LLMs alone lack understanding; the Ontology provides the structured understanding layer that transforms pattern-matching into mission-critical infrastructure.
The semantic layer (Object Types, Properties, Link Types) is necessary but not sufficient; the kinetic layer (Action Types) is what makes AI operational, and the dynamic layer makes it autonomous.
The progression from semantic to kinetic to dynamic mirrors the enterprise AI maturity model: first model the business, then enable AI-driven actions, then automate the decision-action loop.
The analysis positions AIP as fundamentally different from model-centric approaches because it invests in structured context (Ontology) rather than relying solely on model capability.

keyInsights: The data flow for LLM success is: Data sources -> Ontology -> Knowledge Graph -> LLM implementation; the ontology is the critical middle layer
Palantir's key differentiator is crafting custom ontologies per client with governance built into the knowledge structures, unlike competitors like Databricks
An ontology enables the shift from zero-shot to few-shot learning for enterprise LLMs by providing structured, labeled domain data
Palantir can mask underlying data while revealing metadata, allowing LLMs to reason about data structure without accessing sensitive content
Without an ontology, applying a general LLM to enterprise data is like an apprentice learning from mentors who all use different terminology

Best practice is to organize custom content into discrete sections with one topic per heading, avoiding long multi-topic paragraphs that dilute retrieval relevance.
Custom content must not overlap with Palantir public documentation; common terms should be clearly disambiguated within their context to avoid retrieval collisions.
Deprecated or overridden content should be removed or updated at its source rather than annotated with deprecation notices, which confuse the RAG retrieval mechanism.

Few-shot examples in the system prompt significantly enhance LLM performance by making tasks more comprehensible.

title: Purpose-based Access Controls at Palantir (Palantir Explained, #2)
summary: Palantir's official blog post explaining Purpose-Based Access Control (PBAC), where users apply for access to a Purpose rather than individual datasets. Data governance teams scope data to purposes and must record rationale for every access decision, enabling auditors to understand not just who has access but why.
relevantParts: Defines PBAC as the core governance model where access is granted to purposes, not individuals
Explains rationale recording requirements for governance decisions
Describes how purpose owners strengthen accountability and prevent ungoverned dark areas

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry access control mechanism where users apply for access to a Purpose (a scoped collection of data for a specific goal) rather than individual datasets, with mandatory rationale recording for all access decisions.

extractedEntities.name: Purpose
extractedEntities.description: A data governance construct that scopes datasets to a specific user goal, ensuring users receive access to exactly the data needed for their purpose -- no more, no less.

extractedEntities.name: Access Rationale
extractedEntities.description: A mandatory justification recorded when granting access, capturing why a user was given access to data and why data was assigned to a Purpose, enabling audit transparency.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform that implements Purpose-Based Access Controls as a core governance mechanism integrated into the underlying access control system.

extractedEntities.name: Data Governance

extractedEntities.name: Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology operations whose data access is governed by Purpose-based scope.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Trail
extractedEntities.description: The complete record of access decisions including who has access, what data they can see, and the rationale for each decision, available to auditors at any point.

extractedEntities.name: COVID-19 Healthcare Data Governance
extractedEntities.description: A use case where healthcare organizations needed to rapidly bring together data from testing, care homes, and hospitals while maintaining strict access controls for thousands of users.

keyInsights: PBAC inverts the traditional access model: instead of requesting access to individual datasets, users apply for access to a Purpose that contains only the data scoped for their specific goal.
Mandatory rationale recording at both the user-access and data-scoping level creates a complete audit chain explaining not just who has access, but why they were given it.
PBAC is designed for non-technical data governance teams, providing intuitive tooling to enforce access rules without relying on technical colleagues.
The system scales with organizational complexity: as datasets and users grow, Purposes prevent exponential growth of individual access decisions.
PBAC addresses the fundamental governance question of necessity and proportionality -- data governance teams must continually justify that access is strictly necessary for the stated purpose.

title: Overview - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: The main security overview page for Palantir Foundry. extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Controls

extractedEntities.name: Discretionary Controls

extractedEntities.description: Mandatory authentication requirement for all Palantir managed SaaS platform customers, part of the high minimum security bar.

extractedEntities.name: Provenance and Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's capability to track the origin and transformation path of data, used as the propagation mechanism for mandatory security controls.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology property that can be shared across Object Types, requiring its own security consideration in the platform's permission model.

keyInsights: Palantir's security model combines mandatory controls (propagating via data lineage) and discretionary controls (role-based, per-resource), creating a layered defense model.
Mandatory controls follow data as it moves through the platform via provenance and lineage tracking, ensuring security is never lost through data transformations.
The platform supports granular row-level and column-level security based on user attributes, enabling fine-grained data access within shared resources.
Projects serve as the fundamental organizational unit for security, with data, resources, users, and groups all organized and permissioned through Projects.
Organizations enforce strict silos between groups of users and resources as a form of mandatory control, preventing cross-organizational data leakage.
Multi-factor authentication is mandatory (not optional) for all managed SaaS customers, reflecting Palantir's opinionated high-security-minimum approach.

title: Concepts - Classification-based Access Controls - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Classification-Based Access Controls (CBAC), mandatory controls for protecting sensitive government information. CBAC markings restrict access hierarchically (e.g., Secret or below). Classification markings are configured in categories, and a classification can comprise markings from different categories.
relevantParts: Defines CBAC as hierarchical classification markings for government data
Classifications can be composed of markings from multiple categories
Mandatory control that restricts access based on user classification level

extractedEntities.name: Classification-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory controls for protecting sensitive government information using hierarchical classification markings (e.g., Secret, Top Secret) that restrict access based on a user's clearance level.

extractedEntities.name: Classification Marking
extractedEntities.description: A security label from a configured category that restricts access hierarchically, supporting both conjunctive (AND) and disjunctive (OR) behavior across categories.

extractedEntities.name: Marking Category
extractedEntities.description: A grouping of classification markings that defines either the type of data or how it should be disseminated, configured with conjunctive or disjunctive behavior.

extractedEntities.name: Hierarchical Access
extractedEntities.description: A classification model where higher clearance levels automatically include access to lower levels (e.g., Top Secret users can access Secret data).

extractedEntities.name: Disjunctive Marking
extractedEntities.description: A classification marking behavior where satisfying membership in any one of multiple groups (OR logic) grants access, commonly used for releasability between organizations or countries.

extractedEntities.name: Conjunctive Marking
extractedEntities.description: A classification marking behavior where a user must have all markings from a category (AND logic) to access classified data.

extractedEntities.name: Project Classification
extractedEntities.description: A required classification setting on Projects in CBAC-enabled environments that ensures all organizational containers have an explicit security classification.

extractedEntities.name: Data Classification
extractedEntities.description: A required classification on all datasets in CBAC-enabled environments, with raw datasets requiring explicit file classification.

extractedEntities.name: Project
extractedEntities.description: The organizational unit that is required to have a Project Classification set in CBAC-enabled environments.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that supports CBAC as an optional mandatory control layer, requiring Palantir involvement for configuration.

keyInsights: CBAC provides hierarchical security clearance modeling: Top Secret access automatically includes Secret and below, matching real-world government classification structures.
Classification markings support both conjunctive (must have ALL markings) and disjunctive (must have ANY ONE marking) behavior, enabling complex multi-national releasability rules.
In CBAC-enabled environments, classifications are ubiquitous: all Projects must have a Project Classification and all datasets must have a Data Classification, leaving no gaps.
CBAC markings are mandatory controls that combine with other access requirements (discretionary roles, mandatory markings), creating a multi-layered security model.
Configuration of classification markings requires Palantir involvement, reflecting the high-stakes nature of government classification and the need for correct initial setup.

title: Object permissioning - Ontology permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Ontology permissions, the central permissioning model for Ontology resources. Roles grant access at the Ontology level or individual resource level. Project-based permissions allow ontology resources (object types, action types, link types, interfaces, shared properties) to inherit permissions from their containing project.
relevantParts: Roles are the central permissioning model for the Ontology
Project-based permissions let ontology resources inherit from containing projects
Separates metadata permissions (Ontology Editor) from data permissions (datasource access)

extractedEntities.name: Project-Based Permissions
extractedEntities.description: A unified permission model where Ontology resources (object types, action types, link types, interfaces, shared properties) inherit permissions from their containing Compass project.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Permissions
extractedEntities.description: The central permissioning model for Ontology resources, managed through Compass (the platform filesystem), replacing legacy ontology roles and datasource-derived permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Compass
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform filesystem that manages permissions for all resource types including Ontology resources, providing a unified permission interface.

extractedEntities.name: Role
extractedEntities.description: A set of permissions (Viewer, Editor, Owner) assigned to users on Compass projects that determine their access to contained Ontology resources.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology resource that is now saved as a file in a Compass project, with view/edit/manage permissions determined by the user's project role.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology resource requiring edit permissions on both the link type itself and the linked object types for modification.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology resource requiring edit permissions on both the action type and all ontology resource types it edits.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology abstraction that inherits permissions from its containing Compass project under the project-based permissions model.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: An Ontology property shared across Object Types that inherits permissions from its containing Compass project.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type vs Object Permissions
extractedEntities.description: A key distinction: viewing an Object Type (schema) requires View on the object type only, but viewing actual Objects (data) requires View on both the object type AND the backing datasource.

extractedEntities.name: Datasource Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Separate permissions on the backing data store of an Object Type, required in addition to Object Type permissions to view actual object instances.

keyInsights: Object Type permissions and Object permissions are fundamentally different: viewing schema requires only Object Type access, but viewing actual data requires both Object Type AND datasource access.
Project-based permissions unify Ontology resource permissioning with the rest of the platform through Compass, eliminating the need for separate ontology-specific permission systems.
Link type edits require permissions on both the link type and all linked object types, while action type edits require permissions on the action type and all affected resource types.
The new model replaces two legacy systems (ontology roles and datasource-derived permissions) with a single unified approach based on Compass projects.
Ontology resources gain access to Compass features like folders, access requests, markings, and tags by becoming project-based resources, enriching governance capabilities.
Bulk permission management is enabled by setting permissions at the folder or project level, eliminating the need to permission individual ontology items.

title: Object permissioning - Object security policies - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on object and property security policies that achieve cell-level permissions. Object security policies control visibility of object instances (row-level), while property security policies control visibility of specific property values (column-level). These are independent of backing datasource permissions.
relevantParts: Object security policies = row-level security for object instances
Property security policies = column-level security for specific properties
Policies are independent of backing datasource permissions

extractedEntities.name: Object Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Security policy that controls visibility of object instances (row-level security) independently of backing data source permissions. Users must pass the object security policy to view any object instance.

extractedEntities.name: Property Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Security policy that controls visibility of specific property values (column-level security). Identical to object security policies except they apply only to a selection of properties.

extractedEntities.name: Cell-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: The combination of object security policies (row-level) and property security policies (column-level) that achieves cell-level permissions. A user must pass both policies to view a specific property value on a specific object instance.

extractedEntities.name: Row-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security achieved through object security policies that control which object instances a user can see, independent of backing data source permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Column-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security achieved through property security policies that control which property values a user can see, showing null for guarded properties when the user lacks the required policy.

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Control Property
extractedEntities.description: A property on an object type that contains markings (e.g., VIP marking) that must be satisfied by a user to access that specific object instance. Used in granular policies for row-level access.

extractedEntities.name: Granular Policy
extractedEntities.description: A fine-grained access control policy within object security policies that limits access based on mandatory control properties, enabling per-object-instance access decisions.

extractedEntities.name: Marking
extractedEntities.description: A mandatory access control mechanism (e.g., PII, VIP) that can be applied to projects and resources. Users must hold the required markings to access protected content.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A schema definition in the Foundry Ontology representing a class of real-world entities with defined properties, links, and security policies.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: A named attribute on an object type that holds a value for each object instance. Individual properties can be independently secured via property security policies.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data integration and analytics platform that hosts the Ontology and its security controls.

keyInsights: Object security policies decouple object-level permissions from backing datasource permissions, meaning users do not need Viewer access on the underlying dataset to see object instances.

Row-level, column-level, and cell-level security are layered: object security policies provide row-level, property security policies provide column-level, and their combination achieves cell-level granularity.
When a user fails a property security policy but passes the object security policy, they see null values instead of being denied the entire object instance.
Granular policies use mandatory control properties to encode per-row access requirements (e.g., a column of markings), enabling dynamic row-level security without separate ACLs per object.

Object security policies override data source permissions, allowing organizations to stop inheriting sensitive markings (like PII/VIP) from the backing dataset while still controlling access at the ontology layer.

title: Concepts - Projects and roles - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Projects and Roles as the foundation of discretionary access control. Projects are the primary security boundary, and roles are sets of operations granted on resources and their children. Custom roles can be created, and role sets are applied at the space level.
relevantParts: Projects are the primary security boundary in Foundry
Roles are sets of operations granted on resources and inherited by children
Custom roles can be created for different user types

extractedEntities.name: Project
extractedEntities.description: The primary security boundary in Foundry that organizes users, files, and folders for a particular purpose. Projects enforce security boundaries and serve as collaborative spaces where work and its output coexist.

extractedEntities.name: Role
extractedEntities.description: A set of operations that can be granted on resources and their children within Foundry. Roles define what users can do (Viewer, Editor, Owner, Discoverer) on Project contents.

extractedEntities.name: Discretionary Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control model in Foundry managed through Projects and Roles, where resource owners grant varying levels of access to users and groups.

extractedEntities.name: Reference
extractedEntities.description: A mechanism that allows work in one Project to reference and build upon work in other Projects, enabling cross-Project data reuse while respecting security boundaries.

extractedEntities.name: Role Set
extractedEntities.description: A collection of roles applied at the space level that defines the available permissions within that space. Custom roles can be created within role sets.

extractedEntities.name: Discoverer Role
extractedEntities.description: A minimal role in Foundry that allows a user to see that a Project exists and request additional access, without granting content visibility.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A schema definition in the Foundry Ontology that is governed by Project-level and role-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform where Projects and Roles form the foundation of discretionary access control.

extractedEntities.name: Group-Based Permission Management
extractedEntities.description: Recommended pattern of granting roles to groups rather than individual users at the Project level, reducing clutter and enabling uniform access management.

keyInsights: Projects are both the primary organizational unit and the primary security boundary in Foundry — work and its output must live in the same Project.
Roles are inherited: Project-level role grants propagate to all contents within the Project, providing uniform access without per-resource configuration.
Cross-Project data reuse requires References, enforcing that security boundaries are explicitly bridged rather than implicitly bypassed.
Foundry recommends group-based permission management at the Project level rather than individual user grants for maintainability.
The Discoverer role enables a zero-knowledge discoverability model where users can see that a Project exists and request access without seeing any content.
Custom roles can be created within Role Sets at the space level, allowing organizations to tailor permission granularity beyond the built-in Viewer/Editor/Owner roles.

title: Security auditing - Audit logs - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Foundry's comprehensive audit logging system. Audit logs capture who performed an action (identity), what the action was (categorized), when it happened (timestamps), and where (resources/systems). Logs are delivered in audit.2 and audit.3 schemas with append-only infrastructure for integrity.
relevantParts: Comprehensive audit trail answering who/what/when/where for every action
Append-only infrastructure ensures audit trail integrity
External SIEM integration via audit API with 15-minute availability

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log
extractedEntities.description: A comprehensive, append-only record of every action taken in Foundry capturing who (identity), what (action type/category), when (timestamp), and where (resource/system). Used for threat detection, incident investigation, and compliance.

extractedEntities.name: Audit.3 Schema
extractedEntities.description: The new and recommended audit log schema that provides low-latency delivery (~15 minutes), improved structure, and is available via API for SIEM integration or export to Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Audit.2 Schema
extractedEntities.description: The legacy audit log schema being migrated away from in favor of audit.3. Existing analyses should be transitioned to audit.3.

extractedEntities.name: SIEM Integration
extractedEntities.description: Integration mechanism for delivering Foundry audit logs to external Security Information and Event Management solutions for security monitoring and analysis.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log Category
extractedEntities.description: Classification of audit log entries by action type, enabling category-based query patterns for security analysis.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that generates comprehensive audit logs for all user and service account actions.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Delivery Method
extractedEntities.description: Flexible mechanisms for delivering audit logs including API-based delivery for SIEM integration and export to Foundry for in-platform analysis.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing a type of action that is captured and categorized in audit logs.

keyInsights: Foundry audit logs are append-only infrastructure, ensuring integrity and non-repudiation for compliance and security investigations.
Audit.3 schema delivers logs within ~15 minutes of event occurrence, a significant improvement over the legacy audit.2 schema for timely threat detection.
Audit logs capture identity (user/session/service account), action (type and intent), timestamp, and resource context — the four pillars of security investigation.
Audit log contents are themselves considered sensitive (containing PII like names and email addresses) and should be restricted to qualified security personnel.
Foundry can serve as its own lightweight SIEM when a dedicated external SIEM is unavailable, enabling in-platform security monitoring workflows.

title: Concepts - Organizations and spaces - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on Organizations as mandatory access controls that enforce strict silos between groups of users and resources. Users belong to one organization but can guest-access others. Organizations are inherited via file hierarchy and direct dependencies, functioning as mandatory controls.
relevantParts: Organizations enforce strict data silos as mandatory controls
Users belong to one org with optional guest access to others
Organizations propagate via file hierarchy and data dependencies

extractedEntities.name: Organization
extractedEntities.description: A mandatory access control that enforces strict silos between groups of users and resources. Every user belongs to exactly one organization but can be a guest member of multiple organizations. Organizations are inherited via file hierarchy and direct dependencies.

extractedEntities.name: Space
extractedEntities.description: A high-level container of projects with one common ontology, restricted by an organization or set of organizations. Formerly called 'namespace'. Most organizations need only a single space.

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control model where restrictions are enforced by the system (not discretionary). Organizations and markings are both mandatory access controls in Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Cross-Organization Collaboration
extractedEntities.description: Mechanism enabling data sharing and collaboration between users from different organizations through dedicated multi-organization spaces with defined governance terms.

extractedEntities.name: Cross-Organization Discoverability
extractedEntities.description: Configurable rules that control whether users can see names, users, and groups of organizations outside their own, managed by platform administrators.

extractedEntities.name: Guest Membership
extractedEntities.description: A mechanism allowing a user who belongs to one organization to access resources in another organization without changing their primary organization membership.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that exists within a space and is governed by the organization restrictions applied to that space.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that implements organizations and spaces for multitenancy and data isolation.

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry administration tool used to manage organization permissions, create new organizations, and configure cross-organization collaboration.

extractedEntities.name: Multitenancy via Organizations
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern using organizations and spaces to achieve strict data isolation between tenants while enabling controlled collaboration through guest membership and multi-organization spaces.

keyInsights: Organizations are mandatory access controls (not discretionary), meaning they are system-enforced silos that cannot be bypassed by resource owners unlike role-based Project permissions.
Every user belongs to exactly one organization but can guest-access multiple others, maintaining a clear primary affiliation while enabling cross-organizational collaboration.
Spaces are the bridge between organizations and the ontology — each space has one common ontology and is restricted by one or more organizations.

Organizations differ from markings in scope: organizations protect spaces, ontologies, projects, users, groups, tag categories, and collections, while markings can only be applied to projects and resources.
The file path structure in Foundry reflects the space hierarchy: space/project/sub-folder/my-file, making organizational boundaries visible in the resource path.
Multi-organization spaces are the recommended approach for external collaboration, providing a dedicated isolated area rather than granting broad cross-organization access.

title: Third-party applications - Writing OAuth2 clients for Foundry - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on OAuth2 client implementation for Foundry, covering Authorization Code flow (user-context) and Client Credentials grant (service user). Refresh tokens rotate on each use, with reuse detection invalidating all tokens from the grant. Inactive tokens expire after 30 days.
relevantParts: OAuth2 Authorization Code flow for user-context access
Client Credentials grant for service user authentication
Refresh token rotation and reuse detection for compromised token mitigation

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Authorization Code Grant
extractedEntities.description: OAuth2 flow that allows third-party applications to act on behalf of existing Foundry users. Users explicitly grant the client access to requested permissions. Supports PKCE for public clients.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Client Credentials Grant
extractedEntities.description: OAuth2 flow for machine-to-machine authentication where the third-party application acts as a service user rather than on behalf of a human user.

extractedEntities.name: PKCE
extractedEntities.description: Proof Key for Code Exchange — a security extension for OAuth2 using code_verifier and code_challenge (SHA256 + Base64-URL). Required for public clients, recommended for confidential clients.

extractedEntities.name: Refresh Token Rotation
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism where refresh tokens are rotated on each use, and reuse detection invalidates all tokens from the grant. Inactive tokens expire after 30 days.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Scope
extractedEntities.description: Permission boundaries in Foundry's OAuth2 implementation that define what resources and operations a third-party application can access on behalf of a user.

extractedEntities.name: Authorization Endpoint
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry endpoint that initiates the OAuth2 authorization flow, accepting parameters like response_type, client_id, redirect_uri, scope, state, and PKCE challenge.

extractedEntities.name: Token Endpoint
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry endpoint that exchanges authorization codes for access tokens and refresh tokens, and handles token refresh requests.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that implements OAuth2 authorization server for third-party application integration.

extractedEntities.name: Third-Party Application Registration
extractedEntities.description: Process of registering an application in Foundry to obtain a client ID and configure OAuth2 settings before using OAuth2 flows.

extractedEntities.name: Service User
extractedEntities.description: A non-human identity used with the Client Credentials grant for machine-to-machine authentication with Foundry.

keyInsights: Foundry supports two OAuth2 grant types: Authorization Code (user-context with PKCE) and Client Credentials (service user for machine-to-machine), covering both interactive and automated integration scenarios.
Refresh tokens rotate on every use with reuse detection — if a refresh token is used twice, all tokens from that grant are invalidated, providing strong protection against token theft.
PKCE is required for public clients (native apps, SPAs) and recommended for confidential clients (server-based), using SHA256 + Base64-URL encoding for the code challenge.
Inactive refresh tokens expire after 30 days, enforcing a maximum idle session duration for third-party applications.
Foundry's OAuth2 scopes can be restricted to a limited set of resources or grant access to all resources a user can access, providing flexible permission boundaries for third-party apps.

title: Getting started - Requesting justification for sensitive actions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on configuring justification prompts for sensitive actions. Checkpoints interrupt users with prompts requiring justification before performing sensitive operations. Justification types include acknowledgment, free-text response, dropdown selection, and reauthentication.
relevantParts: Checkpoints interrupt sensitive actions to require justification
Four justification types: acknowledgment, response, dropdown, reauthentication
All justifications are saved for administrative review

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoint
extractedEntities.description: A governance mechanism that interrupts users with justification prompts before they can perform sensitive actions. Checkpoints enforce accountability by requiring users to provide a reason before proceeding.

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoint Type
extractedEntities.description: A classification of sensitive actions that can trigger checkpoint justification prompts. Each type of action has a corresponding checkpoint type.

extractedEntities.name: Justification Prompt
extractedEntities.description: The user-facing interface presented by a checkpoint, which can require acknowledgment, free-text response, dropdown selection, or reauthentication before allowing a sensitive action.

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoint Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Settings that define the parameters and conditions of a justification prompt, including what triggers it and what type of justification is required.

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints Application
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring, previewing, and managing checkpoint configurations that enforce justification requirements for sensitive actions.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that provides the Checkpoints governance mechanism for sensitive action justification.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing a type of action that can be governed by checkpoint justification requirements.

extractedEntities.name: Break-the-Glass
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern where checkpoints require explicit justification before performing sensitive actions, creating an auditable record of why access was needed.

keyInsights: Checkpoints are a governance mechanism, not an access control — they allow the action but require justification, creating accountability rather than blocking access entirely.
Four justification types are supported: acknowledgment (confirm awareness), free-text (explain reasoning), dropdown selection (choose predefined reason), and reauthentication (prove identity).
Checkpoint configurations are manageable through a dedicated Checkpoints Application with preview capabilities, allowing administrators to test the user experience before deployment.
Justification records are preserved for audit purposes, creating a chain of accountability linking sensitive actions to the reasons they were performed.

title: Cipher - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of Cipher, Foundry's data obfuscation service using cryptographic operations. Supports AES-GCM-SIV probabilistic encryption, AES-SIV deterministic encryption, SHA-256/512 hashing with pepper, and visual obfuscation for images. Managed through Channels and Licenses for key and permission management.
relevantParts: Multiple encryption algorithms: AES-GCM-SIV, AES-SIV, SHA-256/512
Visual obfuscation for image PII/PHI with reversible scrambling
Channels and Licenses model for key and permission management

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data obfuscation service that uses cryptographic operations (encryption, decryption, hashing) to protect sensitive data. Provides an additional privacy layer on top of Foundry's storage and network encryption.

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Channel
extractedEntities.description: Management construct that reliably manages cryptographic algorithms and keys used by Cipher for encryption, decryption, and hashing operations.

extractedEntities.name: Cipher License
extractedEntities.description: Permission and workflow control for applying cryptographic operations. Licenses allow users to control who can apply operations and to which workflows (e.g., bulk encrypt columns, decrypt individual values).

extractedEntities.name: AES-GCM-SIV Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Probabilistic encryption algorithm supported by Cipher for encrypting data where different ciphertexts are produced for the same plaintext.

extractedEntities.name: AES-SIV Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Deterministic encryption algorithm supported by Cipher where the same plaintext always produces the same ciphertext, enabling equality comparisons on encrypted data.

extractedEntities.name: SHA-256/512 Hashing with Pepper
extractedEntities.description: One-way hashing supported by Cipher using SHA-256 or SHA-512 with a pepper value, providing irreversible data obfuscation.

extractedEntities.name: Visual Obfuscation
extractedEntities.description: Cipher capability for obfuscating image data, extending cryptographic protection beyond text/numeric data to visual content.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Ontology attribute whose values can be protected by Cipher operations (encryption, hashing, obfuscation) at the column level.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that hosts Cipher as an additional privacy layer on top of storage and network encryption.

extractedEntities.name: Object Explorer
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application where Cipher licenses can enable decryption of individual values for authorized users.

keyInsights: Cipher provides application-layer encryption on top of Foundry's infrastructure-level encryption (storage and network), creating defense-in-depth for sensitive data like PII and PHI.
Cipher separates key management (Channels) from permission management (Licenses), enabling independent governance of cryptographic material and operational access.
Both probabilistic (AES-GCM-SIV) and deterministic (AES-SIV) encryption are supported, allowing tradeoffs between security strength and queryability on encrypted data.
Cipher is designed for non-technical users — Licenses enable users without specialized cryptography knowledge to deploy privacy-enhancing tools in legible and reliable ways.
Visual obfuscation extends Cipher beyond structured data to image-based data, covering use cases like face redaction or document anonymization.

title: Object permissioning - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Overview of object permissioning in the Foundry Ontology. Describes how granular, robust, and flexible security controls apply to all ontology entities including object types, link types, and action types. Security is built into the Ontology itself, so application builders need not account for security requirements separately.
relevantParts: Security is resident in the Ontology, not in individual applications
Covers object types, link types, action types, and their instances
Application builders get elevated security without explicit implementation

extractedEntities.name: Object Permissioning
extractedEntities.description: The security framework for the Foundry Ontology providing granular, robust, and flexible controls for all ontology entities. Security is built into the Ontology itself so application builders need not separately account for security.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Resource Permission
extractedEntities.description: Permissions on ontology schema-level resources (object types, link types, action types) that control access to the schema definitions themselves, not the underlying data values.

extractedEntities.name: Object and Link Permission
extractedEntities.description: Permissions on the actual data in the Ontology — the primary key values and property values of object instances and link instances.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource defining the schema (display name, property names, property data types, description) for a class of objects. Governed by ontology resource permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource defining connections between object types. Governed by ontology resource permissions alongside object types and action types.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource defining mutations that can be performed on the ontology. Governed by ontology resource permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform whose Ontology has built-in two-level authorization: schema-level (ontology resources) and data-level (objects and links).

extractedEntities.name: Embedded Security
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where security is built into the Ontology itself rather than being bolted on at the application layer, meaning application builders inherit security controls automatically.

keyInsights: Foundry's Ontology has a two-level authorization structure: schema-level (ontology resources like object types, link types, action types) and data-level (actual object instances and link instances with their values).
Object types, link types, and action types are ontology resources — their schema definitions (names, property types, descriptions) are permissioned separately from the actual data values.

Security is embedded in the Ontology itself, meaning application builders do not need to implement separate security logic — all applications consuming the Ontology automatically inherit its security controls.
The distinction between schema (ontology resources) and data (objects and links) means a user could have access to know an object type exists but not see any of its instances, or vice versa.

title: Palantir Privacy and Governance Whitepaper
summary: Official Palantir whitepaper covering privacy and governance architecture. Describes purpose-based access controls, data lineage tracking, Cipher encryption, Checkpoints justification, and the overall governance framework. Positions Palantir's security investments as competitive advantages for customer success.
relevantParts: Comprehensive treatment of privacy and governance across all features
Links PBAC, lineage, Cipher, and Checkpoints into unified framework
Official Palantir positioning on governance architecture

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Access control model where data access is governed by the stated purpose for which it is being accessed, rather than just identity or role. A key governance principle in Palantir's architecture.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage Tracking

keyInsights: Palantir's governance framework spans five pillars: purpose-based access control, data lineage tracking, Cipher encryption, Checkpoint justification, and comprehensive audit logging.
Purpose-based access control (PBAC) goes beyond role-based access by requiring a stated purpose for data access, enabling governance that aligns with regulatory requirements like GDPR.
Data lineage tracking provides full provenance and transformation history, enabling organizations to understand how data flows and is used across the platform.
Palantir positions security and governance investments as competitive advantages for customer success rather than compliance overhead.
The privacy architecture is designed for defense-in-depth with overlapping controls at infrastructure, platform, ontology, and application layers.

title: palantir/conjure - Strongly typed HTTP/JSON APIs for browsers and microservices
summary: Palantir's open-source toolchain for defining APIs once in human-readable YAML and generating client/server interfaces in multiple languages (Java, TypeScript, Python). Conjure compiles to a JSON-based intermediate representation (IR). Designed to be more opinionated than Swagger/OpenREST, supporting only a restricted subset of HTTP for consistency across Palantir's hundreds of microservices.
relevantParts: Used for Palantir's internal API security model
Contract compliance at the API layer
Core API definition framework used across Palantir's microservice architecture
YAML-to-IR compilation pipeline and multi-language code generation
Design philosophy of opinionated API constraints vs Swagger flexibility

extractedEntities.name: Conjure

extractedEntities.name: API-First Design
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where APIs are defined declaratively before implementation, with client and server code generated from the definition, ensuring consistency across languages.

extractedEntities.name: Intermediate Representation Compilation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of compiling a human-readable definition into a machine-readable IR that is consumed by multiple language-specific code generators, enabling cross-language consistency.

extractedEntities.name: RFC002
extractedEntities.description: Conjure specification that defines how all code generators behave as CLIs, ensuring consistent behavior across the generator ecosystem.

keyInsights: Conjure uses a two-stage compilation: human-readable YAML is compiled to a JSON-based IR, which is then consumed by language-specific generators — the IR is self-hosting (defined as Conjure types itself).
Conjure is deliberately more restrictive than Swagger/OpenREST, supporting only a subset of HTTP patterns to enforce consistency across Palantir's hundreds of microservice repositories.
The Conjure wire format dictates parameter encoding: primitive types as HTTP header/path/query parameters, complex objects as JSON body payload.
Conjure provides the RPC infrastructure for both internal Palantir services and third-party applications, from Java/Go/Python backends to TypeScript browser applications.
All Conjure generators conform to RFC002, a specification ensuring consistent CLI behavior across the generator ecosystem, enabling uniform build tool orchestration.

title: Building Software for a Zero Trust World - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Implementation

extractedEntities.name: Infrastructure-as-Code Security
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where CI enforcement validates authenticated endpoints and infrastructure-as-code configurations for security compliance

extractedEntities.name: NIST SP 800-207

keyInsights: Palantir's Zero Trust approach follows five DOD tenets: assume hostile environment, presume breach, never trust always verify, scrutinize explicitly, and apply unified analytics

Palantir platforms implement multiple Policy Enforcement Points between application components AND in the hosting infrastructure, creating layered security

title: Checkpoints - Overview - Palantir Foundry Documentation

Rich contextual history for administrators reviewing sensitive actions

Checkpoint records capture rich contextual data including timestamp, user, justification, and associated resources/Markings for complete audit trails
Checkpoints support both synchronous (user-initiated) and asynchronous (via Approvals) interaction governance
Configuration is highly granular: administrators can control who sees checkpoints, for which interaction types, with custom prompt language and justification requirements
The Checkpoints application serves dual purpose: configuration management for administrators and personal justification review for all users

title: Cipher - Core concepts - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Core concepts documentation for Cipher covering Channels (algorithm + key configuration), Licenses (permission grants for crypto operations), and the distinction between probabilistic and deterministic encryption. Deterministic encryption preserves join capability while probabilistic provides stronger security.
relevantParts: Channels define algorithm and key configuration
Licenses control who can perform which crypto operations

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's encryption and hashing service that provides column-level data encryption through Channels and Licenses, supporting both probabilistic and deterministic encryption modes

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Channel
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry filesystem resource that defines a specific protocol for obfuscating or de-obfuscating values, including encryption algorithm, key parameters, or hashing algorithm and secret

extractedEntities.name: Cipher License
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry filesystem resource that controls permissions to use cryptographic operations defined in a parent Cipher Channel, granting access to users who can view the License

extractedEntities.name: Cipher Encrypted Value
extractedEntities.description: Encrypted value format following the syntax CIPHER::<channel-rid>::<encrypted-value>::CIPHER, embedding metadata needed for decryption

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform that hosts Cipher as a security service within the filesystem workspace

extractedEntities.name: Deterministic Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Encryption mode where the same plaintext always produces the same ciphertext, preserving join capability across encrypted columns

extractedEntities.name: Probabilistic Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Encryption mode where the same plaintext produces different ciphertexts each time, providing stronger security but preventing joins on encrypted values

keyInsights: Cipher provides two distinct encryption modes: deterministic (preserves join capability) and probabilistic (stronger security), allowing administrators to trade off between data utility and protection level
The Channel-License model separates encryption configuration from access control: Channels define the cryptographic protocol while Licenses control who can use those operations
Cipher encrypted values are self-describing with the format CIPHER::<channel-rid>::<encrypted-value>::CIPHER, embedding enough metadata for the service to decrypt without external context
Licenses are standard Foundry filesystem resources that can be moved, shared, and permissioned like any other resource, leveraging existing access control infrastructure

title: Security glossary - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Comprehensive glossary of security terms used in Foundry. Defines key concepts including Access (visibility of resource existence), Mandatory Controls (all-or-nothing restrictions like markings and organizations), Discretionary Controls (capabilities via roles), and the relationship between them.
relevantParts: Formal definitions of Access, Mandatory Controls, Discretionary Controls
Distinction between access (knowing resource exists) and capabilities (roles)
Central reference for all Foundry security terminology

extractedEntities.name: Mandatory Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: All-or-nothing access restrictions in Foundry implemented through Markings and Organizations that cannot be overridden by discretionary grants

extractedEntities.name: Discretionary Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: Additive capabilities granted via Roles that expand what a user can do beyond their base access, cannot restrict permissions

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control mechanism representing a data classification; when applied to a resource, users without the marking are guaranteed to never access that data

extractedEntities.name: Organization
extractedEntities.description: A mandatory access control boundary that determines which users can access resources; resources can require membership in one or more organizations

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Sets of operations granting specific capabilities on resources. Four default roles exist: Owner, Editor, Viewer, and Discoverer

extractedEntities.name: Access Requirements
extractedEntities.description: The combination of Organizations and Markings that determine which users may access a Project or resource

extractedEntities.name: Groups
extractedEntities.description: Collections of users and/or other groups that can be internal (Foundry-defined) or external (from identity providers)

extractedEntities.name: Identity Provider
extractedEntities.description: System that validates users during login and provides information on users, groups, and attributes

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform with a dual security model combining mandatory and discretionary access controls

extractedEntities.name: Expand Access
extractedEntities.description: Any change in access requirements that broadens the potential audience, such as removing a Marking or adding Organizations when at least one is already applied

keyInsights: Foundry security uses a dual model: Mandatory controls (Markings + Organizations) determine IF a user can access a resource, while Discretionary controls (Roles) determine WHAT they can do with it
Discretionary controls are strictly additive -- they can only grant additional permissions, never restrict them, preventing accidental permission removal through role changes
Four default platform Roles exist (Owner, Editor, Viewer, Discoverer) that can be customized or extended by role administrators
Groups can be internal (Foundry-defined) or external (from identity providers like Active Directory), and internal groups can contain external groups, enabling hybrid access management
Access expansion events are tracked carefully: removing Markings always expands access, while Organization changes follow specific rules depending on whether organizations are already applied

title: How Palantir Meets IL6 Security Requirements with Apollo - Palantir Blog
summary: Blog post on how Apollo meets DoD IL6 security requirements. All artifacts are cryptographically signed with integrity validation. Apollo manages vulnerability detection, alerting teams and using severity criteria to gate deployments. Supports disconnected networks and air-gapped environments.
relevantParts: Cryptographic signing and integrity validation for all deployment artifacts
Vulnerability severity gates for deployment decisions
Support for air-gapped and disconnected network deployments

extractedEntities.name: Apollo

extractedEntities.name: IL6 Authorization
extractedEntities.description: DISA Impact Level 6 provisional authorization covering potentially classified data, the highest currently defined impact level for cloud services in the DoD

extractedEntities.name: Cryptographic Signing

extractedEntities.name: Vulnerability Management
extractedEntities.description: Apollo's capability to detect vulnerabilities, alert teams, and use severity criteria to gate deployments, preventing vulnerable code from reaching production

extractedEntities.name: Air-Gapped Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Deployment pattern for disconnected networks where Apollo manages software delivery without continuous internet connectivity

extractedEntities.name: FedRAMP
extractedEntities.description: Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program providing standardized security assessment for cloud service providers

extractedEntities.name: NIST Risk Management Framework
extractedEntities.description: Framework used by the DoD for security evaluations, providing guidance for identifying and assessing security controls in IT systems

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform that operates within IL6-authorized environments deployed and managed by Apollo

keyInsights: Apollo achieves 3.5-minute average patch/update deployment time, reducing production issue remediation from hours to under 5 minutes
Palantir received DISA IL6 provisional authorization in October 2022, the highest impact level covering potentially classified data
Apollo's design supports disconnected and air-gapped networks, critical for defense and intelligence environments where continuous internet connectivity is not available
All deployment artifacts are cryptographically signed for integrity validation, and vulnerability detection gates prevent deploying compromised code
IL6 is based on DISA's Cloud Computing SRG and NIST RMF, creating a standardized 'Do Once, Use Many' accreditation approach for government cloud services

title: Security - Authorization - Palantir Apollo Documentation
summary: Documentation on Apollo's authorization model. Apollo uses role-based access controls (RBAC) where Teams gain permissions based on assigned roles. Configuration and secrets are decoupled from infrastructure, enabling secure reapplication onto new systems.
relevantParts: Apollo uses RBAC with Teams and assigned roles
Secrets decoupled from infrastructure for secure management
Role-based operations control for deployment actions

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's deployment management platform using role-based access controls (RBAC) for authorization across resources like Release Channels, Products, and Environments

extractedEntities.name: RBAC
extractedEntities.description: Role-Based Access Control model used by Apollo where Teams gain permissions to operations based on assigned roles, grantable globally per resource type or per specific resource instance

extractedEntities.name: Apollo Roles
extractedEntities.description: Named permission sets in Apollo including Administrator, Contributor, Restricted Contributor, Viewer, Approver, and resource-specific role variants

extractedEntities.name: Release Channel
extractedEntities.description: Apollo resource representing a deployment channel (e.g., STAGING) with roles including Administrator, Contributor, Restricted Contributor, and Viewer

extractedEntities.name: Vulnerability Suppression Roles

keyInsights: Apollo RBAC supports both global grants (all resources of a type) and specific resource grants (e.g., only the STAGING Release Channel), providing flexible permission scoping
Roles are assigned to Teams rather than individual users, ensuring consistent permission management across groups
Release Channels have a nuanced contributor model: Contributor can add any Product Release, while Restricted Contributor can only add releases they own
Apollo separates configuration and secrets from infrastructure, enabling secure reapplication onto new systems without exposing sensitive deployment data
Each resource type in Apollo has its own role hierarchy: Release Channels, Products, Labels, Environments, and Vulnerability settings all have distinct role sets

title: Object permissioning - Managing object security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on managing object security including datasource-based and Ontology-based approaches. Object and property policies support cell-level security, and object security can be configured directly in the Ontology Manager view. Covers multi-datasource object types for column-level security.
relevantParts: Cell-level security through combined row and column policies
Column-wise MDOs enable column-level access controls

extractedEntities.name: Object Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-level policy governing visibility of object instances, enabling row-level security independent of backing data source permissions

extractedEntities.name: Property Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-level policy governing visibility of individual property values, enabling column-level security independent of backing data source permissions

extractedEntities.name: Cell-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Combined row and column level security in the Ontology achieved through object and property security policies working together

extractedEntities.name: Data Source Policy
extractedEntities.description: Legacy security approach where object type permissions are implicitly controlled by the permissions applied to the input data sources

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity (e.g., Passenger) whose instances and properties can be secured at row, column, and cell levels through security policies

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Attribute of an Object Type (e.g., Name, Address, Phone Number) that can be individually secured via property security policies

extractedEntities.name: Row-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism restricting visibility of entire object instances (rows) based on markings or other access controls

extractedEntities.name: Column-Level Security
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism restricting visibility of specific properties (columns) based on markings like PII

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool where object and property security policies can be directly configured on each object type

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access control labels (e.g., VIP, PII) applied to objects or properties to enforce row-level and column-level security

keyInsights: Object and property security policies are the recommended approach over data source policies, providing unified cell-level security directly on the object type rather than requiring complex multi-datasource configurations
Cell-level security is the combination of row-level (object instances) and column-level (properties) security, achieved through separate but complementary policy types
Object security policies are independent of backing data source permissions, decoupling Ontology access control from data infrastructure permissions

Media reference properties create a potential security gap: property visibility and referenced media set visibility are independently controlled, requiring administrators to align permissions across both
The Passenger example illustrates the canonical pattern: VIP marking for row-level security on certain passengers, PII marking for column-level security on Name/Address/Phone Number properties

title: Ontology Manager - Ontology roles migration - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on migrating from legacy datasource-derived permissions to Ontology roles. Describes how Ontology roles replaced the old authorization model, applying to object types, link types, and Action types as Ontology resources with dedicated Editor and Owner role types.
relevantParts: Migration path from datasource-derived permissions to Ontology roles
Ontology roles are the default authorization model for Ontology resources
Separate Ontology Editor role that controls metadata without data access

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Roles

extractedEntities.name: Datasource-Derived Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Legacy authorization model where Ontology resource permissions were implicitly derived from the permissions on input data sources

extractedEntities.name: Link Type

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology resource that can be permissioned via Ontology roles alongside object types and link types

extractedEntities.description: Role granting permission to edit Ontology resources and their metadata without requiring permissions on the underlying data source

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Owner Role
extractedEntities.description: Role with full control over an Ontology resource including the ability to manage permissions on that resource

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager

extractedEntities.name: Interface
