extractedEntities.name: Shared Property Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology feature requiring Ontology roles to be enabled, allowing property type definitions to be shared across object types

keyInsights: Ontology roles fundamentally decouple metadata permissions (who can edit the Object Type definition) from data permissions (who can see individual object instances), which remain governed by data source permissions
Ontology roles became the default authorization model for all new enrollments since September 2023, replacing datasource-derived permissions
Migration to Ontology roles is required to use newer Ontology features including shared property types and interfaces

After migration, all users with access to the Ontology gain view permission on all migrated resources (object types, link types, action types), making resource metadata universally visible within the Ontology
Migration can be performed incrementally -- there is no required ordering, and mixed permission models (some resources on old model, some on new) are supported during transition
The latest evolution moves permissioning to the Compass filesystem, making Ontology roles themselves a legacy approach being superseded

title: Functions - Language agnostic features - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on permissions for Functions in the Ontology. Functions run with specific permission contexts that determine what data they can access, ensuring that function execution respects the caller's access level and security policies.
relevantParts: Function access respects caller's security policies
Language-agnostic permission model for all function types

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology computation unit whose authoring and execution are subject to multiple permission layers including repository, Ontology entity, and caller permissions

extractedEntities.name: Function Execution Context
extractedEntities.description: Permission context under which a function runs, determining what data it can access based on caller identity and repository permissions

extractedEntities.name: Repository Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Project-level permissions granted to a functions repository that determine which Ontology entities and data sources are accessible during code generation and execution

extractedEntities.name: Caller Permissions

extractedEntities.name: Object Type

extractedEntities.name: Link Type

extractedEntities.name: Code Assist
extractedEntities.description: Foundry infrastructure for live preview function execution, subject to the same permission requirements as code generation in repositories

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Special data source import required for object types with row-level security enabled, used instead of direct dataset imports

extractedEntities.name: Function Binding
extractedEntities.description: Auto-generated code bindings for every object and link type loaded based on repository permissions during code generation

keyInsights: Functions operate under a three-layer permission model: repository permissions (what entities are available), Ontology entity permissions (what types are accessible), and caller permissions (who is running the function)
Repository permissions are distinct from user permissions and must be explicitly configured -- a user's personal access does not automatically transfer to their repository
Ontology entities (object types, link types) must be explicitly imported at the Project level, and changes in one repository's imports affect all repositories in the same Project
Published functions enforce caller permissions at execution time, ensuring the function can only access data the invoking user is authorized to see
Object types with row-level security require importing Restricted Views instead of direct datasets, adding an additional security layer for sensitive data in function contexts

Foundational concept for Palantir's security architecture

extractedEntities.name: Data Quality Management

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing comprehensive data provenance, lineage, and quality management capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Foundry entity whose data quality and provenance can be traced through the platform's lineage tracking capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Data Harmonization

keyInsights: Data trust in Foundry is built on three pillars: provenance (where data came from), lineage (how it was transformed), and audit trails (who did what)
Poor data quality can propagate insidiously through an organization -- a single bad dataset can compromise entire data-driven initiatives
Data quality must be paired with transparency: users with relevant roles and permissions should have full context necessary to assess and trust their data
Foundry addresses the fundamental challenge of harmonizing data from heterogeneous sources with differing standards, definitions, schemas, and formats into a single source of truth
The COVID-19 response demonstrated that data trust tenets of accountability and transparency are especially critical in crisis situations

title: Getting started - Protecting sensitive data - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Getting started guide for protecting sensitive data in Foundry. Provides a practical overview of combining markings, organizations, restricted views, Cipher, and Checkpoints to build a comprehensive data protection strategy for sensitive data types like PII and PHI.

Covers PII, PHI, and other sensitive data classifications
Starting point for implementing comprehensive data protection

extractedEntities.name: Cipher
extractedEntities.description: Foundry encryption service used as part of the comprehensive data protection strategy for sensitive data types

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints
extractedEntities.description: Governance tool used alongside markings for purpose justification when accessing sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attribute (e.g., date of birth) that may contain sensitive PII data requiring marking-based protection

extractedEntities.name: Sensitive Data Protection Strategy
extractedEntities.description: Combined pattern using markings, organizations, restricted views, Cipher, and Checkpoints to build comprehensive data protection for PII and PHI

keyInsights: Markings propagate automatically along data lineage, meaning applying a PII marking to a raw dataset will cascade to all downstream derived datasets
Marking simulation mode allows administrators to preview the full lineage impact of applying a marking before committing, preventing accidental lockouts of downstream users
The recommended strategy is to keep sensitive data in the pipeline as long as possible and remove sensitive columns only at the final Ontology output layer

Five complementary tools form the comprehensive data protection strategy: Markings (mandatory access), Organizations (boundary controls), Restricted Views (filtered access), Cipher (encryption), and Checkpoints (justification)
Marking categories provide organizational structure (e.g., 'Information' category) enabling scalable classification as new data types (PII, PHI, etc.) are identified

title: Management - Manage roles - Palantir Foundry Documentation

Group-based permission management reduces individual role clutter

Custom roles can include other roles as a base, enabling role composition -- the Merger role includes all Viewer operations plus additional merge-specific operations

Role sets enable Organization-level customization of permissions for different contexts (Projects, Ontology), allowing different organizations on the same enrollment to have different role definitions
The Supporter role pattern demonstrates how to give third-party support teams issue visibility without exposing dataset metadata, schemas, or other sensitive operational details

title: Use case patterns - Multi-organization ecosystems - Palantir Foundry Documentation

keyInsights: Foundry's multi-organization model enables ecosystem-level collaboration while maintaining strict data isolation -- each party controls their own space and governance with precise control over sharing
Organizations move beyond a central point of control: Foundry divides permissions such that every party independently manages their own data governance
Multiple ecosystem models are supported and often combined as the ecosystem grows, including Market Ecosystem and peer-to-peer sharing patterns
The pattern has proven delivery across multiple industries: aviation, automotive, finance, and pharmaceuticals, demonstrating cross-domain applicability
The fundamental value proposition is converting siloed organizations into collaborative ecosystems, reducing lost resources, accelerating innovation, and preventing repeated mistakes

summary: Documentation on compute module execution modes. Pipeline mode enforces strict provenance control and security via the Foundry build system. All inputs/outputs must pass through the build system, maintaining data integrity and traceability. Compute modules operate under zero trust security model.

Zero trust security model for compute modules
No external network access by default for maximum isolation
Execution mode architecture for compute modules
Integration patterns between compute modules and Workshop/OSDK

keyInsights: Compute modules support two fundamentally different security postures: Function mode (serving logic to applications) and Pipeline mode (strict provenance-controlled data processing)
Pipeline mode enforces data provenance by requiring all inputs/outputs to flow through the Foundry build system, enabling complete lineage tracking via the Data Lineage application
Function mode offers a permission spectrum: No Platform Permissions (fully isolated) vs Application Permissions (service-user-based platform access), allowing precise control over module capabilities
Application permissions use a dedicated service user whose access is consistent regardless of who invokes the function, providing predictable and auditable access patterns
The zero trust model means compute modules receive no implicit access -- pipeline mode uses scoped job tokens while function mode requires explicit permission configuration

keyInsights: OAuth2 Authorization Code Flow is the recommended authentication mechanism for third-party applications, replacing service accounts to ensure per-user permissions and clear audit trails

Token isolation is a critical security requirement: data accessed with one user's Foundry token must never be exposed to another user, preventing authorization bypass through the third-party application layer
Inappropriate API use includes circumventing data controls (cross-user token data sharing), performing actions without user consent, and building competing data management interfaces outside Foundry
The guidance explicitly calls out that third-party application integration presents risks to data security and should only be undertaken with system administrator approval

Appropriate uses include external system integration, custom mobile workflows against the Ontology, and pipeline monitoring -- all patterns that extend Foundry's reach while maintaining its security model

title: Ontology Manager - Migrate to project-based permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on migrating Ontology resources to project-based permissions. This new model allows object types, action types, link types, interfaces, and shared properties to be saved within projects and inherit the project's permission structure for unified management.
relevantParts: Project-based permissions unify Ontology resource management
All core Ontology types (object, action, link, interface, shared property) supported

extractedEntities.name: Project-Based Permissions
extractedEntities.description: A new permissions model where ontology resources (object types, action types, link types, interfaces, shared properties) are saved within projects and automatically inherit permissions from those projects, replacing ontology roles and datasource-derived permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Roles
extractedEntities.description: The legacy permissions model for ontology resources that is being replaced by project-based permissions. Once resources are migrated, they cannot revert to ontology roles.

extractedEntities.name: Datasource-Derived Permissions
extractedEntities.description: A legacy permissions model where ontology resource permissions were derived from the backing datasource location. Being replaced by project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: A core ontology resource that can be saved within projects and inherit project-based permissions. Object and link instance permissions remain dependent on backing datasource location.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource representing kinetic operations that can be migrated to project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource representing relationships between object types that can be migrated to project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource defining a contract that object types can implement, migratable to project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Shared Property
extractedEntities.description: An ontology resource representing properties shared across multiple object types, migratable to project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: The Foundry tool for managing ontology resources, providing the migration assistant for transitioning to project-based permissions.

extractedEntities.name: Permission Inheritance
extractedEntities.description: Architectural pattern where ontology resources automatically inherit permissions from their parent project, enabling bulk permission management at the project level.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform where ontology resources and their permissions are managed.

keyInsights: Project-based permissions represent a one-way migration: once an ontology resource is migrated, it cannot revert to ontology roles or datasource-derived permissions.
Object and link instance permissions remain separate from ontology resource permissions and continue to depend on backing datasource location even after migration.
Two migration approaches exist: saving ontology resources alongside datasources for consistent permissions, or in a dedicated project for broad discoverability.
Ontology resource names must conform to Compass conventions (no forward slashes, no duplicates) when migrated to project-based permissions.
Datasources backing object types must be located in the same space as the ontology, establishing a spatial constraint on the permissions model.

title: Action types - Parameters - Object dropdown security considerations - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security considerations for object dropdown parameters in actions. Static value filters in dropdowns risk exposing property value combinations to unauthorized users. Mitigation involves using object properties or parameters rather than static filters.
relevantParts: Static filters in dropdowns can expose data to unauthorized users
Object property-based filters are more secure than static values
Security risk in action parameter configuration

extractedEntities.name: Object Dropdown Security
extractedEntities.description: Security consideration where static value filters in object dropdown validations are exposed to all users who can view the action type, risking exposure of property value combinations to unauthorized users.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing executable business operations whose parameter dropdowns may expose sensitive property values through static filters.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Object Type attribute whose values can be inadvertently exposed through static value filters in action type dropdown parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity (e.g., Document) whose property values may be revealed through action type dropdown filters.

extractedEntities.name: Static Value Filter
extractedEntities.description: A filter type in object dropdown validations that uses hardcoded values, which gets converted into an object set visible in network requests, potentially revealing property values to unauthorized users.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: The runtime representation of an object dropdown validation filter, containing the filter criteria including static values that could be inspected in network requests.

extractedEntities.name: Object Visibility
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism where users cannot view certain objects, but static value filters in action dropdowns may reveal the existence of specific property values for those objects.

extractedEntities.name: Parameter-Based Filtering
extractedEntities.description: Security mitigation pattern where object dropdown filters use object properties or user-provided parameters instead of static values, avoiding exposure of sensitive data in the action type definition.

keyInsights: Static value filters in action type dropdowns are converted into object sets whose filter criteria can be inspected in network requests, potentially revealing property values to unauthorized users.
The mitigation is to use object properties or user-provided parameters for filtering instead of static values, as parameter-based filters do not expose underlying data.

The actions backend redacts sensitive information (like submission criteria) from the action type definition for non-editors, but dropdown validations become visible as object sets at form-viewing time.
This is a visibility concern rather than a direct data access issue -- users can learn that a property value exists but cannot necessarily view the corresponding objects.

Data governance differs dramatically between platform and local MCP usage: platform usage keeps data within Foundry, while local usage sends tool outputs to respective LLM providers (Anthropic, Microsoft, etc.).
MCP write tools are intentionally limited: agents can create datasets but not update/delete existing ones, and all ontology modifications require human-approved proposal reviews.
The security model ensures non-destructive operations by default, with human-in-the-loop approval required for any ontology changes made through MCP-connected AI tools.
Continue in VS Code within Foundry uses Palantir-provided third-party LLMs, keeping data within the organization's Foundry environment.

keyInsights: Palantir was founded on the premise that security and privacy are not a zero-sum tradeoff; software can strengthen both security and civil liberties simultaneously.
RFP security requirements typically address three categories: corporate security, infrastructure security, and operational security, with operational security being the most differentiating.
Purpose-based access controls (PBAC) enforce data usage based on the intended purpose, enabling organizations to share data more broadly while maintaining regulatory compliance.
Data minimization, accountable data usage, and retention/deletion handling collectively increase organizational trust to enable rather than hamper collaboration.
Many organizations set low bars in RFPs by repeating outdated security standards, missing opportunities to evaluate capabilities that promote collaboration alongside security.

title: Download controls - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Download Controls

extractedEntities.name: Checkpoints

extractedEntities.name: Discoverer Role

extractedEntities.name: Custom Roles

extractedEntities.name: Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth for Downloads
extractedEntities.description: Security pattern combining roles, checkpoints, and audit logs to provide layered control over download actions, acknowledging that no single mechanism is comprehensive against all data transfer methods.

extractedEntities.name: Download Definition
extractedEntities.description: In Foundry, a download is an action transferring data to a user's local machine, typically via Export/Download buttons in the UI (CSV export, Contour export, Workshop export to Excel).

extractedEntities.name: Shared Security Responsibility Model
extractedEntities.description: Security model where customers maintain responsibility over their data, access controls, and usage policies, with Foundry providing the technical mechanisms to enforce them.

keyInsights: Download controls alone are not comprehensive; clipboard copying, screenshots, automated exports, webhooks, and external system calls are all alternative data transfer vectors that require separate controls.
Foundry provides layered download defense: roles (Discoverer role or custom roles), checkpoints (user acknowledgement/justification), and audit logs for monitoring.
The Discoverer role specifically excludes download operations, while custom roles can selectively remove download workflows while preserving other privileged operations.
Checkpoints reduce unintentional downloads by requiring acknowledgement or justification before proceeding, creating accountability records.
Foundry operates under a shared security responsibility model where customers own responsibility for data governance while the platform provides enforcement mechanisms.

title: Protecting your self-hosted Foundry installation - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on securing self-hosted Foundry deployments. Covers infrastructure security requirements including network isolation, encryption configuration, HSM recommendations for key management, and TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites.

extractedEntities.description: Security requirement for self-hosted Foundry where all underlying servers and storage devices must use full-disk encryption (LUKS, self-encrypting drives, or cloud provider solutions) with AES-256 or AES-128, in addition to Foundry's application-level encryption.

extractedEntities.name: Data in Transit Encryption
extractedEntities.description: Security requirement mandating TLS 1.2+ for all client-Foundry communications, with strong cipher suites (ECDHE with CHACHA-POLY1305 or AES-GCM), avoiding CBC-mode ciphers where possible.

extractedEntities.name: HSM Key Management
extractedEntities.description: Recommendation to store cryptographic key material in hardware security modules for self-hosted Foundry installations.

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Architecture

extractedEntities.name: Identity Security

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core platform designed for secure collaboration that can be deployed self-hosted in customer datacenters or clouds.

extractedEntities.name: Apollo

keyInsights: Self-hosted Foundry installations require defense in depth: physical security, full-disk encryption (AES-256/128), application-level encryption, and TLS 1.2+ with strong cipher suites.
HSM-backed key management is recommended for cryptographic material, and ECDHE with CHACHA-POLY1305 or AES-GCM is the recommended cipher suite configuration.
Identity security mandates centralized SSO, named accounts (no sharing), and strong MFA preferring FIDO2, with SMS/email-based MFA explicitly discouraged.
Zero trust architecture is recommended where no implicit trust is granted based on network position, complementing traditional perimeter-based security.
Foundry provides application-level encryption at rest by default, but self-hosted deployments must additionally encrypt underlying storage and enforce full-disk encryption.

title: Why you should govern data access through Purpose-Based Access Control - Dataminded on Medium
summary: Third-party analysis of Purpose-Based Access Control as a governance strategy, referencing Palantir's implementation. Explains how PBAC is simpler and more agile than traditional access management while maintaining compliance with regulatory requirements.
relevantParts: Independent analysis of PBAC as governance pattern
Comparison with traditional access management approaches

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Data access governance methodology where access is granted to purposes (applications of data) rather than individuals, allowing users to work within the context of a single purpose while potentially being assigned to multiple purposes.

extractedEntities.name: RBAC
extractedEntities.description: Role-Based Access Control, a technical implementation for organizing permission sets that can be combined with PBAC by scoping purpose-based permissions to roles like data scientist or data engineer.

extractedEntities.name: ABAC
extractedEntities.description: Attribute-Based Access Control, a technical permission mechanism that can be combined with PBAC at the purpose level.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's governance platform referenced as an implementation of purpose-based access controls for data governance.

extractedEntities.name: Purpose-Scoped Data Access
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where all data access occurs within the context of a single purpose, ensuring that cross-referencing of data across purposes requires explicit approval and maintaining GDPR-aligned purpose limitation.

extractedEntities.name: Data Access Governance
extractedEntities.description: The component of data governance concerned with controlling who has access to what data, serving as a defense against cyberattacks, data leakage, and enabling regulatory compliance.

keyInsights: PBAC grants access to purposes (applications of data) rather than individuals, with users always accessing data within the context of a single purpose even if assigned to multiple purposes.
PBAC operates at a different level than RBAC/ABAC and can be combined with them; RBAC/ABAC define permission sets while PBAC assigns those sets to purposes instead of individuals.

Benefits include flexible team onboarding/offboarding (all purpose access is bundled), consistent access across team members in the same role, and GDPR-aligned purpose limitation and data minimization.
PBAC makes cross-referencing of data across purposes require explicit approval, preventing unauthorized data combination and maintaining proportionality principles.
Palantir Foundry is cited as a governance tool implementing purpose-based access controls, demonstrating the concept's enterprise applicability.

relevantParts: Third-party perspective on Palantir security best practices
Compliance framework alignment: HIPAA, GDPR, ITAR

extractedEntities.name: Encryption at Rest
extractedEntities.description: Data protection mechanism using AES-256/128 encryption for stored data in Foundry databases

extractedEntities.name: Encryption in Transit
extractedEntities.description: Data protection mechanism using TLS 1.2+ encryption for data transmitted across networks in Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: Foundational security element defining user roles and permissions to ensure only authorized personnel access sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Data Anonymization

extractedEntities.name: Audit Logging

extractedEntities.name: HIPAA Compliance
extractedEntities.description: Healthcare regulatory compliance framework supported by Palantir's security controls

extractedEntities.name: GDPR Compliance
extractedEntities.description: European data protection regulatory compliance supported by Palantir's privacy and security features

extractedEntities.name: ITAR Compliance
extractedEntities.description: International Traffic in Arms Regulations compliance supported by Palantir's data governance capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform supporting robust encryption, access controls, audit logging, and regulatory compliance

keyInsights: Palantir supports robust encryption standards including TLS 1.2+ for transit and AES-256/128 for data at rest
Foundry provides built-in tools for data minimization and anonymization to reduce risk of sensitive data exposure
Comprehensive audit logging tracks user activities and data access patterns enabling real-time anomaly detection
Palantir platforms are designed to meet stringent compliance requirements including GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and ITAR
Human error is identified as a leading cause of data breaches, making employee security training essential alongside platform controls

title: Data Connection - Listeners - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation
summary: Documentation on security for Data Connection Listeners, which allow external systems to push data into Foundry. Listeners can be mounted to dedicated subdomains for granular ingress control, governance workflows, and isolation of less secure endpoints from primary enrollment domains.
relevantParts: Dedicated subdomains for granular ingress control
Isolation of less secure endpoints from primary domains
Governance workflows for data ingestion security

extractedEntities.name: Listener
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection component that allows external systems to push data into Foundry, implementing security protocols defined by the external system rather than standard Foundry authentication

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data ingestion platform that supports listeners for external system push-based data flow

extractedEntities.name: Listener Subdomain
extractedEntities.description: Dedicated subdomain assigned to listeners for granular ingress control, governance workflows, and isolation from primary enrollment domains

extractedEntities.name: Endpoint Rotation
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism allowing compromised listener endpoints to be migrated to new endpoints with zero downtime

extractedEntities.name: Listener Security
extractedEntities.description: Security paradigm for listeners where external system protocols define request authorization rather than standard Foundry authentication

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security labels that can be applied to listeners for additional access control and data governance

extractedEntities.name: Subdomain Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of isolating less secure listener endpoints from primary enrollment domains using dedicated subdomains

extractedEntities.name: Output Stream
extractedEntities.description: The data stream produced by a listener that must be secured alongside the listener itself

keyInsights: Listeners bypass standard Foundry authentication, instead implementing security protocols defined by the external systems pushing data
Dedicated subdomains provide granular ingress control and isolate less secure listener endpoints from primary Foundry enrollment domains
Endpoint rotation enables zero-downtime migration when a listener endpoint is compromised, supporting one active endpoint at a time
Data redaction on incoming listener data is best-effort only; Palantir cannot guarantee full removal of sensitive tokens from request bodies
Both the listener itself and its output stream must be secured, typically by placing both in a restricted project and applying markings

title: GitHub - palantir/foundry-platform-typescript: The official TypeScript library for the Foundry API

Can be used with either an Ontology SDK client or standalone platform SDK client. Requires Developer Console application setup with client ID and redirect URL.
relevantParts: Platform-level TypeScript SDK separate from OSDK
Namespace-based package organization for Foundry and Gotham APIs

extractedEntities.description: Official TypeScript library providing bindings for the Foundry and Gotham Platform APIs. Packages published as @osdk/foundry.{namespace} and @osdk/gotham.{namespace} on NPM.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK

keyInsights: The Platform TypeScript SDK provides two client types: a platform client (ontology-agnostic, cross-ontology) and an ontology client (tied to a specific ontology RID), serving different integration patterns.
SDK packages are organized by API namespace (@osdk/foundry.{namespace}) enabling granular dependency management — install only the API surfaces you need.
The OSDK is language-agnostic with official support for TypeScript, Python, Java, and OpenAPI specs for any other language.
Authentication requires a Developer Console application registration to obtain a client ID, using the same OAuth2 infrastructure as other third-party applications.
The SDK covers both Foundry and Gotham APIs, indicating a unified developer experience across Palantir's product lines.

title: Introducing Conjure, Palantir's toolchain for HTTP/JSON APIs - Palantir Blog
summary: Official Palantir blog post introducing Conjure, explaining why they built it instead of using Swagger/OpenREST. Covers the design philosophy of using a restricted API definition language for consistency, the intermediate representation approach, and how it scales across hundreds of microservice repositories.
relevantParts: Design rationale for building Conjure vs using existing tools
Battle-tested across hundreds of repos at Palantir scale

extractedEntities.name: Conjure
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's API definition toolchain that compiles declarative YAML definitions into language-specific client and server bindings via a JSON intermediate representation. Originally planned as a stepping-stone to gRPC but became valued for its own design constraints.

extractedEntities.name: Restricted API Design Language
extractedEntities.description: Conjure's deliberate philosophy of supporting only a restricted subset of HTTP/JSON patterns to enforce consistency, focus, and clarity across all API definitions — in contrast to more permissive tools like Swagger.

extractedEntities.name: Conjure Wire Format
extractedEntities.description: Specification for encoding messages between clients and servers: primitives as HTTP header/path/query parameters, complex objects as JSON body payload in HTTP requests and responses.

extractedEntities.name: Conjure Type System
extractedEntities.description: Expressive type system supporting enums, union types, maps, lists, sets, and custom objects for modeling API domains.

extractedEntities.name: Conjure IR (Self-Hosting)
extractedEntities.description: The intermediate representation format is defined as Conjure data types itself, enabling bootstrapped deserialization in target languages before code generation.

extractedEntities.name: Backwards Compatibility Preservation
extractedEntities.description: Design principle in Conjure that helps preserve backwards compatibility — old clients can communicate with new servers, enabling incremental API evolution.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform whose internal and third-party APIs are defined using Conjure.

extractedEntities.name: FlightSearch API Example
extractedEntities.description: Example Conjure API definition demonstrating data types (Connection, SearchResult) and service endpoints for a flight search service.

keyInsights: Conjure was originally planned as a stepping-stone to gRPC, but Palantir learned to value the constraints it imposed on API design and chose to keep it as the primary API toolchain.
The restricted API design philosophy is Conjure's key differentiator from Swagger/OpenREST — by limiting what can be expressed, it forces consistency across hundreds of API definitions.
Conjure's IR is self-hosting: it is defined as Conjure types, meaning generators can deserialize API definitions into native ASTs 'for free' after bootstrapping.
Conjure drives engineering velocity through simplicity rather than sophistication, enabling cross-team and cross-language collaboration at scale.
The wire format strictly separates primitive parameters (header/path/query) from complex objects (JSON body), creating a clean and predictable HTTP contract.

title: Compute modules - Overview - Palantir
summary: Official documentation for Foundry Compute Modules, which enable running containerized workloads in Foundry regardless of programming language. Supports container-backed functions callable from Workshop or Slate, with dynamic horizontal scaling based on load. Functions are registered with API names following com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName> pattern.
relevantParts: Containerized compute architecture for custom workloads
Function registration and API naming conventions
Dynamic horizontal scaling based on load

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Foundry capability for deploying interactive containerized workloads (Docker images) on the Palantir platform with dynamic horizontal scaling. Supports any programming language and enables container-backed functions, data integration, and model hosting.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept that can be implemented as a container-backed function via Compute Modules, callable from Workshop or Slate applications.

extractedEntities.name: Container-Backed Function
extractedEntities.description: A function implemented inside a Docker container that is registered with an API name following the pattern com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName> and can be queried from Workshop or Slate.

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module API Name Pattern
extractedEntities.description: The naming convention for compute module functions: com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName>, used to register and reference container-backed functions.

extractedEntities.name: Dynamic Horizontal Scaling
extractedEntities.description: Compute modules automatically scale the number of replicas up or down based on current and historic request load, providing predictive scaling for varying workloads.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application builder that can query container-backed functions deployed as Compute Modules.

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application builder that can query container-backed functions deployed as Compute Modules.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform hosting Compute Modules for serverless containerized workloads.

extractedEntities.name: Container-Based Data Integration
extractedEntities.description: Use case where Compute Modules connect to arbitrary data sources and ingest data into Foundry streams, datasets, or media sets.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Model Hosting
extractedEntities.description: Use case where Compute Modules host custom or open-source ML models and serve them interactively from Foundry applications.

keyInsights: Compute Modules enable any programming language to run on the Palantir platform by containerizing code as Docker images, removing the language restrictions of native Foundry compute.
Container-backed functions bridge the gap between external code and the Ontology — containers are registered with API names and become callable from Workshop and Slate like native Foundry functions.
Dynamic horizontal scaling is both reactive (current load) and predictive (historic load patterns), differentiating it from simple autoscaling.
Compute Modules are not suitable for workloads requiring dynamic vertical scaling (e.g., single requests varying from 1MB to 100GB), which is an important limitation to understand.
Three primary use cases: container-backed functions for interactive queries, container-based data integration for ingestion, and custom model hosting for ML inference.

title: Workshop - Overview - Palantir
summary: Official Workshop documentation covering the no-code/low-code application builder. Workshop empowers users to create applications from no-code, low-code, and code-based widgets using the Object layer as the primary building block. Supports common patterns like Inboxes for task triage and Common Operational Pictures (COPs) for big-screen views with maps and statistics.
relevantParts: Object layer as primary building block for applications

extractedEntities.name: Workshop

keyInsights: Workshop uses the Object layer as its primary building block — all data comes from the Object Data Layer, making the Ontology the single source of truth for application data.
Workshop follows three principles: Object data (Ontology as building block), Consistent design (unified design system), and Interactivity (beyond typical dashboards).
Actions provide writeback to object data and Functions provide business logic, creating a clean separation between read (Object layer), write (Actions), and compute (Functions) in Workshop.
Workshop aims to match the quality of custom React applications while being accessible to no-code builders, positioning it between dashboard tools and full custom development.
Common patterns (Inbox, COP) suggest Workshop is optimized for operational use cases — task management, situational awareness, and decision support rather than pure analytics.

title: Developer Console - Overview - Palantir
summary: Documentation for Developer Console, the portal for building and managing Custom Applications with their associated authorization clients, SDKs, and web hosting. Components include OAuth client, generated SDKs, auto-generated API documentation, permissions, metrics, and optional web hosting on Foundry subdomains.
relevantParts: Developer Console as the central SDK/app management portal
OAuth client management and SDK generation workflow

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry portal for building and managing Custom Applications along with their associated authorization clients, SDKs, web hosting, permissions, and usage metrics.

extractedEntities.name: Custom Application
extractedEntities.description: A developer-defined application that extends Foundry capabilities with custom logic, composed of an OAuth client, generated SDKs, API documentation, permissions, metrics, and optional web hosting.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth Client
extractedEntities.description: Authorization client that grants human and service users programmatic access to Foundry resources. Legacy OAuth clients provide unscoped access; newer custom applications provide scoped access.

extractedEntities.name: Generated SDK
extractedEntities.description: Auto-generated SDK packages created from ontology resources added to a custom application's scope, available in TypeScript, Python, and other supported languages.

extractedEntities.name: SDK Documentation
extractedEntities.description: Auto-generated API documentation and code snippets tailored to an application's SDK content, including installation guides, code examples for loading objects, and reference materials for object types, action types, and functions.

extractedEntities.name: Application Scope
extractedEntities.description: Configuration that defines which ontology resources a custom application's tokens can access, ensuring tokens only grant access to approved resources.

extractedEntities.name: Web Hosting
extractedEntities.description: Optional capability to host frontend-only applications such as React SPAs directly on a Foundry subdomain, eliminating the need for external hosting infrastructure.

extractedEntities.name: Application Metrics
extractedEntities.description: Usage and performance monitoring for custom applications including request volume, success rates, and API latency.

extractedEntities.name: Scoped Token
extractedEntities.description: Long-lived token generated for programmatic access that is scoped to only the resources approved for a specific application.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data integration and analytics platform that hosts the Developer Console and custom applications.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit that allows external applications to interact with the Foundry Ontology, hosted via Developer Console web hosting.

keyInsights: Developer Console is the single portal for managing custom applications, their OAuth clients, generated SDKs, and web hosting on Foundry.
Custom applications replace legacy OAuth clients by providing scoped access instead of unscoped access, improving security posture.
SDKs are auto-generated from ontology resources added to an application's scope, with documentation auto-generated in TypeScript, Python, and other languages.
Frontend-only applications like React SPAs can be hosted directly on Foundry subdomains, eliminating external hosting dependencies.
Application scoping ensures that tokens only grant access to resources explicitly approved for the application, enforcing least-privilege access.
Legacy OAuth clients are being deprecated in favor of scoped custom applications.

title: Platform overview - Overview - Palantir
summary: High-level overview of the Palantir platform, explaining how AIP, Foundry, and Apollo work together. Covers the platform's design philosophy as an enterprise operating system with integrated development environments like VS Code and JupyterLab providing connectivity with Ontology-driven applications.
relevantParts: Platform-level overview of all three products

extractedEntities.name: Palantir AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI platform that powers real-time, AI-driven decision-making by connecting generative AI to operations. Part of the AI Mesh alongside Foundry and Apollo.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data operations platform that provides the core data infrastructure, compute primitives, and Ontology system for enterprise use.

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's mission control for autonomous software deployment, the continuous delivery platform that underpins AIP and Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The core architecture representing enterprise decisions (not just data) by integrating data, logic, and action components into a modern, AI-accessible computing environment.

extractedEntities.name: Decision Components
extractedEntities.description: Foundational pattern decomposing every enterprise decision into three components: data (relevant facts), logic (business rules, models, probabilities), and actions (kinetic effects in the world).

extractedEntities.name: AI Mesh
extractedEntities.description: The collective architecture of AIP, Foundry, and Apollo that can deliver the full range of AI-driven products from LLM-powered web apps to edge applications with localized AI.

extractedEntities.name: AI Teaming
extractedEntities.description: A design pattern where all Ontology decision components (data, logic, actions) are designed to facilitate collaboration between humans and AI agents.

extractedEntities.name: AIP Bootcamps
extractedEntities.description: Hands-on sessions where customers achieve outcomes with AI quickly, serving as a key mechanism for driving platform adoption.

extractedEntities.name: Action
extractedEntities.description: The kinetic decision component representing how decisions manifest in the world, including effects and outcomes in production settings.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Logic components within the Ontology that encode business rules, forecasting models, and computational pipelines.

keyInsights: The Ontology is designed to represent enterprise decisions (data, logic, actions), not simply data, making it fundamentally different from traditional data modeling approaches.
Palantir's platform consists of three interconnected platforms: AIP (generative AI), Foundry (data operations), and Apollo (continuous delivery), collectively forming an 'AI Mesh'.
The key differentiator of the Palantir platform is its software architecture revolving around the Ontology, which enables deep two-way interoperability with existing enterprise systems.
Every decision component in the Ontology is designed to facilitate AI teaming patterns, unlocking the potential of operators, analysts, and subject-matter experts working alongside AI.
The AI Mesh can deliver the full spectrum of AI products: from LLM-powered web applications to mobile apps using vision-language models to edge applications with localized AI.

extractedEntities.name: Multimodal Security Controls
extractedEntities.description: Military-grade security controls that encompass objects, links, actions, functions, and other semantic/kinetic primitives within the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Object

keyInsights: AIP and Foundry collectively consist of 300+ microservices running in a highly available, autoscaling compute mesh atop zero-trust security infrastructure.
The Ontology system is described as the 'heart' of Palantir's architecture, integrating data, logic, action, and security policies into a single representation usable by humans and AI.
Military-grade, multimodal security controls encompass all Ontology primitives (objects, links, actions, functions), ensuring both humans and AI agents operate with precision and guardrails.
Forward Deployed Engineering is Palantir's core methodology: teams get close to problems while working with core engineering to synthesize feedback, analogous to neural network backpropagation.
Palantir's common architecture (AIP, Foundry, Apollo) functions as an enterprise operating system spanning 50+ verticals from healthcare to shipbuilding to energy production.
Aggressive node cycling is used as a security measure to guard against advanced persistent threats within the platform infrastructure.

title: Ontology SDK - TypeScript OSDK migration guide - Palantir
summary: Migration guide for upgrading from OSDK v1 to TypeScript OSDK 2.0. Documents breaking changes, new API patterns, and performance improvements in the 2.0 release including streamlined code generation and linear scaling with Ontology shape.
relevantParts: OSDK version migration patterns and breaking changes
API evolution from v1 to v2

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript OSDK 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Major version upgrade of the Ontology SDK for TypeScript that scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata, supports lazy loading, separates client from generated code, and provides streamlined code generation.

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript OSDK 1.x
extractedEntities.description: Original version of the TypeScript Ontology SDK that generated full implementations for every Ontology item, scaling with entire Ontology size and closely coupling client to generated code.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Lazy Loading
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript OSDK 2.0 feature where applications only require Ontology items when they use them, rather than loading the entire Ontology upfront.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Code Generation
extractedEntities.description: The process of generating SDK code from Ontology definitions. OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata rather than actual Ontology content, significantly improving performance.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Client Separation
extractedEntities.description: OSDK 2.0 architectural change that decouples the client library from generated code, enabling rapid hotfixes through library updates without SDK regeneration.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's in-platform development environment where SDKs are generated, managed, and documented. Provides version picker for accessing 2.0 documentation.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology concept that OSDK generates typed interfaces for, with 2.0 providing lighter-weight metadata-based representations.

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept that requires OSDK 2.0 for support, not available in 1.x.

extractedEntities.name: Media Set
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept for media content that requires OSDK 2.0 for support.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK that provides generated, type-safe access to Foundry Ontology from external applications in Python, Java, and TypeScript.

keyInsights: OSDK 1.x generated full implementations for every Ontology item, causing it to scale with the entire Ontology size; OSDK 2.0 scales linearly with Ontology shape/metadata instead.
Client-code separation in OSDK 2.0 enables rapid hotfixes through library dependency updates without requiring SDK regeneration, a major operational improvement.
New features like interfaces and media sets require OSDK 2.0, making migration necessary for accessing the full Ontology feature set.
OSDK 2.0 supports lazy loading, meaning applications only load Ontology items when they are actually used, improving application performance.
Palantir committed to maintaining OSDK 1.x support for at least one year after OSDK 2.0 release, providing a migration window.

title: Ontology SDK - Subscribe to Ontology changes with the TypeScript OSDK - Palantir
summary: Documentation for the TypeScript OSDK subscriptions feature, enabling real-time updates when objects in a specified object set change. Covers addition/deletion of objects and property change notifications, representing the reactive data layer of the OSDK.
relevantParts: Real-time subscription architecture for Ontology changes

extractedEntities.name: OSDK Subscriptions
extractedEntities.description: TypeScript OSDK feature (v2.1+) enabling real-time updates when objects in a specified object set are changed, including additions, deletions, and property changes.

extractedEntities.name: .subscribe Method
extractedEntities.description: OSDK method chained onto an object set that accepts lifecycle event handlers (onChange, onSuccessfulSubscription, onOutOfDate, onError) and returns an unsubscribe function.

extractedEntities.name: onChange Handler
extractedEntities.description: Subscription event handler triggered when an object is added, updated, or deleted. Provides the object instance and a state enum (ADDED_OR_UPDATED or DELETED).

extractedEntities.name: onOutOfDate Handler
extractedEntities.description: Subscription event handler triggered when the subscription may have missed updates, indicating the consumer should refresh its local data.

extractedEntities.name: onSuccessfulSubscription Handler
extractedEntities.description: Subscription event handler triggered when the subscription connection is successfully established.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A collection of objects that can be subscribed to for real-time change notifications, supporting filtering via .where() clauses.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: The Ontology concept representing a type of object (e.g., Country) whose instances can be monitored through subscriptions.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Individual attributes on objects (e.g., population) that trigger change notifications when modified.

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript OSDK 2.1
extractedEntities.description: The OSDK version (2.1.x) that introduced the subscriptions feature for real-time Ontology change tracking.

extractedEntities.name: Reactive Data Layer
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the OSDK provides real-time push-based updates from the Ontology rather than requiring polling, enabling event-driven application architectures.

keyInsights: Subscriptions provide three object states: ADDED_OR_UPDATED (object added to set or property changed) and DELETED (object removed), enabling full lifecycle tracking.
Object sets constructed using .pivotTo (link traversal) are explicitly not supported for subscriptions, limiting real-time updates to direct object set queries.
The onOutOfDate handler signals that the subscription may have missed updates, requiring consumers to implement a refresh strategy for data consistency.
Subscriptions can be filtered using .where() clauses on the object set, narrowing the scope of real-time updates to specific property conditions.
The properties array parameter in .subscribe allows selecting which property changes trigger notifications, enabling fine-grained update control.
Subscriptions require OSDK v2.1.x with a matching @osdk/client package version, establishing a version dependency for the feature.

title: Compute modules - Configuration - Functions - Palantir
summary: Documentation for configuring functions within Compute Modules, including function registration, API name conventions (com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName>), testing via the Test tab, and streaming response patterns. Functions are always registered with version 0.0.0.
relevantParts: Streaming response architecture for compute module functions

extractedEntities.name: Compute Modules
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's containerized compute workload system that can run in function execution mode, allowing registration of callable functions with typed inputs and outputs.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A callable unit within Compute Modules that accepts typed JSON inputs and returns typed outputs, registered with API names following the pattern com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName>.

extractedEntities.name: Function API Name
extractedEntities.description: The function locator following the format com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName> where namespace is lowercase and MyApiName is camelCase. Used to call functions from other Foundry code.

extractedEntities.name: Function Schema Registration
extractedEntities.description: Process of defining function inputs, outputs, and API names either through the Compute Modules UI or via HTTP POST request. Functions are always registered at version 0.0.0.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Response
extractedEntities.description: Compute Module capability where function outputs of type Array are streamed as individual JSON elements rather than returned as a single response.

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module Type Mapping
extractedEntities.description: The mapping between Foundry types (Integer, Boolean, Date, Timestamp, etc.) and their HTTP serialization formats (int, boolean, string, milliseconds since epoch, etc.).

extractedEntities.name: Struct
extractedEntities.description: A custom composite type in Compute Modules, serialized as JSON with named fields, used for complex function inputs and outputs.

extractedEntities.name: Automatic Function Schema Inference
extractedEntities.description: Compute Modules SDK feature that automatically infers function schemas from code, simplifying the registration process.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: The platform environment where Compute Module functions are registered and called from other code.

keyInsights: Compute Module functions always register at version 0.0.0 and updates overwrite the existing version, meaning there is no function versioning within a compute module.
Function API names follow a strict convention: com.<namespace>.computemodules.<MyApiName>, and changing the API name breaks all consumer code.
Timestamps are serialized as milliseconds since epoch over HTTP, while Dates and Decimals are serialized as strings, reflecting careful type-safety decisions.
Streaming responses serialize Array outputs as individual JSON elements streamed over HTTP, enabling efficient handling of large result sets.
The Compute Modules SDK can automatically infer function schemas from code, reducing the manual registration burden and ensuring type safety.
Structs serve as the mechanism for complex composite types in function inputs/outputs, serialized as JSON objects with named fields.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Write API

extractedEntities.name: Pipelines Mode

extractedEntities.name: Compute Modules

keyInsights: The TypeScript Compute Module SDK uses @sinclair/typebox for schema definitions, enabling compile-time type safety while generating runtime schemas for automatic function registration.
Streaming responses require each write to produce valid JSON individually, even for simple string literals (JSON.stringify is mandatory per element).
The SDK supports both Functions mode (callable endpoints) and Pipelines mode (data pipeline aliases), representing two distinct compute module execution paradigms.
A .default() handler can be registered to handle unsupported query names, providing graceful error handling for unknown function calls.
The SDK provides utilities for service discovery, authentication token management, and external source access within the containerized workload environment.

extractedEntities.name: Automatic Function Discovery

extractedEntities.name: Explicit Function Registration
extractedEntities.description: Alternative pattern using add_function/add_functions plus start_compute_module for registering functions without decorators.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Result Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Python pattern where functions returning Iterable types with streaming=True stream individual JSON-serializable elements back to the caller.

extractedEntities.name: Compute Modules
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's containerized compute platform that the Python SDK enables building against.

keyInsights: The Python SDK provides two function registration patterns: the @function decorator for simple cases and explicit add_function/add_functions for more control.
Automatic function discovery inspects Python type hints to auto-generate function schemas, reducing manual configuration and ensuring type consistency.
Streaming is enabled by declaring Iterable return types with streaming=True, requiring elements to be JSON-serializable for HTTP streaming.
The context parameter provides access to source secrets and configurations, enabling authenticated access to external systems from within compute modules.
The SDK is incubating and subject to change, indicating active development of the Compute Modules ecosystem.

title: Overview - App building - Palantir

Developer Console for custom React app development

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology concept serving as a semantic primitive consumed by application building tools like Workshop and Slate.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Kinetic primitive in the Ontology consumed by Workshop and other application building tools for user-driven workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept enabling code-based enrichment that can be embedded within Workshop widgets for complex interactions and data capture.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's developer environment for building OSDK-powered custom applications.

keyInsights: Workshop categorizes Ontology elements into semantic primitives (objects, links for data) and kinetic primitives (Actions, Functions for operations), creating a clear separation of concerns.
All application building tools in Palantir leverage the same core security, lineage, data, and compute primitives, ensuring consistent governance across all development approaches.
The platform supports a spectrum from no-code (Workshop widgets) to low-code (Slate drag-and-drop) to full-code (OSDK custom React apps), accommodating different builder skill levels.
Knowledge compounds across the enterprise because every tool continuously enriches a consistent set of data and model assets contained within the Ontology.
Workflow Lineage provides graph-based provenance for understanding and debugging workflows, covering objects, actions, functions, LLMs, and applications.
Functions can be seamlessly embedded within Workshop widgets, enabling complex interactions, cascading processes, and advanced data capture without leaving the no-code environment.

keyInsights: The advanced to-do app demonstrates six key OSDK features: interface polymorphism, media content, runtime-derived properties, metadata-driven interfaces, real-time subscriptions, and efficient user data handling.
OsdkProvider from @osdk/react provides the authentication context pattern, wrapping the entire application router for consistent client access.
Data service hooks encapsulate OSDK interactions following SWR (stale-while-revalidate) strategy, giving each component exactly the data it needs with background updates.
Environment configuration uses meta tags in index.html for key variables, with separate development and production environment files.
Code Workspaces provides an in-platform VS Code environment where developers can create applications from curated example templates including the to-do app.

keyInsights: External OSDK applications require three configuration elements: a public OAuth client in Developer Console, CORS policy in Control Panel, and environment variables for API URL and client ID.
The OSDK uses an application-specific NPM registry, meaning each Foundry application has its own generated package with typed interfaces for its selected Ontology objects.
A client-facing (public) OAuth client must be selected during application creation; choosing a confidential client prevents browser-based login and cannot be changed afterward.
The starter app demonstrates the minimum viable architecture: client.ts for OSDK initialization, home.tsx for Ontology data consumption, and auth/callback route for OAuth flow.
Production deployment requires storing FOUNDRY_CLIENT_ID, FOUNDRY_API_URL, and APPLICATION_REDIRECT_URL in CI/CD secret management.

Blueprint takes a least-intrusion approach to global styling, limiting it to typography normalization to avoid conflicts with other CSS frameworks.
The monorepo contains 7+ library packages (@blueprintjs/core, table, select, datetime, icons, etc.) plus documentation and build tooling packages.
Blueprint provides the foundational UI layer that Workshop, Slate, and custom OSDK applications build upon for rendering Ontology data.

keyInsights: Apollo is considered Palantir's third platform alongside Foundry and Gotham, serving as the continuous delivery infrastructure that makes both SaaS platforms possible.
Apollo runs as an independent, standalone platform decoupled from Foundry, sitting as a layer between applications and underlying infrastructure.
Palantir achieved DoD IL-5 (the highest SaaS authorization for Mission-Critical and National Security Systems), enabling all new government customers to use public cloud for unclassified work.
Apollo enables deployment across the full spectrum of environments: public cloud, government clouds, classified networks, on-premises, and disconnected edge devices (submarines, drones).
Since 2018, every new commercial customer and nearly all new US government customers opted for the SaaS model, demonstrating Apollo's success in enabling environment-agnostic deployment.
Apollo was conceived alongside Foundry specifically to solve the challenge of bringing a single SaaS platform to all customers regardless of environment constraints.

title: Software development kit (SDK) - API Reference - Palantir
summary: API reference documentation for Foundry Platform SDKs, covering the two SDK types: Ontology SDK (OSDK) for generated Ontology-specific access in Python/Java/TypeScript, and Platform SDKs for generic Foundry API interaction. OSDK supports object types, actions, functions, and AIP Logic functions.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK

extractedEntities.name: Platform SDK
extractedEntities.description: Generic libraries for interacting with all Foundry APIs including the Ontology, available in Python and TypeScript. Suitable for administrative tasks, governance workflows, or cross-enrollment portability.

extractedEntities.name: Per-Enrollment Code Generation
extractedEntities.description: OSDK generates enrollment-specific code that includes classes matching your Ontology (e.g., an Employee class for an Employee object type), providing a more intuitive interface than generic platform SDKs.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology concept that OSDKs generate typed classes for (e.g., Employee object type generates Employee class with methods).

extractedEntities.description: Two-tier SDK architecture where Platform SDKs abstract Foundry APIs and OSDKs provide further abstractions on top of Platform SDKs for Ontology-specific interactions.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: The platform whose APIs are accessed through both Platform SDKs and OSDKs.

keyInsights: Palantir offers a two-tier SDK architecture: Platform SDKs for generic Foundry API access, and OSDKs for enrollment-specific typed Ontology access built on top of Platform SDKs.
OSDKs generate enrollment-specific code (e.g., an Employee class for an Employee object type), providing a significantly more intuitive developer experience than generic API wrappers.
Platform SDKs should be used when not reading/writing Ontology data, automating admin/governance tasks, or building cross-enrollment portable applications.
OSDKs should be used when building applications that primarily read and write Ontology data, as they provide the most developer-friendly typed interface.
Platform SDKs are currently available in Python and TypeScript, while OSDKs support Python, Java, and TypeScript.

title: The Palantir Developer Reformation - Medium
summary: Analysis of Palantir's developer experience challenges and evolution. Identifies gaps including lack of CLI tooling, documentation issues, and how the Forward Deployed Engineering (FDE) model historically excluded external developers. Notes the organizational shift toward improving developer experience.

FDE model's impact on external developer tooling

extractedEntities.name: Forward Deployed Engineering Model
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's product development model where engineers embed with customers to solve challenging problems, tightly coupling FDE and PD teams to shape products to match real-world problems

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform with 'Lego block' components used by FDEs to rapidly assemble solutions, historically lacking developer-facing tooling like CLIs

extractedEntities.name: Developer Experience Gap
extractedEntities.description: Structural gap in Palantir's platform caused by the FDE model excluding external developers, resulting in missing CLI tooling, documentation issues, and PD teams not empathizing with developer needs

extractedEntities.name: CLI Absence
extractedEntities.description: Foundry historically lacked a command-line interface because the FDE model did not require one — CLIs are solutions in search of a problem under this lens

extractedEntities.name: ClickOps vs DevOps
extractedEntities.description: Anti-pattern where developers must navigate console UIs manually instead of automating tasks via CLI, contrasting with hyperscaler best practices

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Public interfaces that many parts of Foundry lack, preventing exposure via CLI or external tooling

keyInsights: The FDE model tightly couples Forward Deployed Engineers with Product Development teams, ensuring products match real-world problem shapes rather than theoretical requirements documents
The FDE model historically excluded external developers, creating a developer experience that leaves engineers 'feeling ill upon first exposure' to the platform
Foundry lacked a CLI because the user category that would use one did not exist in sufficient quantity under the FDE model, and many platform parts lack public interfaces
PD teams culturally reframe developer problems as Foundry-shaped solutions rather than building standard developer tooling like CLIs
The recommended 'medicine' for developers is to think more like FDEs and less like software engineers, though this underutilizes engineering capabilities

Palantir's API evolution philosophy: build tooling that naturally leads developers into a 'pit of success' so they focus on design rather than schema change subtleties
The deployment model allows asynchronous, uncoordinated updates of clients and servers — critical for microservice architectures with independent team ownership
Backwards-compatible API evolution is strongly preferred, where clients don't even notice changes have occurred

extractedEntities.description: osquery's capability to record network connections by consuming bind and connect syscall audit events.

extractedEntities.name: Audit Consumer
extractedEntities.description: Userland application (such as osquery or auditd) that processes audit events emitted from the Linux kernel's audit subsystem.

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise Endpoint Auditing
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of deploying osquery at enterprise scale with properly tuned audit configurations to balance security visibility with system performance.

keyInsights: osquery and auditd serve nearly identical roles as audit consumers, both processing kernel audit events for logging.
Improperly configured auditing causes performance issues, resource conflicts, and incomplete data that weakens detection.
Linux audit rules cover three categories: control rules for system behavior, file watches for access monitoring, and syscall rules for execution logging.
Palantir deploys osquery at enterprise scale, requiring careful tuning of audit configurations to avoid performance degradation.

keyInsights: Palantir recommends using 'de-identification' instead of 'anonymization' because anonymization conveys a false sense of security by implying data cannot be re-identified

Connections can be established via direct secure egress pipelines or through deployed data connection agents when direct connections pose security concerns

The diversity of data types, hosting environments, update frequencies, and governance policies makes data connection far more complex than simple data transfer
Change data capture is a key mechanism enabling efficient incremental synchronization without full re-ingestion of source data

keyInsights: Apollo's Binary Transfer Service autonomously coordinates software delivery across security domains, replacing the traditional sneakernet process of burning DVDs and physically walking them between networks
Air-gapped deployment is a fundamental requirement for national security, defense, and highly-regulated environments where networks cannot connect to the public internet
Cross Domain Solutions enable data passage between security domains but lack native DevOps support, requiring Apollo to layer intelligent delivery orchestration on top

Traditional cross-domain software delivery was so time-intensive that some companies forked codebases into public and private versions, causing government users to fall behind on features and security patches
Apollo uses constraint-based delivery that adapts to each environment's specific security classification, connectivity, and compliance requirements

title: Designing for Deletion (Palantir Explained, #6)
summary: In this blog post, we explain what deletion is, why it matters, and what challenges it poses to constructing a world-class framework for data protection.

extractedEntities.name: Data Deletion Framework
extractedEntities.description: Systematic process for making data less accessible over time, progressing from soft deletion (reversible) to hard deletion (irreversible), with auditing and accountability

extractedEntities.name: Soft Deletion
extractedEntities.description: Reversible stage of deletion where front-end user access is removed but data can still be recovered within a predefined period

extractedEntities.name: Hard Deletion
extractedEntities.description: End goal of deletion process where data is made irreversibly inaccessible and can no longer be processed

extractedEntities.name: GDPR Right to Erasure
extractedEntities.description: GDPR provision affording data subjects the right to request erasure of personal data, also known as the 'right to be forgotten'

extractedEntities.name: Data Minimization
extractedEntities.description: Core data protection principle limiting personal data processing to what is necessary and proportional for legitimate processing purposes

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that helps customers design and implement effective deletion processes as part of their data protection framework

keyInsights: Deletion is defined as a staged process of making data less accessible over time, not a single event — progressing from removing front-end access to soft deletion to eventual hard deletion
What matters most is the result, not the process: when data is deleted, it must no longer be possible to process it
Organizations must balance immediacy of deletion with reversibility — errors happen, so soft deletion periods allow recovery before hard deletion
Deletion frameworks require understanding what data the organization holds, how it is structured, and how it is processed in accordance with regulatory requirements
The 'right to be forgotten' under GDPR specifically grants data subjects the right to request erasure, making deletion capability a legal requirement

title: Escaping the Cargo Cult: Exchanging blind faith for real-world outcomes
summary: Enterprise accountability requires realignment to real-world outcomes after years of scaling isolated systems bred a “cargo cult” of performative progress.

keyInsights: The 'cargo cult' in enterprise software manifests as self-referential processes across engineering, procurement, implementation, and operations that prioritize internal metrics over real-world outcomes
Software companies, procurement professionals, consultants, IT teams, and financial analysts all tend toward easier-to-achieve metrics rather than focusing on core mission outcomes
Palantir positions its platform as the forcing function that aligns all activity toward measurable operational outcomes rather than performative progress
The misalignment between B2B SaaS value chain activities and actual organizational needs is identified as a root cause of stagnation in enterprise digital transformation
